Jeff had the career. The income. The family. The Medical degree."
"By every external measure, he’d won."
""
"And yet he told me,"
"“I wake up every morning unhappy… just trying to get to the end of the day.”"
""
"He wasn’t broken."
"This wasn’t a crisis."
"It was a reckoning."
""
"This is what the Normal 40 actually looks like—and what happens when you stop waiting."
""
"This is the awesome power of what can happen, when you let yourself ask."
""
"Today, meet Jeff.

Maybe the best decision of your lifetime isn’t behind you after all.  "
" "
"Maybe it is right before you, just waiting for you to act.  "
" "
"Maybe the worst decision of your life is never finding out if you could have done it. "
" "
"There is no greater regret than the memory of a shot you never took. "
" "
"Let’s be up to something.

Dear Invisible Returning Reader,"
""
"I see you. More than you know."
""
"Most days I’m writing to you. I literally search for the words to put right here that maybe, just maybe, grab you long enough to look in the mirror. "
""
"I sit here writing to you."
"Not someone like you, not someone ahead of you or behind you. You. "
""
"Right where you sit. "
"Right where you feel stuck. "
"Right where you left me yesterday."
"Right where it hurts."
""
"I know why you’re here. And I know you don’t want to be."
""
"At this stage in life, you think you should have the answers. That it should be obvious what to do next. That it should be other people who feel like you do. That you should feel better about all that you have. "
""
"But, for reasons you can’t articulate, you don’t."
""
"You’re restless. You’re discontented. You’re frustrated. And, on most days, it feels like you’re drowning. "
""
"I get it."
"I’ve been there. "
"So have thousands of others—just like you."
""
"But here’s the trick …"
""
"You are asking the wrong questions."
"— “Will it work?”"
"— “Will I make enough?”"
"— “Will I lose my income?”"
"— “Will I have to come crawling back?”"
"— “Will I blow up what I’ve built?”"
"— “Will people think I’ve lost it?”"
""
"These questions feel responsible. They feel smart. They feel safe."
""
"But they’re the reason your life hasn’t moved in years."
""
"Here are the questions that actually matter:"
" — Will you recognize the person you’ve become if you stay?"
" — Will your kids admire the courage you never used?"
" — Will you be proud of the chapter you refused to write?"
" — Will you be okay dying with your best work still inside you?"
""
"Because that—not the money, not the title, not the praise of people who’d forget your name a week after you resign—is the real cost."
""
"The cost of waiting. Of watching."
"The cost of asking the wrong questions. "
""
"The cost is your life."
"Your spark. Your potential. Your only shot at the best decade you have left."
""
"The best days of your life are waiting, and they don’t need to be."
""
"One email."
"One conversation."
"One declaration: “I’m done waiting.”"
"One ramble or Insider call with me …"
""
"It all means you’re waiting. "
"And it means we’re working on the right questions."
""
"Nobody here has your answers, so quit looking for them."
"If you’re lucky, you’ll find someone here with the right questions."
""
"When you do, connect with them. Follow them. Learn from them."
""
"The best decade of your life is right in front of you. "
"You can wait on it, or you can chase it."
"Whichever you choose is on you."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40."
"You’re going to like the way you live…"
"I guarantee it."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"Oh, and PS — This might be the best ad campagn of all time."

Dear CEO — Your best employees want me to tell you this. Most of them don’t think you’ll listen. I disagree—you’ll listen."
""
"But I don’t know if you’ll do anything about it."
""
"I talk to people in your company every day—thousands of them."
""
"They tell me why they came to work for you, what they admire about you, the milestones they hit with you, and the pride they felt wearing your logo. They talk a lot about who they used to be and how they used to feel—while working for you."
""
"Then they tell me how it’s changed."
""
"They tell me how they used to have say in decisions, control over their calendar, and your trust in their ability to do the job. They also tell me how they used to be able to do what was best for their customers and their team—without fear of consequences."
""
"Who tells me this?"
""
"Your C-suite."
"Those in the ER and OR"
"Your top performers."
"The 10% you assume would never leave."
""
"They don’t reach out because they’ve lost passion or want to coast."
"They reach out because they’ve run out of options—and hope."
""
"They don’t call me to complain."
"They call, hoping to reclaim control over the best years of their life."
""
"They think you gave up on them."
""
"I don’t think you have."
"But I don’t think you’ve told them that either."
""
"And I get it…that’s hard when the Board is coming down on you."
""
"Look—I’ve sat in your seat."
"It’s lonely, relentless, thankless, and public."
""
"You’ve got 20 hours of work to do today—and a family waiting for what’s left of you when you get home. Amd most days you’re wondering if the board is given up on you."
""
"You thought it would be better than this, too. "
"But this is your problem to solve, and this one matters."
""
"I promise you this:"
"Someone on your leadership team is thinking about leaving."
"They’re brushing up their resume, checking who they know, and wondering if a dude on the internet might understand them more than you do."
""
"The best thing you can do—for you—is this:"
""
"Let your team know they matter and that you see them."
"That you hear them, you trust them and you value them."
""
"Then, prove it—in actions."
""
"Not in pay."
"In control. In freedom."
"In their ability to make decisions free of fear of consequence. "
""
"Get in the boat with them and grab an ore."
""
"I keep a list of the Top 10 Reasons Elite Performers Leave."
""
"Number one is this:"
"“I no longer feel seen, valued, or heard. They reward my output, ignore my input, and measure my value by throughput.”"
""
"Your greatest assets feel like cogs."
""
"Some will stay and tolerate it."
"But the best?"
"They’ll leave."
""
"And when they do?"
"You’ll have a new problem to solve."
""
"Want to know who is thinking about leaving and why? "
"I would like to tell you…just send me a note."
""
"My goal isn’t to get anyone to leave—that’s not the answer."
"My goal is to inspire everyone to find their home."
""
"Today, let’s be up to something."
"You’ll be glad you did."
""
"It’s your ball, boss."
"Pass or shoot."
""

"Confession - I buy a lot of dumb shit. Mostly because I can, but probably also for the quick hit of dopamine. After a decade of this, I have more crap - good stuff, actually - that I don’t use, don’t need, and never brought me joy. "
""
"But I no longer have my W-2 addiction, so I also have to change my spending addiction. "
""
"My guess is you can relate, at least on some level. "
""
"Join me in this challenge over the next 100 days:"
""
"1.) Spend 40% LESS on shit you don’t need. If you need examples, look around your garage or at your Amazon purchases for the last 100 days. "
""

"The enemy is the voice that convinces you to wait. The one that tells you it’s “not the right time,” that you should “just get through this quarter,” or “be grateful for what you have.”"

"It sounds reasonable. It sounds like you."

"The enemy is indecision."

"And it steals your life."

An open letter to anyone here who has never shared an original thought of your own on this platform:"
""
"Dear LinkedIn Spectator,"
""
"Thank you for once again taking the time to be here. For scrolling over a few good quotes, checking out what your competitors are up to, and secretly checking in on a few people who tend to write what you’re thinking."
""
"Thanks for just checking things out. For judging others’ content and comparing what you would have done differently had you made the post?"
""
"Oh, and thank you for posting about that job in another department in your company three months ago. I agree; Rich would be a great guy to work for. That was useful, probably. "
""
"I used to be just like you. I used to simply post about quarterly results, acquisitions, and who is hiring. I used to see what others were doing and think about what I would do if I only knew where to start."
""
"I used to be a spectator. I used to log in, scroll, judge, wish, and then log out."
""
"I used to think nobody would want to hear from me."
"I used to believe what I had to say had already been said."
"I used to fear what my company would think if I sounded more like me than I did like them."
""
"So, I was a spectator. "
"- Until I learned how to play."
"- Until I learned that I have my voice."
"- Until I discovered I had something to offer."
"- Until I knew what it sounded like when I got to sound like me."
""
"There’s a reason you’re here. And I assure you that being here is not an accident or an algorithm—it’s you. "
""
"You are here because you have something to say. "
"You are here because there’s a larger story in you."
"You are here to see and judge what I do—good or bad."
"You are here because something in you is stirring."
"You are here because your best work is in you."
"You are here because you don’t know how to start."
""
"You are not here hoping that I will inspire you to quit."
"You are here hoping that I will inspire you to start."
""
"And your start is just one post away."
"That’s where it starts."
""
"I have helped a thousand people write their first posts—Doctors, law partners, accounting partners, career military, and clergy. "
""
"When you decide you’re ready, book a call with me. I’ll show you how to build your ten posts. "
""
"You will owe me nothing if it doesn’t 10X your confidence and trajectory. "
""
"But if it does, you owe me a note of thanks."
""

""

There is a new way to teach math in school."
""
"Most parents, parents my age, parents who learned a different way to do math from their parents, tend to harbor a general disdain for this ‘new way’ of solving the same problem."
""
"Why?"
""
"Well, probably because the old way worked just fine. And, even more, because it’s the only way you know."
""
"So, parents question the new process. Worse, they make up unflattering names for the new math, for the curriculum, and sometimes for the teacher."
""
"Then, to show some administrators how they feel, parents sit down and teach their kids the old way. The right way. The way that worked for them. "
""
"But that begs the question, did it work for you, or did it merely get you by?"
""
"Did it set a course, or satisfy a requirement? "
""
"Did it teach you something or merely prove you could follow directions?"
""
"Why is this so bothersome?"
""
"Maybe it’s because you don’t want to learn a new way of doing something you’ve already learned to do."
""
"Maybe it’s because you would prefer nothing change so that you don’t have to?"
""
"Maybe because it makes you feel … Old, lost, stale, or irrelevant?"
""
"Maybe it’s because the world is changing—the math is changing all around you—and you don’t know what to do about it."
""
"Maybe this is a post about middle school math, and maybe this is a post about more than that."
""
"Maybe this is about the math in your life. The math you wake up with. The math you’ve always used. The math that had always worked. The math that brought you here."
""
"Maybe this is about the math that is changing all around you."
"The math you want to solve the old way."
""
"Maybe this is a post about how you feel, and if you will ever deal with it."
""
"Less than two years ago, after years of avoiding the reality of how I felt — the years I was avoiding the math — I finally found time and a place to learn the new math in my life."
""
"It is hard to articulate how the new math changed how I saw the world. But the new math shifted one thing in a way the old math never could: The new math keeps score of your time, not your bank account."
""
"I don’t know why I felt this was a good message for Christmas Eve. For a while, I thought maybe this doesn’t fit the moment's grace. But then, I did the math."
""
"This holiday is the result of the new math that’s possible in your life. This holiday should be more about how we spend our time than how we spend our dollars."
""
"Over the next few days, maybe you’ll have time to think about the math in your life. How the math that got you here, might not be the math that gets you there."
""
"There is no better time in your life than this week to think about the math. Your math. The math that matters. The math that keeps score with your time, not your 401k. It is the only math that will ever truly matter."
""
"Winston Churchill once said, “However beautiful the strategy, you should occasionally look at the results.” It turns out he was talking about math."
""
"The math of a lifetime."
""

At age 19, you made a decision that shaped the rest of your life; you chose a career, a major or enlisted."
""
"A decision from a lifetime ago has led us to our reality. BUT-"
""
"Somewhere in your Normal 40, it gnaws on you that you have natural talents that have been largely ignored. And for the first time, you’re feeling a growing tug in your gut to do something else, something that tests you. Something is urging you, almost begging you, to leave what it is you’ve become known for doing, and create what it is you are capable of being."
""
"Somewhere in your Normal 40, you begin to care a little less about the size of the paycheck, and more about the amount of control you have over your time. You realize time is no longer on your side and that a dream ignored has the energy to one day become a thorn of regret."
""
"Somewhere in your Normal 40, you quit ignoring the discontent, and you will crack the door open to what feels like an irresponsible concept; to trade what you have become for the chance to see who you are capable of being next."
""
"You wonder if the work you chose 30 years ago has done all it can for you - and you for it. And you find great curiosity to learn from those who gave up the grind and relief of a paycheck for the chance to live “on mission.”"
""
"Spoiler alert: You’re still reading for a reason. And I’m writing to you, boss."
""
"I know what you feel inside feels radical. It feels reckless, unsafe, unpredictable, and irresponsible. And you wonder what others would think. You wish you weren’t feeling a call for change and you could feel today like you did ten years ago. I was in your seat."
""
"You have a choice to make. One that isn’t radical, reckless, unsafe, or unpredictable. A choice that will set the tone and the feeling of significance for the rest of your life. A choice you have to explore, or you will regret never embracing."
""
"When Normal 40 finds you, you should find me."
""
"My mission isn’t to sell you a program; I won’t."
""
"My mission is to inspire you to consider that maybe, just maybe, this is the day you’ve been hoping for. And maybe, just maybe, this is the sign you were hoping would give you the courage to act. And maybe, just maybe, a call with this dude from the internet can help you put your thoughts in order."

"The Next 40 awaits."

"I was feeling a little off this week - and I don’t know why.  I guess that happens from time to time. Maybe it was because things were feeling too tactical. Maybe it’s because I want something to be moving faster.  Maybe it’s because I’m feeling the challenge of reaching more people. "

""
"When I feel off I grab a pen and then I permit myself to dream. This morning, before the house was up, that’s what I did; I dreamed. "
""
"Below is what I was dreaming about. It was so helpful. It’s my words - to me. And now to you. "
""
"This weekend, take time to dream. Don’t label it, don’t judge it, and don’t deadline it.  "
""
"Don’t do it for someone else and don’t burden it with returns. And please don’t think about the how. Save that for your Monday life. Just dream about a future state. "

Welcome to Men in Midlife. "
""
"I am 47. I have, by most accounts, been successful. "
""
"I have saved well. I have married beyond my means. I have (and do) work hard. "
""
"I have three healthy, motivated and decent children. I am a public company executive. "
""
"I've been personally blessed with good health and the world's best friends."
""
"I've got a record of success, with a few disasters to my name, too. "
""
"I am very happy and eternally grateful for my life's blessings. All of them.  But ..."
""
"Around the age 45, something inside me started wafting around. The arrow that pointed straight up and to the right started lose it's ability to compel me."
""
"Something was a little different, but I couldn't explain it. "
""
"There wasn't a problem at home or work, in fact these were at high points. "
""
"But I could feel myself shifting from decisions of the mind, the instrument that got me to where I am, to decisions from the gut."
""
"I started to think about and take actions on things that didn't have a resume impact, or even a financial impact, but just felt like things my gut thought would be good.  Turns out, it might be right."
""
"There's a lot more to this story, but I simply want to know what others have experienced or are experiencing.  "
""
"And dude, this is where you give in order to get. If you come here only to harvest, we can all expect a drought.  Get in and bring in.  Lets see what happens.  "
""
"Own your clock. "
""
"This is your Normal 40. "
""
"And it is your second-half story. "
""

An open note to corporate leaders: "
"1 in 3 of the people working for you are looking for something new."
"1 in 3 are not using their core gifts during any given week."
"1 in three have updated their resume in the last 6 months."
""
"This is not 1 in 3 bad employees. "
"Not 1 in 3 underperformers. "
"Not 1 in 3 that you don't know and can't name. "
""
"This is 1 in 3 of the most accomplished, credentialed, experienced people in the room. Not any room. Your boardroom."
""
"One in three is doing work that doesn't ask anything real of them. One in three shows up here, to LinkedIn, looking to make The Trade."
""
"Many of them call me."
""
"I surveyed 702 elite performers.  These are the people who've spent 15-plus years building a career, but also serving a company, their customers, cleints and patients.  "
""
" -- 33% said they do not use their core gifts every day."
" -- 63% HOPE they're not still doing the same job in three years."
""
"That's not a performance problem. "
"That's a leadership problem."
"That's a culture problem."
""
"A culture where your top performers are willing to say this to me, because the org chart makes it impossible to say it to you."
""
"The gap between what your best people are capable of and what you're asking of them to endure compounds quietly. It doesn't show up in a performance review. It doesn't flag in an engagement survey. Because they're that good."
""
"But it shows up when they leave, and you didn't see it coming."
""
"Your best people have already started looking for something new."
""
"Maybe it's time you do better at seeing them."
""
"Maybe it's time we talk about what else they tell me, but won't tell you. "
""
"Maybe we should ramble.

This is one of nearly 1100 rambles I've had since leaving my corporate life. "
""
"Calls with people who decided two decades ago what they wanted their life to look like. And mostly nailed it."
""
"People who have come to a place in their life where they would trade what they have, but they're not yet sure what to trade it for."
""
"These are their stories."
""
"Today, meet Josh."
""
"#normal40

It’s 9:00 AM after a three-day weekend, and …."
""
"You’re about to spend eight hours doing… nothing that’s yours."
""
"Where’s the proof?"
"Your calendar."
"Oh, and your suffocating soul."
"But that’s right, we don’t talk about that."
""
"Fine."
""
"Most of you will spend the very best hours of your day, in the very best years of your life, in meetings. "
""
"Meetings you didn’t lead, talking about projects you no longer have a passion for, to hit targets you didn’t set to line the pockets of people you’ll never meet."
""
"Simply because that’s what you did yesterday."
""
"But you’ve felt this slipping for months. "
"Actually, years."
""
"Same calendar. Same projects. Same initiatives. Same strategy. Same questions. Same cycle. Same expectations. Same you."
""
"Only older."
"And more frustrated, distracted, and discontent. "
""
"Why?"
""
"At some point, elite performers outgrow the dreams they set out to chase 20 years ago. "
""
"They find themselves hanging on to something they once wanted, frustrated that they can’t feel like they once did. "
""
"They don't have the energy they once had. They can’t even muster the ability to give a damn about things that used to matter."
""

"It’s quiet here. From where I sit, I can see a few locals scurrying around the entrance, someone cleaning pots used for generations, and a single welcoming vat of coffee on the counter."
""

""
"My family is with me. The five us us. A rare sight, experience, or feeling for us as our kids age."

""

""
"Today, that changed."
""

""
"Yet, here I am. No, here we are."
""
"This year, for the first time, Mindi and didn’t plan to travel for our kids. Our kids invited us to travel here—for them."

He came here by trusting the omens as they presented themselves. He came here not because this place had any answers, but because it issued a call."

"Today, we’re in Linares, Mexico, celebrating together. An experience they created for us."

You’re not stuck—you’ve outgrown what brought you here."
""
"For the past decade, you’ve chased the best possible life. You set a high bar, and you have done well. You’ve earned the title, the paycheck, the recognition, and the reputation. By all accounts, you have what you set out to get. But the rewards feel smaller than they once did. "
""
"Call it what you will, but I call it growth. "
""
"The ancient Greeks nailed this. They had two words for what elite performers pursue: hedonia (pleasure) and eudaimonia (purpose). "
""
"Hedonia is chasing what feels good in the moment—more money, more stuff, more applause, more obvious achievement. It’s chasing a single post that gets thousands of hits. But it’s fleeting, a sugar rush that never lasts and leaves you chasing the next hit."
""
"Eudaimonia is different. It’s about chasing meaning, impact, and alignment. It’s about creating a life that feels good on the inside, not just impressive on the outside. It’s about presence and ease of self. It’s about writing content every day for years, hoping it changes a single person's life."
""
"If Hedonia is your life as measured by the size of the splash everyone sees, then Eudaimonia is your life as measured by the impact of your ripples on people you may never know."
""
"This creates the razor-fine line between who you are and who you have the potential to be."
""
"And this moment is here to call you out. This is The Trade. This is the moment. This is why you’re here."
""
"And while you won’t confess outwardly, you can’t help but lean back and know it’s also true. "
""
"Look, it’s common to get here, and it’s common to be frustrated by it. Heck, I named my company “Normal 40” because, well, this is normal."
""
"It’s also common to never face it, or wait until you’re too old to deal with it. And it’s common to look back with regret for never mustering the courage to be on you—who you were capable of being. I want to change that. "
""
"My mission is to inspire you to act. To show that you’re not alone. To give you examples of others who are doing it and have done it. To offer you a process that helps you get clear on your life’s best chapter and helps you live out your potential—on your terms."
""
"My metric is to show up here relentlessly—in pursuit of notes of a thousand notes of thanks from the people who finally acted and the family members who were impacted."
""
"To be clear—my target is you. Only you. Not for the splash, but for the ripple. Not mine; yours."
""
"Twice each year, I open up Vintage coaching. It’s an eight-month program that changes lives. It’s a cohort of six, and everything about it is personal, intimate, and intentional. I built it, I lead it, and together, we will go through it."
""
"Vintage Four is open now. Send me a note, and I’ll tell you all about it."
""
"If 2025 is your year, let’s be up to something."
""
"Not because it’s easy, but because it’s time."
""
"#normal40 #coaching #leadership

The Farm: Day 2 — Connecting with an old friend and the incredible gift of slowing it down."
""
"Shouldn’t you be doing something else right now?"
""
"Think of all the tasks you’re putting off until you have more time. - The thing you were going to do yesterday."
"- The thing you have to report on later today."
"- The thing that’s due by noon Friday."
"- The thing that needs to be shipped by quarter end."
"- The call to a friend, the note of thanks to a colleague."
"- The volunteer board competing for your time …"
""
"You should do that."
""
"Or, maybe you shouldn’t."
""
"I am cursed by my “shoulds.” Worse, I judge myself harshly for how I live up to them."
""
"I am a dreamer—it never stops. It’s a relentless pursuit of the possible. The pursuit of what I’m capable of doing, who I am capable of inspiring, and how I can get one person to do one thing more."
""
"The list of things I COULD do for Normal 40 is long. BUT— Once I believe I COULD do something, I immediately tell myself I SHOULD—when I have time."
""
"We all live with the false assumption that more time is just around the corner. We convince ourselves that we will have more time after this week, this project, this quarter, and this fiscal year …"
""
"But the free time never comes. Instead, we repeat it over the next week, month, quarter, or year. "
""
"This makes us unmistakably human—to know what’s possible and calculate the right time to chase it or not. But…"
""
"Freedom will never find you."
""
"The freedom of time won’t be given to you. Freedom and control over your life must be taken. It must be traded for. It must become worth the fight—the fight of your life."
""
"Even now, in a lifestyle I’ve worked hard to build for myself and my family, I run out of time."
""
"But now I see it. I name it. I know when it’s trying to suck me in—and I fight for it. Sometimes this fight costs money in lost opporunity. Sometimes, it costs me lost experiences. But it never costs me the feeling of being anything but me."
""
"This is worth the cost and worthy of the fight, every time."
""
"There will be a long list of things you should be doing right now. Chances are, even taking time to read this is competing with the other voice telling you what you SHOULD be doing instead."
""
"It happens to all of us. But today, maybe you should fight for a little more for you. A little more time to wander, and a little more time to wonder. "
""
"Today, make it clear that who we are capable of being is worth the fight."
""
"Don’t do anything irrational; go for a walk. Call a friend. Quit a committee. Write a note. But live your life."
""
"Give yourself the grace to remember what it feels like when you get to feel like you, and then live it."
""
"On my run today, I ran into Blueberry, my niece’s horse. He was standing on the roadway as I ran past, so I took the time to reconnect. "
""
"This simple little thing was possible simply because I fought for it. And you can, too."
""
"The next Insider call is tomorrow, and you are invited."
""
"Be up to something."
"Life lives better."
""

" - A dude who maybe isn’t all that different from … you."

I’ve had a great first half.  In fact, pretty impressive.  Just sayn. "
""
"But, I don’t feel like I have my second half figured out.  "
""
"I don’t want more of the same.  Even thought it was great, there’s more.  "
""
"But I struggle to know how to define what my second half should be.  I struggle finding “the one thing” I need to do in my second half to get maximum joy.  "
""
"Anyone else there?

I believe N40 is a feeling dudes experience at some point in life. The obstacles of N40 manifest themselves as we’re unable to always able to understand our own feelings. Here are a few N40 symptoms I’ve heard: "
"- If you’re a high achiever but you’re not as satisfied at work as you used to be;"
"- If you don’t feel like making a career change is worth the risk of losing the position you have;"
"- If you’ve tried supllments to bring back the feeling of fulfillment;"
"- If you’ve turned on your “open to work” toggle on LinkedIn;"
"- If you don’t feel like your climbing as fast as you used to;"
"- If you see other people making change and you wonder how they mustered the courage;"
"- If you cry at America’s Got Talent (or other shows that otherwise would have not phased you 10 years ago);"
"- If you check your retirement account weekly, and then to some “how long do I have to work until I retire” math;"
"- If you have a pit in your stomach on Sunday at 4:00PM as you think about retuning to work;"
"- If you’re happy to let people around you take on the responsibility at work;"
"- If you feel like you’re not as successful as you used to be, or as successful as other people think you are;"
"….then you might be in your N40."
""
"What other symptoms do you feel?

Wow. You're good at this."
""
"And that's where the problem starts."
""
"You've been good at it so long that nobody — including you — ever stopped to ask if it's what you actually want."
""
"Every promotion said yes. Every bonus said yes. Every performance review said yes."
""
"So you kept going."
""
"And somewhere in there, without a funeral or an announcement or even a bad day to point to, you stopped growing. You just got... better. At the same thing. For the same people. Toward the same endless pit."
""
"That's not a career."
""
"That's solitary confinement with good lighting and a toilet."
""
"I've talked to over a thousand people sitting exactly where you're sitting. Physicians. Executives. Founders...All of them successful. "
""
"Most of them suffocating inside the dream that once was."
""
"They don't have a performance problem."
""
"They have a gap problem."
""
"The Gap between what they know they are capable of and what they're being asked for instead."
""
"Nobody on the outside sees it."
"Nobody on the outside feels it."
"Nobody on the outside fixes it."
""
"This is an inside job."
"It starts with a call."
""
"A call you schedule, or"
"A call you avoid."
""
"But it is your call."

They called it a milestone. You just called it Monday."
""
"Harvard psychologist Tal Ben-Shahar named it the Arrival Fallacy. The belief that when you reach the goal — the title, the exit, the diagnosis beaten, the number hit — you'll finally feel what you've been working toward."
""
"Then you arrive. And you feel... Tuesday."
""
"A year ago, I rambled with Steve. He booked a call from my website, and it was on my calendar. So, I hit "Join meeting.""
""
"Steve is a cancer survivor. South Tampa. He beat it. Against real odds. Came out the other side."
""
"He told me that the six months after remission were the most disorienting of his life. He'd spent a year fighting for his life, and he'd won. Then...nothing. "
""
"Just the calendar. The inbox. The same commute."
"It was just like it was, only emptier. "
""
"He said, "I thought I'd feel like a different person. I just felt tired and confused about why I wasn't celebrating.""
""
"This happens without cancer, too. After the promotion. After the exit. After the kids leave. After the house, the car, the clubs the bonus ... "
""
"The destination arrives. The feeling doesn't."
""
"That is The Gap. The separation you feel in your gut.  The separation between arrival and meaning. The distance between where you are and your potential. The distance between what you do and what you want to one day build. "
""
"This is where Steve was living, quietly."
"Maybe you are, too. "
""
"When it's time to close The Gap, do more than nothing."
""
"Be up to something.

""
"You approach both with the same broken question:"
"You ask: “What does my spouse want from me?”"
""
"Look, it makes sense."
"You’ve been rewarded your whole life for output."
"See the problem. Fix it. Carry the load. Perform. Repeat."
""
"That instinct built your career. It got you here. But …"
"It also quietly costs you something along the way."
""
"Over time, you quit asking and started assuming."
"You assume you know what your spouse wants."
"You assume it hasn’t changed since marriage."
"You assume it’s more of something tangible."
" — A club. A home. A car. A lifestyle…"
""
"You assume."
"And you never ask."
""
"Dude. "
"Ask."
""
"When you do, this time ask a better question. The right question. "
"This is no longer about what they want from you … "
"It’s about what they want for you."
""
"The Question:"
"“What do you want for me?”"
""
"What do you want for the person standing here today."
"For the one who goes all in. Who provides. Who carries. Who tries to give everyone a good life—and gets it wrong while trying to get it right."
""
"   — What do you want for me?"
""
""
""
"For ……. Me."
""
""
""
"If you give that question space, it changes things."
"For everyone."
""
"For you — because now it gets uncomfortable."
"Why? Because now it feels like you are the problem."
""
"It feels uncomfortable because you’re never the problem, and it feels awkward because the question suggests you are. "
""
"In the story of your life, there will be pages where you get to have the attention."
""
"In your story, there comes a moment when proving you can still do what you did yesterday stops mattering—to you."
""
"When you no longer want to keep doing what you’re doing, how you’re doing it, where you’re doing it, or who you’re doing it for."
""
"That moment isn’t failure."
"It’s an invitation."
""
"Where does faith come in?"
""
"Well, you make the same expired assumptions in your faith as you do in your family."
""
"Your faith was built on discipline, obedience, and sacrifice … "
"But in 20 years, you’ve never asked what your faith might want for you, not from you."
""
"Elite performers don’t break from lack of discipline."
"They break from bad assumptions."
""
"You don’t have to announce, “I have a problem.”"
"But it might change your life to admit it quietly."
"And then update your assumptions."
""
"Today, ask your spouse:"
" — “What do you want for me?”"
""
"If they don’t know, ask this:"
" — “When did you see me the happiest?”"
""
"Those questions don’t make you a problem."

"They give you answers."
"They give you alignment."
"They give you a team."
""
"In this lifetime, you get to be the problem."
""
"Once you see that as the gift—one that you’ve earned…"
"Everything changes."
""
"Today, be up to something."
""
"You are who I help."

""

""

"Stretching you."
"Building you."

“Why are you selling?”"
""
"For a decade, I led M&A at a public company. "
""
"Every deal started with the same question to the founder or CEO:"
""
"“Why are you selling?”"
""
"You’d think this would be the easiest one."
""
"A founder."
"15–20 years in."
"Blood, weekends, identity poured into the thing."
""
"But most couldn’t answer it. And if they did …"
""
"They danced."
"They generalized."
"They gave us words that sounded right but felt hollow."
""
"That wasn’t lost on me."
""
"Because at that exact moment—when the stakes were highest—their senses were fully awake."
""
"Emotion louder than logic."
"Doubt sharper than certainty."
""
"In that moment, they weren’t avoiding the question because they didn’t know."
""
"They were avoiding it because they had never confronted it."
""
"Not because the decision was wrong."
"But because they’d never had to say it out loud."
"They never had to publicly confess it."
"They had never had to turn it into words."
""
"It confused me then."
"It makes perfect sense to me now."
""
"I can see it in the people who are here."
"I can see it in you."
""
"Incredible leaders enduring situations they won’t name."
"Tolerating lives they won’t confront in real time."
"Accepting terms they never consciously agreed to."
""
"Not because you lack courage."
"Not because they lack confidence."
""
"But because you’re avoiding the honesty."
"And because you’ve never had to say it out loud."
""
"You’re one act away from a reckoning. An act you can do today, but one you shouldn’t do alone. Answer this:"
""
"What’s the honest truth you’re avoiding now?"
"And why are you selling?"
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40."
""
"We are up to something."

Growing up, my mom always insisted on a family photo."
""
"She didn’t care where or when. If we were together, there would be a photo."
""
"We rolled our eyes. Complained. Said it was ridiculous."
"“We just took one last time.”"
""
"Fast forward 30 years, and now—I get it."
""
"Last Christmas was the first time our family of five wasn’t all together."
""
"Not because of drama or health. But because our kids now have lives of their own. Lives that include us, but don’t revolve around us."
""
"This year, we’re back together."
"Even if only for a few nights."
"Even if only for a few meals."
"Even if only for a few laughs, memories, and photos."
""
"Because life, doing what it does, changes the circle."
""
"The circle of family. Of childhood. Of presence. Of being known."
"The circle of life."
""
"That circle doesn’t break."
"But it bends to jobs, partners, travel, and growth."
"It shifts under the weight of time."
"And it gets lost in the pursuit of assets."
""
"But now, I see the circle. And I see my job within it has changed. It’s my job now to strengthen it."
"With presence over presents."
""
"In the 20 hours we’ve been together, we’ve told stories. Relived old memories. Laughed at each other’s clothes. Rage-baited their mom, and outwitted each other in every story we told."
""
"In other words, we’ve been a family."
""
"I no longer have the life where my kids go to bed in my house each night."
"That’s a luxury now. A gift."
""
"I no longer have a life where we’re the center of their world."
"And that too, is a gift."
""
"Because it means the circle is doing what it’s meant to do: grow."
""
"Last night, we were all together."
"And just like the generation before—and the one before that—we took a photo."
""
"And, just like we did as kids, my kids complained."
"“We already have a million of these.”"
""
"But in 30 years, when they gather their own kids in one room…"
"Even for just a few days…"
"They’ll get it."
""
"They’ll see the circle."
"They’ll feel the window."
""
"On this circle are windows that open and close one time."
"And for all of us, we’re standing in now."
"A window that’s open, but one that will close."
""
"What remains once a window closes?"
"A photo."
""
"And, if you’re lucky, new memories, new stories, new parts of the circle."
""
"It’s Christmas Eve."
"Wherever you are in your circle—see it, honor it, move through it, and strengthen it."
""
"Do it all before the window closes. "
"Say the words. Give the apology. Send the hug. Bury the past. Live for the future. "
""
"And then… seal it with a photo."
""
"Just like Mom used to do."
""
"From me to you …May the circle be unbroken, by and by, Lord, by and by … "

""

""

""

"It never did, and it never was."

"And it pays it off quarterly. "

"Welcome to the new Quarter End Culture."

"Something worth fighting for."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40."
"This is what we do."

"Be up to something."

"That sentence sits at the root of every ramble I’ve ever had."

"Let me ask you something. It’s a serious question:"

"or reflexively check your phone."

"Your mind wondered."

"Wondered where you’d be in a year. "
"Wondered about your impact."
"Wondered who you would meet and how you would help."
"Wondered about what’s possible."

"And it did it without telling you it wasn’t possible. "
"It didn’t tell you to wait."
"Or that chasing it would be irresponsible. "
""
"Today, we fail at wondering, and we succeed at wandering."

"Wondering becomes wandering in the moment the text from the boss comes in. A customer is pissed about something you can’t control. The CEO wants a new initiative. The board needs a new slide. The administrator needs an explanation… not for their understanding, but to satisfy some BS legal requirement."
""
"After five years free of wondering, you wake up to discover you’re leading a life full of wandering. "
""
"Wandering right back into the same rut. Wandering right back into the same spiral. Wandering right back into the setting that is getting worse, not better."
""
"You didn’t choose this."

"The Machine did."
""
"It pays you just enough."
"Keeps you just busy enough."
"So you stop asking dangerous questions like:"
""
"“Is this the life I want?”"
""
"You’re not broken."
"You’re just drifting."
""
"Just enough to prevent you from acting on your wonder."
"Just enough to keep you in the wander."
""
"So now what?"
""
"What if you start to wonder again?"
""
"What if you gave the best hours to yourself rather than your inbox?"
""
"What if you took time to get clear on the life you want…and you chased it?"
""
"What if your calendar looked like you wanted it to look?"
""
"What if you wondered about your life if you did it?"
"And worried about your life if you don’t? "
""
"If you would trade what you have, but don’t know what you would trade it for ..."
""

The lesson I took from M&A that changed my life: "
" "
"It takes the same amount of work to chase, court, negotiate and achieve a small deal as it takes to live out a big deal. "
" "
"No matter deal’s size, you go through the same sequence. You ask the same questions, you fill out the same reports, you give the same briefs, you wrestle the same dragons, you work the same strategic integration plan, you have the same self-doubt, and you use the same math. "
" "
"Then, at the one-year mark, you answer the same questions: "
"1.) Are we better as a result of this deal? "
"2.) Did we think big enough when we chased the deal? "
"3.) What should this deal look like in another year? "
" "
"NOW re-read and replace the word “deal” with “dream.” "
"______________________________________________ "
" "
"Dude, dream bigger. "
" "
"Dream bigger than who it is you already are, and bigger than what you might someday do if you win the lottery. "
" "
"Surround yourself with those who dream big and act. In hours you will start to dream big and act. "
" "
"Look, I love Normal 40. Some days I can’t believe what it’s doing for others - and me. I am “on mission.” "
" "
"But we are just getting started. My dream for Normal 40 is so much bigger than how it looks today. I’m not talking revenue - I’m talking lives inspired. And you, so long as you care to be here, get to see it go from where it was, to where it is, to where it will take us. "
""
"Dream bigger. Nobody will do it for you.

The fight of your life will find you in one of two places:"
"in the courage to walk away from what you’ve built,"
"or in the quiet regret of looking back and wishing you had tried."
""
"One has a price."
"The other a cost."
""
"The price of chasing something new, or the cost of wishing you had."
""
"Every day, I lead an incredible group of leaders I call Insiders. The men and women who arrive at this place I call Normal 40. "
""
"Normal 40 is not an age. It’s not about the years behind you. And it’s not for other people. "
""
"Normal 40 is about the years ahead of you. About getting clear who you really are, what you’re truly best at, and who you’re capable of being."
""
"Normal 40 is about the community. About people. About being purely human and showing up for others simply because you can. "
""
"Normal 40 is about finally dealing with what you’re avoiding. About what you’re waiting to try. About losing the best years of your love to something you used to love, but is now smothering your soul."
""
"It’s about finally living the best decade of your life."
""
"Normal 40 is not an age. It’s a feeling. "
"The feeling that brings you here. "
""
"It’s about accepting that nobody is going to fix this for you."
"It’s about permitting yourself to explore something new."
"It’s about finally seeing you don’t have to do it alone."
""
"You have two options: Pay the price of finally being you, or suffer with the cost of wishing you had tried."
""
"This is what I do. It is my gift. It’s what I do better than anyone you’re going to come to meet. And I’m inviting you to let me prove it."
""
"When you’re ready, I’ll be here. I can’t push or pull. I can only invite."
""
"And when you arrive,  I’ll bring a few thousand people—just like you—with me. People you can’t believe were here all along, if only you had started sooner."
""
"Our community is called The Insider. And we meet every Thursday. "
""
"The meeting has a price."
"Missing it has a cost."
""
"You decide."
""
"Be up to something."

It was the fall of 2019. Someone snapped a photo at a family gathering. Nothing special. Nothing out of place. Just me, my family, smiling, drink in hand, arm around people I love."
""
"Later, that night, when we got home, I looked at it. Then, froze."
""
"The man in the photo looked tired. Distant. Softer. Older. Heavier."
""
"He looked weary, and he looked like someone who’d been worn out by something, and that age had come crawling in before it was invited."
""
"I stared at that picture longer than I care to admit. The dude in the photo was me, but I didn’t recognize the face. "
""
"In this moment, I decided something had to change. That something? How I take care of me. "
""
"That photo didn’t just change my diet. It changed my outlook, and it changed my trajectory."
""
"I stopped feeding my comfort. I started feeding my potential."
"In a hundred days, my body started to change. My mind followed."

I found this photo taken in the fall of 2015. It was the whiteboard in my office. I was the president of a company that was changing its strategy, and we were going all in."
""
"Three months later, I was removed."
""
"I took this photo in the quiet, early hours of the day. In the calm before my leadership team arrived to discuss it. I remember sitting, staring, planning, prepping, and, well, hoping that we were doing it right."
""
"It’s during a time when our CFO reminded me that hope is not a strategy. And I reminded him that any strategy free of hope had already failed."
""
"We agreed to disagree."
""
"Ten years later, I can still smell the markers."
""
"I can still feel the tension of the moment, and taste the desire to get this one right. And oddly, I’m still inspired by what we were able to pull off all those years ago."
""
"Across the top, the definition of a Campaign: “A connected series of operations, designed to yield a particular result.”"
""
"Below it, five pursuits—the five things that had to go right if we were going to survive the year (literally). The five things I believed were possible."
""
"Elsewhere on the board is my definition of GRIT: courage and resolve, with strength of character."
""
"There are also a few quotes from the leadership team—and one more thing. In the bottom-right corner, a note from my daughter, Grace, age nine:"
""
"“Hi, Dad. Have a good day at work. I love you. —Gracie.”"
""
"I never erased it. It was the only thing left on the board when I was reassigned from that office a few months later."
""
"It’s incredible how much life can fit on a single whiteboard: priorities, position, perspective, scars, hope, a whole company’s heartbeat in dry-erase ink. Ink that’s there today, and gone tomorrow."
""
"I was 40 years old the day I wrote this. I had no idea this photo would become a metaphor for life’s priorities: set clear goals, adapt through adversity, and stay connected to what matters most, never sacrificing your character. "
""
"And I had no idea this whiteboard would still give me hope."
""
"Courage and resolve, with strength of character."
""
"What was on your whiteboard ten years ago?"
"What’s still on it today?"
"What’s your priority?"
""
"Write it down and share it."
"It just might change your life."

"Today, be up to something."

Each morning, we all have three options that shape our lives. You will choose one of these today: "
""
"1. 𝐒𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐥𝐞: You choose to stay where you are--flying just beneath the radar. It’s comfortable, predictable, and familiar, and the pay is safe. It’s the ‘responsible’ act. You accept that this is all there is, and that this is the best work of your life. "
""
"Ask yourself: If this is all I ever offer, will I be happy with it?"
"—————-"
""
""
"2. 𝐆𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐝: You keep chasing more of what you already have—more of what you were chasing yesterday. Owned by your Outlook calendar and finding a twisted joy in Diamond Medallion airline and hotel status. Chasing a number on a spreadsheet by answering calls, texts, and emails at all hours of the day, night, and weekend."
""
"Ask yourself: What will I really have left when this path ends, and what am I trading away to be on it?"
"————-"
""
""
""
"3. 𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐤: You choose to pause. To take some time. To create a strategy for the best decade of your life—the one right before you. You assess where you are, where you want to go, and what needs to change. You get clear on the life you want—for you. This is not a weakness—it’s the most powerful move available to you. And it’s within your control…and that’s what makes it hard."
""
"Ask yourself: What will be the title of the best chapter of my life?"
"—————"
""
""
""
"Being up to something starts when we rethink the future we want. When you quit settling, decide to stop grinding, and give yourself some space to think."
""
"Until you do that, you do what you did yesterday. "
""
"You don’t have to quit in order to start. But you do have to start if you ever hope to quit. "
""
"Today, let's be up to something."
"You'll be glad you did it."
"This is what I do."
"You're invited."
"To think."

“How do I resign?”"
"I get that question a lot."
""
"Resigning is one of the biggest moments of your life—the culmination of a thousand micro-decisions that deliver you to this decision. You’ve dreamed about it, agonized over it, rehearsed it, but now you’re work here is done, and this is your time."
""
"So, do it right. The key? Create a story that gives them someone to root for. Be the type of person worth rooting for. It doesn’t mean they will, but that’s not the point. "
""
"The point is that mindset allows you to be true to yourself."
""
"Here are the rules:"
""
"1. Be Grateful."
"Start with gratitude. Thank them for the opportunity, the lessons, the trust. Tell a leader how they shaped your life and what you admire. Tell them what they taught you that you are taking with you. Gratitude sets the tone and makes you memorable."
""
"2. Share the Dream."
"Show them your fire. Speak with clarity about what’s next and why now is the time. People rarely root for a plan; they root for people who have a passion. Show them what you have."
""
"3. Keep the Door Open."
"You can’t control their reaction, but you can control yours. Check yourself for tone and be kind, confident, and equal. This is the moment you own your story. "
""
"4. Ask for Their Support."
"Invite them to believe in you. To stay part of your story. To bet on the underdog. To answer when called. Get them on the sidelines cheering for your path."
""
"Get this right, and you won’t just leave on good terms—you’ll leave with your first new fan."
""
"You can’t control how they will react. The beauty? You don’t need to.  But how you leave will be remembered longer than what you achieved. If it’s going sideways, don’t take the bait. Anchor back to your passion for what’s next. "
""
"So, don’t wing it. Do it according to plan—yours. Own your journey. Build the clarity. Chase what’s in you. Do whatever it is you’re been made ready to do next."
""
"Most of all, don’t do nothing.  "
""
"My name is Lon Stroschein, and I help elite performers navigate the most significant professional move in their lifetime—because I’ve done it…and have helped thousands of others do it, too."
""
"Welcome to your Normal 40"
"Be up to something."
""
"#normal40  #thetrade #jfds #careerchange"

Everyone wants change."
"Few know where to start."
""
"Here’s the framework I built and work from…in it’s simplest terms. "
""
"1.)  Accept."
"At some point, you accept that this feeling isn’t going away. You have to accept this isn’t your best story, best chapter or best work. And accept this is going to be on you to change, or accept that you never did."
""
""
"2.)  Permit."
"Then comes the hardest part—giving yourself permission. No boss. No validation. Just you saying, “My work here is done.” Permit yourself to explore something new. Permit yourself to think bigger, act more boldly and take your shot. Permit yourslef to finding the people who can help you get there."
""
""
"3.)  Act."
"Finally, you move. You do something. You do one thing more than yesterday. You make the call. Write the post. Book the meeting. Try the thing. It’s messy and uncertain. It’s not about the result, it’s about the motion. It’s where you’re seen as being, doing, or acting on something new. It doesn’t mean quit; it means start."
""
"That’s the process."
""
"Every transformation you’ve ever admired didn’t start with a burst of confidence. It started with curiousity and a moment of courage. The courage to be seen. The courage to ship the work."
""
"Accept. Permit. Act. Repeat."
""
"Be up to something."

""
"“Freedom to” and “Freedom from.”"
""
"They sound the same, but they shape your life differently. They represent two very different places on the same journey, and two very different mindsets. One is about chasing a dream, the other about looking to escape. "

""
"“Freedom From” is about a release—the escape from the calendar that controls you, the inbox that never rests, the charts, memos, and reports that choke the joy out of the very life you said you wanted."
""
"Freedom To doesn’t require clarity, only a sense of direction."
""
"Freedom From doesn’t require you to quit, only that you believe it’s an option. (Ohh, got you on that one.)"

"Freedom To gives you direction to someplace new."
"Freedom From just wants the exhaustion to end."
""
"It’s time to take back your freedom—your Freedom To and your Freedom From."

"The mightiest of your days won’t come from the credentials on your wall or the titles on your resume."

"When I resigned as a public company executive, I thought my future would come from buying and running a company. "

"But when I started writing about the truth of leaving (on this platform), about the frustration, the loneliness, the lack of control in my own life, the endless bad mood, and the inability to escape the trappings of success—I discovered something I didn’t expect."
""
"People just wanted to talk to me. Not because of a title or an association with my company, but because they could relate to my story. It gave them space, hope, and a home. They wanted to talk to me for being me."
""
"They connected with the honesty. They felt what I felt and were looking for someone who might understand."
""
"Some dude on the Internet who made what they were feeling … normal."

"No way."
""
"I thought my gifts were in deal-making and strategy. Making money for other people. Motivating and inspiring teams. Hitting the quarter and closing the deals. "
""
"Turns out, my true gifts were much more ordinary: listening, asking the right questions, creating space for honesty, and building a community of people like us who build lives like this."

"None of that showed up on a resume. It never had a chance."

"I had a call recently with a man named Caleb. He’s a fifth-generation farmer, a precision ag leader, and a father of two young girls. His career has been defined by momentum—building teams, inspiring people, mentoring young leaders. His greatest strength? Passion. And he knew it."

"He hadn’t stopped caring. He’d just stopped feeling alive."
""
"And I know that story. So do you."
""
"I wrote about that quiet, aching transition in the book The Trade. Not the one where you fall apart—but the one where you begin to fall asleep. Where your gifts are underused, your schedule is owned by everyone else, and your calling feels like it’s waiting in the wings."
""
"Caleb read that book on a plane, sandwiched between his daughters, trying to make sense of why something inside him hurt. And it found him—right there in the middle of Toy Story 3 and Frozen. And for 30 mintes, as he read Chapter 12, he wept as he read. And if you’ve read it, you know why. "

The 25 Lessons of A Thousand Rambles:"
""
"After a few thousand rambles, you learn a lot. You get good at your craft. You build a deep research library. Then you have a choice: keep it to yourself, or share it freely."
""
"I’m sharing it freely."
""
"These aren’t theories. They’re not pulled from a book. They aren’t made up or random."
""
"These are the true stories I hear in real conversations with elite performers. The men and women who spent decades climbing. Those who earned everything they told others they would. Those who get there look around and then wonder what happened…to the way they used to feel. Those who quietly wonder:"
""
"“Is this it?”"
""
"I’ve collected the stories for one reason: To identify, and name the process and patterns that appear repeatedly in doctors, lawyers, VPs, engineers, founders…, and anyone who has worked 20 years to get exactly where they are."
""
"The journey is uniquely thier own, but the emotions of the journey, the patterns and the process to move forward? They’re  universal."
""
"Not always in the same order."
"Not always in the same words."
"But always the same shift."
"Always the same emotion."
""
"Each week inside The Inevitable newsletter, I’ll unpack one of these 25. I’ll tell you the real story of the person behind it. The frustration that triggered it. The language they used to finally say it out loud. And what we did next."
""
"You’ll hear where they are now—and maybe, where you are too."
""
"The image below is the full reading list of lessons."
""
"Sign up for my free newsletter."
"It’s called The Inevitable. "
"Letters from me to you."
""
"#normal40 #beuptosomething #leadership #careerchange"

""
"I never know who I’m going to meet every time I hit “Join Meeting.” "
""
"Today, in The Inevitable, we’re going to meet Tim."
""
"Turns out he wasn’t broken. "
"He was being called."
""
"This is his story.

The Pre-Dawn Drive: Episode 2 — “The Not So Dead End”"
""
"No map. No destination. No place to be, no one to see, no deadline to fill. Just the open road, my thoughts, and, well, space to write."
""
"I’m sitting on a dusty Florida road. Far from what most people think when they think Florida. I found a county sign that said “No Outlet” and a man-made sign that said “Dead End.” That told me a stodgy crumudgeon probably had a residence at the end of the road."
""
"Turns out, I was right."
""
"But as of this writing, his chickens don’t yet care that I’m at their dead end, his horse seems chill, and the sunrise is…incredible. "
""
"Since Feb. 2022, I’ve posted more than 2000 times. "
""
"Somewhat incredibly, my posts have been seen more than 11 million times. But who's counting …"
""
"In all of those posts, I’ve never talked about faith. Not once."
""
"Not because I don’t have it or don’t practice it. Mainly because the people who find me, the people I talk with here, the person reading this, gets to see their faith their own way. "
""
"And so do I."
""
"I believe in a lot of things. I believe in the power of a sunrise, the unforgiving force of the ocean, the vastness of the prairie—and that if you listen, all of them can give you some perspective."
""
"I also believe that there’s a force in the world that nudges us. That moves us. That shows up and tries to guide us. But most of us are too busy or out of practice to hear it."
""
"We don’t ignore it. We simply don’t slow down enough to think, “Wait, that was interesting.”"
""
"Today, I had one of those omens. It happened about six minutes ago. It was an omen to share a short story about faith…and the wonders of what might find us…if only we bother to listen."
""
"During my morning drive, a single sheet of paper blew around the cab. Small, white, and folded, it was just outside of my reach. It floated and flittered on the passenger floor and then under the seat."
""
"I eventually stopped at The Dead End and got out to watch the sunrise, talk to a horse, listen to the chickens, and then write about whatever came to mind."
""
"As I started to write, I remembered that small, flittering piece of paper. I grabbed it and placed it in my pocket to discard. Then I saw writing on it—not my writing, but the handwriting of my 12-year-old son."
""
"An Omen."
""
"Not only for me, but for anyone who bothers to read my writing.  No, wait—for anyone who bothers to read Oliver’s writing."
""
"I don’t know when he wrote this. I don’t know how it got into my Jeep. I can’t imagine the sequence of events that brought it to this Dead End Road where I’m sitting now…and I can’t imagine who is also reading it now."
""
"But if it found you, I hope you use it. "
"After all, this is an omen."
""
"Turns out, there are no dead ends."
"There are answers in the flittering."
"There are omens in your life."
""
"According to Oliver, all you have to do is “be honest with God.”"
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40 Highway."
"Share this with someone who needs it."
""
"Today, be an omen."
""

Sometimes, all it takes is a new view of the same scene to give you an entirely new perspective. Sometimes it’s in a sunrise."
""
"I woke up this morning craving a sunrise. Not uncommon for a kid from the prairie. Just some coffee, serene beauty, quiet, calming, warming body, awakening birds and an inspiring view. "
""
"So, before 5:30, I hopped in the old Jeep and started driving towards the place that offers free sunrises every morning…I started driving East."
""
"I needed to chase a sunrise—I could feel it. There was something about it that my heart, soul, brain, energy … something I can’t articualte needed it."
""
"So, I listened."
""
"Turns out, chasing a sunrise on Florida’s Gulf coast is harder than you might think. "
""
"Well, maybe that’s not true. But chasing a sunrise that’s unobstructed by human chaos…that’s a bit harder to find."
""
"I found myself wandering. No map. No target destination. No place I had to be. No one waiting for me to arrive. No one wondering when I would arrive. No one offering opinions."
""
"Just me, a cup of coffee, a jeep, and a restless soul."
"Just me, doing what felt pure."
"Just me, and a moment."
""
"As I left, I didn’t know what I was looking for. "
"Yet, I knew I would find it…Or, "
"Maybe, when it found me."
""
"All I took with me was a sense of direction and an appetite of wonder and curiosity."
""
"And with that, it’s all upside. It’s all progress. It’s all momentum."
""
"It’s too late for you to chase a sunrise today. But if you’re one of the lucky ones, you’ll have the chance to do it tomorrow."
""
"All you have to do is wake up early, make a cup of coffee, and start moving east with a sense of wonder and curiosity."
""
"It will create an ease of mind and a calm of body that you forgot was available. It will be the most pure thing you do all week. And you’ll wonder why you haven’t done it more."
""
"And if you’re feeling especially connected in that moment, throw up a few lines of thanks for the things in your life you literally walked by this morning to get where you are. After all, just look around you now…damn."
""
"When your soul is restless, my advice? Listen to it. "
"It’s not trying to deceive you or trick you; it’s trying to show you."
""
"And I’m here to remind you to take time to hear it."
""
"Today, go be up to something."
"And make it yours."
"Make it pure."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40, boss."
"You’re going to like it here."
"I built it for you."
"So, use it."

I woke up today in Linares, Mexico. A place that only a few months ago was not on my “places to visit before I die.”"
""
"But life has a funny way of moving you, but only if you allow yourself the gift of listening to it. "
""
"To be clear, the call didn’t bring me here; listening to it did."
""
"As I sit here, alone, in a little Spanish-inspired villa nestled next to a dusty street, contemplating how I got here."
""
"That pondering takes me back to a single question that used to occupy my mind more than I care to admit. It found me in the months before I decided to leave my W2. It caused me to wrestle with a new truth."
""
"The question:"
"“What if you’re more powerful than you think?”"
""
"What if the best work is still inside of you, and it can’t be found where you are standing?"
""
"I used to talk myself out of trying something new all the time, hoping something obvious would come along and make me feel like I used to—something that looked logical on the outside and would give me something new to do in the meantime."
""
"Nothing came along. So, I left…to bet on me. I listened to my gut."
""
"I had no idea how my life would unfold over the next few years. I had no idea who I would meet, what I would do, or where I would travel. I had no idea I was one act of courage away from it for years."
""
"Yet, here I am, waking up in this small town nestled in the Mexican countryside. All because I felt called to do something more than protect what I had spent 15 years building."
""
"I’m in Linares with my son, Dawson. At age 19, he has a knack for trusting his gut; for knowing where he’s being called—and following it. It took me to age 45. He has it dialed in as a teenager."
""
"To be clear, Dawson isn’t here with me; I’m here with him. "
""
"This is his new town, his new mission, his passion, and his calling. I just happened to get invited, and I am learning."
""
"Through a series of connections most wouldn’t believe, connections I’ve had a front-row seat to watching unfold, Dawson felt called to spend a year of his life here teaching English to 11-year-olds in a small Christian school—kids who might never otherwise get the chance to learn the language and meet and share life with (albeit with some bias), one of the finest young people the United States has to offer."
""
"And I think it was all possible because D never bothered to question his power, or the call. He simply followed it."
""
"Today, before you talk yourself out of trying something new, before you wait to build the clarity on what might be next, before you assume it’s not possible for you right now, and maybe never…Remember—you’re more powerful than you think."
""
"Listen to your gut. The one that holds the calling. The one begging you to slow down enough to hear it—and then do something to act upon it."
""
"It’s time to be up to something."
"You’re more powerful than you think."
""

""

"You’re not lost. You’re not broken. You’re ready."

This is the last mile of a four-mile lap I’ve run hundreds of times."
""
"In the distance, you can see the farm where I grew up."
""
"Everything in this photo was placed there by my family. Everything."
""
"Even the road was vacated by the township and is now under the care of our family."
""
"After all, according to the township, this road doesn’t really lead you anywhere."
""
"It's ironic how differently we can choose to see the same thing."
""
"On this mile, the last mile, I chose to run with my head up."
""
"In the miles before, I could be distracted by uneven ground, the curiosity of a neighbor’s operation, thoughts of fatigue, or a nearby sound."
""
"But on this mile, the last mile, I run above the pain as I reel in the distance between where I was, where I am, and where I started."
""
"It’s the return to the place that built me. And I choose to see it."
""
"The four-mile trek around the section has become a metaphor for life."
""
"The aggressive start down the road as we leave home to prove to the world who we are."
""
"The second mile, uncharted territory, moving us away from our roots. We are measured by our pace, move farther away, and chase whatever is just up the road."
""
"Then there’s a turn, literally, spiritually, physically, and emotionally, as we start mile three. When we grow tired of the chase, we choose to see our surroundings…differently."
""
"In mile three, what matters shifts, and we long for something … pure. "
""
"Something that matters when the race is over. Something of our own."
""
"Then, in mile four, the final mile, the outline of home in the distance starts its gentle tug."
""
"As I move closer, step by step, the memories of a thousand laughs and a thousand struggles romance around in my mind."
""
"In the final mile, I give thanks for each step behind me as each prepared me for this."
""
"And I thank the mile ahead, the mile that’s been vacated, the mile that’s my own, as it delivers me back to where I started. "
""
"Home isn’t the place you ever truly leave. It’s the place you return to, physically, mentally, or spiritually, when you need it. "
""
"Home is a place you rediscover…only after you decide to lift your head and look for it."
""
"There are two ingredients you’ll need in the final mile: "
"1.) Clarity for what you’re running towards, and"
"2.) A team who will show up on the dusty, dirty, vacated road to cheer you on. "
""
"If you have a place, pay it a visit. And a park will do perfectly."
""
"If you have a tribe, connect with them today."
"If you need a tribe, join ours."
""
"I couldn’t love the #Normal40 tribe more."
"It’s real, and you’re invited."
""

""
"He had missed it."
"Not physically. Emotionally. Intentionally."
"Damn."
"And later that night, when the house had gone quiet and the hotel room went dark, he couldn't sleep. He was frustrated, pissed and confused. So, he scrolled. He wandered. He looked for something to silence the ache he couldn't name and fill the gap that was ripping open in his chest every time he thought about Monday…and his return to the office."
"And then he remembered a post he had read from someone he'd never met. Something from a dude named Lon who had just quit his C-suite job. He said something about money. Something about retirement. Something about using your future to bet on your present."
"What if some of your savings weren't meant to be used in 20 years?"
"What if it was there to build the next 20 years of your life? To create the life you want now?"
"What if you took a small part of what you have and used it to bet on yourself?"
"Tim set his phone down. And for the first time in weeks, maybe months, he slept."
""
"Tim had everything he needed: Permission to be someone new."
"That's what a moment of truth feels like."
""
"Not loud. Not cinematic. Just quiet clarity in the middle of a storm. A moment when what you have to do becomes clear, even though it will be hard."
"Tim is not an outlier."
""
"He is one of the thousands I've rambled with since 2022."
"One of the thousand men and women who have sat across from me, through a screen or across a table, and whispered the same confession:"
""I don't think I can do this anymore.""
"___________________________________"
"If you'd like to read the rest of Tim's story, you can find it on the Normal 40 page. "
""
"These are not stories of change for others; they are the seeds of change in you."
""
"It's time to be up to something. "
"And you can feel it."
""

"Ready to stop defending a title that no longer fits."
""
"Most don’t lack courage. Just look at where they are."

"You need a guide."

""
"But I see you. And I’m coming for you."
""
"I’m launching something new. Something different. "
""
"Something that raises the bar…for me. "
"Something that lands even harder…for you."

"What I’m creating is raw, real, and lasting. It will be the best work of my lifetime. "
""
"And while I’ve tried to avoid it … It’s inevitable that I try."

"You can lurk or launch. "
""

"Doing it is a vote for you…It’s the next step in finding the answer to a problem you’re almost ready to admit you have."

"Doing this is a vote for the person who needs you to show up as the person they need you to be. The person you don’t yet know and can’t believe is waiting for you—for your gifts and your story."

"Over the next year, I’m going to share the best work of my lifetime, so we can get busy creating the best work of yours."

What do people respect about you?"
""
"It’s not something we ask out loud. We’re not supposed to. But it lives just beneath the surface of almost every decision we mak. Especially once we’ve spent years building something that tells the world exactly who we are…as judged by what they can see."
""
"Most people think respect comes from doing hard things well. And for a while, that’s true. "
""
"You show up. You perform. You deliver. You become dependable. "
""
"People trust you. They defer to you. They invite you in and ask your opinion. "
""
"And slowly, something forms around you: a reputation. A narrative. A version of you that others begin to expect. So, you live into it a little more each day. "
""
"Then, at some point, you begin to protect it. You become convicted by it. On the outside it’s something more to admire. On the inside, it’s something more to protect."
""
"That’s where the problem starts. "
""
"Because respect and reputation take years to build, but only a moment to lose. "
""
"And when the day comes that the title no longer fits, or the job no longer fulfills, or you’re quietly thinking of leaving it all behin…you hesitate."
""
"Not because you’re uncertain about the decision."
"Because you’re uncertain what it will cost you."
""
"You wonder what happens to the respect when the role is gone."
"You wonder what happens to your identity when the routine disappears."
""
"You wonder what people will think when you choose something for yourself."
""
"And here’s the part no one tells you:"
""
"When you finally take the risk—when you leave, when you leap—something unexpected happens."
""
"You gain something far more powerful than external approval."
""
"You gain self-respect. "
"You release a new version of you."
""
"And the hardest respect in life to earn, and the easiest to lose, is that one. The respect of self, simply for being who you are. "
""
"That’s the respect that doesn’t come from a title or a paycheck. It comes from alignment. From choosing a path you know is yours, even if nobody else understands it yet. From knowing what you sound like when you get to sound like you…and to do it without fear of consequence."
""
"Ironically, that’s my definition of freedom. "
""
"That kind of respect doesn’t come fast."
"But when it does, it stays."
""
"And it changes everything."
""
"Today, be up to something."
"Be Inevitable. "
""
"You can keep this to yourself, or you can share it."
"But I hope you choose to share it. "
"Like it. Comment on it."
"Or not."
""
"I’m here when you need me."
""

"It’s not burnout. It’s not boredom. It’s worse. "

"That call? "

"The very thing that got you here, wants to take you there."

"Wasting."

"If you weren’t so caught up in who you need to be for others, maybe you could come to terms with the fact that later today you could start. "

""
"But make no mistake, if you’re reading this, you’re living beneith it. "

"So, what if the weight you’re carrying isn’t burnout? "
"What if it’s the burden of unlived potential?"

"I’m writing my next book on Substack. I’ll share the whole thing as I write and as it comes together. Learning how to meet your potential is a central theme. You would love it."

"Here’s the first rule: you don’t need any more motivation."

"They get you out of your head. "

""
"And, honestly, I’m sick of seeing you bury your best days."
""
"So, no, burnout isn’t killing you. "
"That’s an excuse for you to blame something else."
""
"You’re squandering your potential. And that’s on you."
"That kills you slowly."
""
"Okay, gotta run. Heading over to my Substack page. I’m coming face-to-face with my own potential over there. "

There is a fine line between loyalty and obedience. Don’t be fooled."
""
"“I was let go last week after 24 years with my company. I knew layoffs were coming, but I thought my service to them would be enough to save me.”"
"— Tim, N40 Ramble"
""
"“Our company sold two years ago. Since then, it’s all changed. As the company I used to love to work for slowly died, all that remained was me.”"
"— Jen, N40 Ramble"
""
"Loyalty is an admirable trait."
""
"But if it doesn’t work both ways, it’s not loyalty. "
""
"It’s obedience."
""
"Here’s the hard truth: if you don’t have a Plan B, everything rides on your Plan A. "
""
"It becomes something you can’t lose. Something you would do anything to keep—it’s all you have. "
""
"Soon you are a tool. A lever. A cog. And most days, nights, and weekend check-ins, you’re probably pretty obedient."
""
"And it sucks. "
""
"Most people don’t need a better Plan B."
"They need a new Plan A—before they’re left with neither."
""
"Starting your Plan B is easier than you think, and you’re more ready than you know. If you worked 10% as hard at a Plan B as you did at a Plan A, you wouldn’t be wondering what it might be, who you might help, and how much you might make."
""
"You would know. "
""
"Courage to try gives you options."
"Options give you control."
"And control gives leverage."
"Leverage gives you courage."
"Courage give you options…."
""
"See how that works? "
""
"I’m showing people how to start, just as Tim, Jen and a few thousand more have done. "
""
"Their Plan B started when they did three things:"
"1.) Booked a ramble with me"
"2.) Joined The Insider—my closed, private community "
"3.) Connected on Substack, where I share it all (see comments)"
""
"Today, these are all available to you. "
""
"Now, what are you going to do with it?"
""
"Be up to something. "
