A note about my Mom: In June 2023, I hosted a small event to launch my book, The Trade."
""
"It was the first time I would speak publicly about what was on the pages. The first time I’d say my words out loud, in front of people who mattered to me. People who knew parts of the story—and people who were part of it."
""
"Before that night, I used to wonder who would show up for me if I ever made The Trade. That night, I found out."
""
"I will never forget who was there for me that day. Every moment, every conversation, every face—it’s etched."
""
"But this photo—it’s something else."
""
"This is my mom. And she just heard a story she’d never before heard."
""
"Sitting near the front of the room, she listened. She watched her son speak, hearing him tell a story that started long before the book did. It started in South Dakota, on our family farm—the place I still call home, where my parents still live, and where my sister and her family operate today."
""
"I open The Trade with that story because it is a story of hardship, risk, and perseverance. It’s a story I tell, but it’s one my mom (and dad) wrote."
""
"The 1980s farm crisis hurt more than the land—it cracked the families who lived on it. It emptied homes, broke banks, and drained bank accounts. It broke hearts, broke dreams, and ripped open souls."
""
"I was ten."
""
"I didn’t understand economics, inflation, or interest rates, but I understood silence, tension, and that something sacred was being tested in our home. I was given a front-row seat to the incredible power or grit, the unwavering value of a family’s name, and the fact that nobody can take your integrity—try as they may."
""
"And I understood—later, much later—what my mom did in those years."
""
"She held the damn thing together."
""
"All of it."
""
"She did it by taking a job for groceries and some insurnace, but only after making a hotdish so we would have lunch. She did it by managing calendars and with her quiet courage. And her grit, and her integrity."
""
"She did it with no spotlight, no applause, and no assurance it was going to get better. She did it through winter after winter, when the numbers didn’t work and the weight didn’t lift."
""
"She didn’t lead with speeches. She led with presence."
""
"This photo is that presence. This was taken in the moments after she heard me tell the story—that story—as lived through the lens of a younger me."
""
"It’s the quiet strength of a mom who kept the house steady when the whole world was shaking. It’s the look of a mother watching her son share her story without saying her name."
""
"Because she never asked for credit. "
"She just kept showing up."
""
"So today, on Mother’s Day, I want to say … thank you for being the world’s greatest mother. For always showing up. Always putting others first, and …"
""
"Always giving the hugs."
""
"With much love,"
""
" - Lon "
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #mothersday

It’s your image, stupid."
""
"Not the job. Not the pay. Not the work. Not the fear of starting."
""
"It’s the fear of leaving."
""
"The fear of no longer having this title, this office, this white coat, this authority, this pension, and these associations."
""
"The fear of putting aside the image you’ve worked so hard to build. The image anyone would trade for in a moment."
""
"It’s the version of you they trust, promote, quote, depend on."
"The one who has it together."
"The one who’s always solid."
""
"And you are. Or at least you were. When it felt as good on the inside as you work to make it look on the outside."
""
"Without even realizing it, you’ve stopped growing. Not because you’re not capable or that you’re lazy. Hell no. And not because you don’t want more, but because you can’t imagine who you are if it’s not some version of more of what you already have."
""
"You can’t even, in your mind, imagine who you might be if not this."
""
"And here’s the worst part: Nobody does this part for you."
""
"Nobody shows up and says, “Here, do this.”"
""
"Well, actually, they do. But be prepared for a pitch on the easy living of a franchise owner. "
""
"I hear it so much that I’ve named it: 𝐄̲𝐬̲𝐭̲𝐚̲𝐛̲𝐥̲𝐢̲𝐬̲𝐡̲𝐞̲𝐝̲ ̲𝐈̲𝐝̲𝐞̲𝐧̲𝐭̲𝐢̲𝐭̲𝐲̲ ̲𝐁̲𝐢̲𝐚̲𝐬̲.̲"
""
"I’ve seen hundreds of conversations with high performers. "
""
"To be clear, I saw it in myself, first."
""
"People who’ve done everything right—until “right” stopped feeling…right. But changing it felt…irresponsible."
""
"None of these people wanted to retire or quit. None of them are afraid to learn and to push. None are afraid of growth. "
""
"They’re afraid of loss."
""
"Loss of respect. Of stability. Of the status that came with knowing exactly where they fit."
""
"They are only afraid of losing two things:"
"Income and Image."
""
"So they stay."
""
"Even though they know something’s off."
"Even though they feel themselves drifting."
"Even though the image no longer feels like them."
"Even though the best days of their life are fading. "
"Even though the best work of their lifetime is still in them."
""
"They settle. They wait. They sit. They obey. They tolerate."
""

It will find you at the top of your game, and you’ll ignore it."
""
"At first, it doesn’t feel like a problem. It feels like fatigue, a little frustration, maybe just a tough few quarters at work. You tell yourself it’s temporary, that after this project, this reporting period, this product launch, or this initiative, everything will feel good again. But after a few years, nothing comes back. Nothing feels like it did. And nobody is talking about it."
""
"But deep down, you know it’s something more."
""
"A few years ago, I left my executive role without a backup plan—only a hunch that I wasn’t the only one who felt what I felt: successful on paper, restless in private."
""
"So I opened my calendar. I called it a “Ramble.”"
"Just a conversation. No pitch. No agenda."
""
"After 50 calls, I noticed a pattern."
"After 100, it became apparent."
"After 300, I knew: This is a thing."
""
"I call it The Drift™."
""
"A time when you’re at your professional pinnacle but slowly dying inside. It doesn’t show up as a crisis. It doesn’t take your ability to perform. It takes something more precious—your voice, your clarity, your sense of control."
""
"It leaves you alone, frustrated, and completely unsure of what to do about it. "
""
"So you do what we all do."
""
"You drift."
""
"Here’s what I found:"
""
"People in The Drift don’t share a profession."
"Some are physicians. Others, executives. Military officers. Founders. Accountants. Pastors…."
""
"The resume doesn’t matter."
""
"What they share is something deeper:"
"	•	They’re tired of faking fulfillment"
"	•	They feel like they have no ‘responsible’ options"
"	•	They feel trapped in a life they designed for decades"
"	•	They feel like they are wasting their life’s best days"
" 	•	They feel completely alone with nobody to talk to"
""
"They use the exact words without realizing it:"
""
"Stuck, Lost, Tired, Disconnected, Empty, Restless, Ready, Trapped, Overwhelmed, Numb, Off-course, Burned out, Boxed in, Unseen, Unfulfilled, Resentful."
""
"They feel guilty for having it so good, and shame for feeling so little. "
""
"But most of all, they feel alone. "
""
"Nobody here is broken; they’re simply drifting."
""
"The only way to recover from The Drift is:"
"1.) Acknowledge that this is you, "
"2.) Accept that it is on you to deal with it—head-on"
"3.) Permit yourself to do something about it"
"4.) Act—Do something"
""
"The Drift isn’t the end. It’s merely a sign. A sign that you’ve outgrown the life you have, and you’re ready for something more."
""
"You can choose to be frustrated by that or invigorated."
""
"What you choose to feel dictates how you will decide to act."
"How you act dictates who you become in a year."
""
"Who you are becoming cures The Drift."
""
"Today, let’s be done with The Drift."
"Today, let’s be up to something."
"Today, grab the stick and pull."
""
"I’m here when you need me."
""
"#normal40 #uptosomething #inspiratin #leadership

You pay for it every day, but never see the incredible cost."
""
"It shows up in hesitation. In indecision. In the gap between what you know you should do and what you settle for instead."
""
"It’s accumulated in the moments you almost spoke up, nearly sent the email, almost made the post, nearly asked a friend, nearly booked a ramble—but didn’t."
""
"It’s the compounding tax of overthinking. And it’s paid in lost time."

"The Overthinking Tax is a head game. It won’t drain your bank account; it drains your clarity and energy, your confidence and peace, your ability to trust yourself, and your willingness to try."
""
"But externally, you think it looks smart. It looks like you’re being strategic, diligent, and responsible."

"But it’s not strategic or responsible, and you know it. That’s why you are here. That’s why you’re stilll reading. You’re paying the tax of hiding behind this post rather than writing one of your own."
""
"The Overthinking Tax is just fear os starting dressed like regard for what you already have.  And it’s keeping you stuck."
""
"And worse, it steals your best days and replaces them with … another yesterday."
""
"That uneasy feeling in your gut when someone else does the thing you’ve been talking about for months? That’s the tax."
""
"That feeling of friction every time you try to convince yourself you’re “not ready yet”? That’s the tax."
""
"The fact that you’ve been “thinking about it” for two years but think it’s too risky to try? That’s the tax."
""
"You’re not waiting for more information. You’re waiting for it to be safe. And this is as safe as it’s ever going to be."
""
"Here’s the truth: clarity doesn’t preceed action. Wondering if it will work won’t give you proof. And Building in your head isn’t building anything."
""
"Clarity follows action, not thought."
""
"You don’t need more time to think. The only one being fooled by that is you. The only one wasting time is you. The only one who can pay the tax of lost time is you."
""
"You don’t need more thought; you need movement."
""
"You need to be #uptosomething."

"Life lives better that way."

In every lifetime, there’s a line."

"The one with energy. Presence. Peace."
"The one who isn’t proving anymore."
"The one who builds for purpose, not applause."
""
"The only thing between you and your potential is a line."
""
"A line that holds you back, locks you in, makes you wait, and steals your best days. "
""
"You can see over the line. Sometimes you can even reach over the line. But living on that side of the line requires you to cross it."
""
"It’s that line that brings you here. "
""
"And it’s this line that keeps you there."
""
"You don’t drift across that line. You don’t wait your way over."

""

"Your Second Resume is your personal proprietary."

""
"Ironically, it contains the answer to the prayers of someone you’ve not yet met—someone who needs your second resume so they have a chance in hell to survive theirs. "

"Your Second Resume is no longer something that happened to you. "

"We all have a Second Resume. "

Hi, my name is Lon Stroschein, and I’m a Lurkaholic."
""
"For me, the problem started small. I used to only lurk on nights and weekends. Scroll a little, read a few posts, maybe save one. It wasn’t a problem. I could control it."
""
"Then, something shifted. I don’t know exactly when, but it was around 2019. I was doing really well at work; better than most. Anyone would’ve said I had it made. But something inside wasn’t right. So, I started lurking more."
""
"At first, it was mornings before work. Then over lunch. Then every time I had a quiet moment—waiting for a meeting to start, standing in line, walking to my car, lying in bed at night. I even lurked in the bathroom."
""
"I justified it. I told myself I was “researching” or “studying what worked.” I said I was “preparing” to post. But I wasn’t preparing."
""
"I was hiding."
""
"Hiding from the discomfort of being seen."
"Hiding from the fear of being misunderstood."
"Hiding from the dream I hadn’t yet dared to chase."
"Hiding who I was to preserve the life I no longer wanted."
""
"I thought if I could just wait long enough, the right words would find me, the perfect post would write itself, the confidence would show up, and everything would immediately feel right."
""
"But it never did."
""
"𝐋𝐮𝐫𝐤𝐚𝐡𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐦:"
"𝘈 𝘤𝘩𝘳𝘰𝘯𝘪𝘤 𝘩𝘦𝘴𝘪𝘵𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘴𝘩𝘢𝘳𝘦 𝘺𝘰𝘶𝘳 𝘷𝘰𝘪𝘤𝘦 𝘱𝘶𝘣𝘭𝘪𝘤𝘭𝘺, 𝘳𝘰𝘰𝘵𝘦𝘥 𝘪𝘯 𝘧𝘦𝘢𝘳 𝘰𝘧 𝘫𝘶𝘥𝘨𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵, 𝘱𝘦𝘳𝘧𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘦𝘥 𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘦𝘳 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘨𝘶𝘪𝘴𝘦 𝘰𝘧 𝘰𝘣𝘴𝘦𝘳𝘷𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯."
""
"That was me. Then, one day, I posted."
"It wasn’t great, but that wasn’t the point. "
""
"I posted. I shared. My first post was a question. I even stared at that one-sentence question before posting it, but then I did the hardest thing imaginable:"
""
"I hit post."
""
"The curse of Lurkaholism started to melt, and my voice started to form."
""
"Maybe this is you."
""
"If it is, you are not alone. You’re not broken. And you’re not invisible. I see you. Hell, I talk to you, most days."
""
"But I do want you to know this: Posting tomorrow won’t be easier. Starting tomorrow won’t make it easier. Re-writing something 40 times won’t make it better. "
""
"The only thing that will heal your Lurking is posting, liking, or sharing something. Anything."
""
"There is only one thing on the Lurkaholic’s scorecard:"
" — Did you post something today?"
""
"That’s it."
""
"Did you share something. Ask something. Promote something or someone."
""
"When the answer is yes, you’re beating the addiction."
""
"Look, I’ll keep writing for you until the day you decide you’re ready."
""
"Because one day, the silence won’t sit right."
""
"One day, you will be done lurking; it will be your day to start."
""
"That first post won’t chang the world, but it will start to change yours."
""
"I know it’s possible for you, because it did for me."
""
"Welcome to Lurkaholics Ananamous."
""
"Most of us meet in The Insider."
"We meet on Thursdays."
""
"You are my people, and "
"I am your fan."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""

Interviewer: C’mon, Lon. These people aren’t really stuck. They’ve got money, options, resources. What are they so afraid of?"
""
"Lon: "
"They’re afraid of losing their identity, and I get it."
""
"They’ve spent 20, 30, maybe even 40,000 hours building the life they have. They are experts in being who they are now. The job title, the salary, the LinkedIn profile, the house, the lifestyle—that’s not just success. That’s who they’ve become."
""
"So what are they afraid of?"
""
"They’re afraid that if they walk away, the wheels come off. That they’ll fold into their biggest fear. That they’ll become the kind of person they used to quietly judge—the dreamers, the floaters, the ones who “couldn’t hack it."
""
"They don’t fear the thought of change; they want it. They fear the process of change and what it will cost them if they get it wrong. "
""
"They fear starting over. They fear the separation. "
""
"Because if your whole identity is built on always knowing what to do next, what happens when you finally admit you don’t? If you're known for having the answers—the right answers—what would it look like if you got this one wrong?"
""
"Better believe that’s scary. It’s ‘effing’ terrifying. "
""
"Look, identity matters. If you spend your entire adult life becoming something, it becomes your truth. That doesn’t make it wrong. But it sure as hell makes it hard to change."

"So, who are you without your title? "

Ugh. You’re thinking about it wrong. It doesn’t start with the perfect resignation letter or a perfect LinkedIn post. Nope…"

"The real battle doesn’t start on the outside; it rages on the inside."

"And it starts alone. It festers. It persists. "
"It brings you here."

"It starts with a feeling that something’s off, but you don’t know what. Like there’s more to give, more to chase, more to life and that something has to change…but you don’t know what."

"You know there’s a battle, but you can’t even see an enemy."

"But that’s not the problem. "
"That’s the symptom."

"The problem? "
" — Your conviction."

"Hold on, Rocky. Let me explain."
""
"Let’s be clear: your conviction is a gift. It’s a blessing. It’s what sets you apart and has burned you into the life you have. You know the battle. And winning the war was fueled by your conviction."
""
"And it works great—until it doesn’t."
"Like everything, conviction has a shelf life."
"And you’re is expiring."
""
"Conviction says, “This is who I am, damn it.”"
"Conviction says, “This is what I do, damn it.”"
"Conviction says, “This is where we fight, damn it.”"
""
"Ironically, Addiction says the same thing."
""
"But Addiction believes it simply because you believed it yesterday. It cares only about today with no thought of tomorrow."
""
"So, this begs the question: Are you Addicted to your your life, or Convicted by it?"
""
"Are you doing it because it was worth it yesterday, or because it’s taking you where you want to be tomorrow?"
""
"Right now… are you giving your best days to a conviction, or an addiction?"
""
"If the life you’re living is built on conviction—keep it."
"If the life you’re living is built on addiction—Trade it."

"The premise of The Trade is so simple it’s almost embarrassing…"

"Do this: Trade your conviction for your curiosity."

Dear terminated employee,"
""
"There’s very little more emotional than losing a job."
"It’s frustrating, frightening, embarrassing…and public."
""
"The walk of shame. The box on the desk. The awkward conversations. It’s an effing disaster."
""
"Nobody really knows what you’re feeling, so I won’t throw that crap at you. "
""
"But I know …. "
"You gave everything to that job."
"You gave it your years of incredible work."
"You gave it your mornings, weekends, and nights."
"You gave it your vacations and let it interrupt your dinners."
"You gave it your best ideas, your greatest energy, and more of your sanity than you care to admit."
""
"And they gave you a box to carry to your car."
""
"Whatever you are feeling, it’s not an overreaction."
"You’re not being dramatic. You’re grieving."
""
"And grieving simply means you gave a damn."
"And I admire that."
""
"What you are experiencing is  Corporate Detox."
""
"It’s coming for you, and you need to know it."
""
"And for most people, it takes 100–200 days."
""
"It shows up differently for everyone, but you’ll have all of the emotions…sadness, shame, embarrassment, anger, and regret, and then it repeats."
""
"But then something else happens: Your morning becomes THE MORNING."
""
"The Morning You wake up…different."
"The Morning you wake up…lighter."
"The Morning you wake up with an idea or an impulse."
"The Morning when how you see what happens changes, and you realize the blessings of what just unfolded."
""
"The Morning when anger fades, and the frustration ends."
""
"The Morning you wake up feeling like you."
""
"And that morning is coming for you."
""
"It’s The Morning when you take a real account of who you are really), what you have (really), and where you have been and apply it to what you might do next."
""
"It’s The Morning you realize that the company took a lot from you… but that it also just removed every barrier to chase something larger. "
""
"It’s the The Morning you come to discover that the only thing they took was your addiction to what was familair, and that everything else has come with you."
""
"It’s The Morning you learn they didn’t take away your options; they took away your reason to wait. And they gave you a reason to start."
""
"They didn’t stuff you into a box; they released you from it."
""
"You will go through Corporate Detox, and it will suck. "
""
"But when you come out the other side, there’s a whole new life waiting for you to chase it."
""
"A new morning is coming for you. "
"When it finds you—"
"I’ll be here."
""
"We’ve got work to do."
"And you are ready."
""
"It’s time to #beuptosomething"
""
"#normal40 #thetrade "
""
"PS - This is what The Morning sounds like at The Normal 40 Ranch.  It’s magic…and it hits all your senses at once.

Even the best advice has a shelf life. Take career advice through the generations as Exhibit A:"
""
"𝑨𝒅𝒗𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒇𝒓𝒐𝒎 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒈𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒕 𝒈𝒓𝒂𝒏𝒅𝒑𝒂𝒓𝒆𝒏𝒕𝒔:"
"Follow the work. Be thankful for a paycheck, food, and a roof over your head. In my day, we took whatever work we could get—indoors or out. Your goal is to make sure your family never goes hungry or wonders where they’ll sleep."
""
"𝑨𝒅𝒗𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒇𝒓𝒐𝒎 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒈𝒓𝒂𝒏𝒅𝒑𝒂𝒓𝒆𝒏𝒕𝒔:"
"Find stability. Get a job, and stay loyal. Don’t rock the boat—just do great work. If you get it right, by 65, you’ll have enough saved to buy a boat and some fishing tackle."
""
"𝑨𝒅𝒗𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒇𝒓𝒐𝒎 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒑𝒂𝒓𝒆𝒏𝒕𝒔:"
"Get a degree. Use it to land a job with benefits. Find a company where you can stay 30 years and retire with a gold watch and a pension. If you’re lucky, you’ll spend a few months each winter somewhere warm."
""
"𝑨𝒅𝒗𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒕𝒐 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒔𝒆𝒍𝒇:"
"Grind to the top. I’m 20 years into the life I chased, and it’s starting to suck. My best days are here—but I’m not living them."
"I have a greater story to tell and a greater dream to chase, but I can’t walk away from what I’ve built."
"Leaving would look foolish, and I owe it to my family to keep going until every possible downside is reduced to zero."
""
"𝑨𝒅𝒗𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒇𝒓𝒐𝒎 𝒚𝒐𝒖𝒓 𝒌𝒊𝒅𝒔:"
"Why don’t you just quit? Do something else. Wait, why ARE you still there? This makes no sense—and it’s the opposite of the advice you would give me. "
"Seriously. If you are not happy, stop wasting your time."
"You’re not staying there for us—we’re fine. You’re staying there for you. Do you know what we want?"
" - We just want our mom/dad back."
" - We just want to trust your mood."
" - We just want you to be present."
"I have no idea what you’re so worried about."
"______________________________________________"
""
"Everything has a shelf life—even advice."
""
"Today, throw out the spoiled advice and replace it with new."
""
"My advice? Find a tribe that is going through what you are. A tribe that will connect you, support you, and root for you. And you will do the same for them. "
""
"If you need a tribe, join ours. It’s called The Insider. We meet again tomorrow. "
""
"Today, be up to something."

“I’m sorry, but it’s time to get your affairs in order.”"
""
"The heart-wrenching advice given to other people. Until it isn’t."
""
"In your life, you will get an opportunity to hear this for yourself. We all do. "
""
"When it’s your turn to hear those words, you won’t be thinking of your 401(k), your IRA, or your automobiles."
""
"You won’t be thinking about the project due next Thursday, the quarterly results, your annual bonus, your promotion, your title, your image, or your memberships. "
""
"It’s only in your health that these things matter. "
""
"At the moment your health is taken, all of the above shed all of their value to you—their value plummets."
""
"Instead, the moment you lose your health, you will be thinking about how you will adequately express the depth of your love to your spouse and wish you would have done it more often."
""
"You’ll be thinking about how to articulate the pride you feel for your kids from the moment of their birth and wish that you would have made them a greater priority."
""
"You will be wondering if you created enough memories, done enough traveling, shared enough laughs, and offered enough guidance to last their lifetime, now that it’s outlasted yours."
""
"The affairs you will care about most in your final days will not require a court to split or a state to assess a tax."
""
"Your life‘s most valuable affairs are not the assets you accumulate in your health. "
""
"Your life’s most valuable affairs will reduce to the experiences you shared with those you love and will be measured by how you feel you did to show them."
""
"Getting your affairs in order doesn’t start with a diagnosis. Only the urgency does. "
""
"Getting your life affairs in order begins when you’re done reading this post.  It starts in your health."
""
"And if you’re lucky, you have that today."
""
"These are your lifetimes best days. The best you have left."
""
"So, that begs the question: "
""
"Are your affairs in order?"
""
"If not, be #uptosomething."
""
"Then, be glad you did it."

I quit consuming the news two years ago. It might be the best diet I’ve ever been on. Why did I quit? "
""
"Not because I stopped caring, but because I started noticing."
""
"Every time I turned it on, I left worse."
"Not informed—angry."
"Not empowered—exhausted."
"Not connected—divided."
"Not healthier—infected."
""
"And then I realized…that was the point."
"I wasn’t getting updates; I was getting programmed."
"It was changing who I was."
"It was making me a label."
"And causing me to label others."
""
"Fear was the product."
"Division was the strategy."
"Anger was the delivery system."
"I was the target."
""
"And wouldn’t you know it, I was addicted."
""
"So, I quit."
""
"I traded the time I was giving it and turned it into time I could use elsewhere. "
""
"I stopped feeding the beast."
"Stopped handing over my peace for a dopamine spike disguised as “breaking news.”"
""
"I stopped reacting to someone’s outrage, and started living as me again."
""
"You know what I lost?"
"Nothing."
""
"You know what I gained?"
"My time. My clarity. My voice."
"My ability to connect through curiosity."
""
"What goes into us always shows up somewhere."
""
"Want to lose weight? Change what goes in."
"Stop eating fast food for 100 days."
"Don’t change anything else."
"Watch what happens."
"You will change"
""
"The news is no different. I decided it was time for a diet. I worked the process: AAPA"
"Aware of a problem"
"Accept something needs to change"
"Permit myself to test something new"
"Act on the decision."
""
"And, it works…."
""
"The News Diet dropped the noise in my life faster than anything I’ve ever tried. It changed everything. "
""
"I see friends for who they are."
"Neighbors for who they help."
"Communities for what they need."
""
"It has allowed me to be curious about all I don’t truly know about someone instead of being convicted about what I think I do."
""
"And every one of them, and everyone I will meet this week, is better without a label assigned by someone else."
""
"If there’s a label maker in my life, please let it be me."
"I will build my labels using my curiosity."
""
"You should try The News Diet. "
"It will change what goes in, and it will change what comes out."
""
"I like this view. It’s quieter. It’s clearer."
"And the only thing I had to give up to get it?"
" — Fear of not being who I was."
""
"That’s a trade I’ll make every time."
""
"Join me. It’s free. And Freeing."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
" — Lon"

Dear LinkedIn,"
""
"My name is Lon. I really like it here. But lately, you’ve changed."
""
"Let me be clear—this platform changed the direction of my life. One post. One connection. One day at a time. I owe you a lot."
""
"I’ve also learned a lot. I’ve learned to share how I see things. So, I guess that’s what I’m doing with you."
""
"Look, whether we admit it or not, everyone here is searching for something they don’t yet have."
""
"Everyone."
""
"Even you."
""
"Me? I came here looking for connection. To see if anyone else felt like I did while at the top of their game."
""
"Turns out, I wasn’t alone."
""
"Most of us lurk for a while before we Like, Comment, or Share. But we do read. We take a long time to decide who is legit and who isn’t."
""
"That’s what makes your users so different—discernment."
""
"While quiet, we are here. Reading. Studying. Watching. Listening. Calculating. Nodding in silence."
""
"We also hope someone is crazy enough to say what needs to be said, mostly so they can judge it when it fails, or tell others later about how they almost said the same thing when it doesn’t."
""
"But I digress."
""
"In 2022, I started writing here. I didn’t know I was a writer."
"Turns out… maybe I am."
""
"So, I decided to post every day—or at least try."
"It was a commitment I made for me."
"After all, I don’t work for you."
""
"(Crap. Do I?)"
""
"Anyway…"
""
"I didn’t come here to go viral or build a brand. I come here to share and build."
""
"I come to talk to people who are quietly craving something more. People who need a friend, a Guide, or just to talk to a guy who has been where they are."
""
"I come here to build a space where people could feel—maybe for the first time in a long time—like themselves again."
""
"I come here to Link people In."
""
"Some take it, others leave it."
""
"LinkedIn was perfect for that."
"No filters. No noise. No distractions. No political garbage."
"Just clarity, connection, and real people showing up for genuine reasons. People who were searching…for someone to help."
""
"But lately… it feels like you’re trying to be someone else."
"And the truth? You’re not great at it. It doesn’t even fit."
"Or maybe I’m just not built for what you’re becoming."
"Either way, it’s a bummer."
""
"The platforms you’re mimicking already exist."
"They won the vibe war. They’re flashy. Entertaining. Loud."
"But they didn’t win us. You did."
""
"We’re not here to watch a reel."
"We’re here to do something real."
"To feel something real."
""
"The real voice. The real chance. The real bet."
"The real us."
""
"I know your algorithm doesn’t love me."
"Long posts. Black-and-white photos. No polish. No bait."
"By your metrics, I should be a blog."
""
"But I know why I’m here."
"And I know why I’m not there."
""
"It’s not because I can’t figure out TikTok."
"It’s because I don’t want the noise."
"And I might not be alone."
""
"So if you need me, and if you’ll keep me, I’ll still be here—"
"Writing. Sharing. Connecting. Guiding."
"In long-form words and in black and white."
""
"Thanks for allowing me to be up to something."
""
"If you want to #ramble, you have my number."
""
"— Lon"

Here, do this."
"— For most of your life, those three words carried you forward."
""
"These words were shared by teachers, coaches, mentors, bosses, and leaders—people who had a plan for you before you even needed one. The next step was always clear. Apply for this. Chase that. Take this on. Follow that path. Do this work. Avoid that risk. "
""
"Here, do this."
""
"Sometimes, it was exactly what you wanted. But more often, it was what you were supposed to do—according to others. "
""
"The system had a structure. You followed it. You worked the plan. "
""
"You advanced. You performed. You delivered. You earned the title, the trust, the status. You built the life you were told to chase."
""
"It was a gift, and it worked."
"Until it didn’t."
"Until today."
""
"Today, that voice is gone. Nobody is calling for you."
"No more “here, do this.”"
""
"At this point in your life, these people stop showing up. They quit giving you options. It quits being easy."
""
"And that’s where the confusion sets in. Self-doubt steps in. It’s when the imposter sets in. "
""
"You begin to question your value. Not because you’ve lost it, but because no one affirms it. The silence is unfamiliar. And it’s lonely. "
""
"This is the moment that breaks so many. It broke me."
""
"The moment when the world stops handing you the next step. It stops giving you what’s obvious. It stops giving you the answer to what’s next and starts demanding that you discover it for yourself."
""
"All that silence isn’t a sign of fading—it’s a sign of starting."
""
"It’s a sign—your sign—to explore something new. "
"It’s a sign to Accept that this is you, to Permit yourself to explore, and then to Act on your instinct."
""
"Your lifetime’s best chapter, the one you’re writing today, won’t come as a gift from others, but as a gift from your self."
""
"There will be a day when you will come to discover you have been made ready to chase something more."
""
"When that day finds you, Accept it."
"It’s a gift, but only if you take it. "
""
"Here, do this…"
""
"Be up to something."
"And never look back."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40."
"It’s a thing."

I was invited to speak at a private event. It was small, off the radar, and awkwardly intimate. No social posts. No cameras. No agenda. Only curiosity. I didn’t expect it to unfold like it did."
""
"The people in the room weren’t just accomplished; they were exceptional. They had spent the last 20 years building all they had. They had dedicated their life to a single profession, industry, business, or boss. "
""
"The reward? Titles, image, reputation, and the spoils of a life that checked every box success promised to deliver."
""
"They were the ones everyone else leaned on. Doers. Problem solvers. Steady hands in high-stakes situations. Loyal and dependable. But for the first time in their life, they weren’t sure what came next. And they didn’t know where to go to find out."
""
"Most were feeling burnout. Not from the hours—from the pretending. Pretending they still wanted what they had spent 20 years building. "
""
"As I spoke, most hung to every word. My job was easy—all I had to do was tell them who I saw as I looked at each of them. "
""
"Halfway through my ramble, making eye contact with each, I stopped and asked a question."
""
"Who have you told?"
""
"Four words, and the room melted. "
""
"Some told their spouse, but not everything."
"Some told a friend, but on the tee-box of #12."
"Some told a colleague, but it was a bitch session."
""
"Most hadn’t told anyone."
""
"But by being there, they were telling me."
"And I was listening."
""
"People like this—people like you— carry it. You hide it—because you solve problems, not create them."
""
"But the people in that room are not the people I worry about. The people in that room had not found a tribe—they had told me how they felt. Just being there was a start. "
""
"I worry about the people who never raise their hand and who never share how they feel with anyone. "
""
"The people I worry about are the ones reading this—wondering how I could know their world and wishing that one day they could relieve the pressure from the life they’ve built."
""
"And to do it in a way that didn’t wreck their image."
""
"I worry about the ones who are drowning and don’t know what to do about it. I worry about you. But …"
""
"I see you. I hear you, and I worry about you."
""
"Now, I want to connect with you."
""
"What am I selling: You. I’m selling you back—to you. I’m selling…"
""
"A space where you get to feel like you again."
"A space where you don’t have to hide."
"A space where your title, at work, means nothing to me."
"A space where you have an answer to: “Who have you told.”"
""
"And when you’re ready to ramble it, I’ll be there."
""
"The price of a call or to join a community is zero dollars."
"The price of never admitting this note found you when you needed it will cost you the best years of your life."
""
"Maybe more live events are what’s next for me?"
"Maybe doing one with you is next?"
""
"I’m ready to be up to something more…with you."
"The two of us make a tribe…"
"A tribe others will seek."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40."
""
"This is your tribe."
"This is your sign."
"This is your start."
""

This is not a post about politics. But the other side has a problem—and they seem unable to change."

"When they hear something they already believe, they lean in."

"They believe it without any real objectivity or proof."

"They used to think for themselves and could be reasoned with."
""
"They surround themselves with people who think like they think, talk like they talk, immediatly label like they lable, and post what they post—so they never have to rethink a position."
""
"They stay insulated from anyone else who might have a point."
"They refuse to think about a better way or to consider that maybe, just maybe, they can point out the flaws of those on their own side. "
""
"They refuse to allow for a conversation about where there might be common ground. "
"They are the problem."
"They need to change."
""
"And in doing so, they turn disagreement into division."
"They mistake noise for truth."
""
"This closed loop? It perpetuates the problem."
"It kills meaningful conversation."
"It turns neighbors into enemies."
"It hands our future to fear and division."
""
"And it’s not just politics."
"It’s business."
"It’s faith."
"It’s culture."
"It’s friendships, or what’s left of them."
"It’s every space where people dig in, close off, and let their identity be defined by “us vs. them.”"
""
"Why can’t they accept they are making this worse?"
""
"They continue to push a wedge rather than consider maybe, just maybe, I have a point in my thinking."
""
"All because they’re afraid."
"Afraid to step back."
"Afraid to admit the other side might have a point."
"Afraid to look out of step with the bigger voices."
"Afraid to speak out when we have something wrong."
"Afraid to believe that we are better together, all of us, than we are apart."
""
"I hope they find the courage."
"The courage to question their tribe."
"To extend an olive branch."
"To open a door."
"To listen without needing to respond."
"To offer space where there’s only been judgment."
"To feel about their neighbor like they used to feel about them."
""
"I hope they stop believing only what’s been fed."
"I hope they consider how much stronger we could all be if we did this thing together."

""
"They can do this first."

I’ve dedicated my life to understanding the quiet battles successful people silently endure—and why they do it."
""
"Today, Re-entry Syndrome (RES):"
""
"RES presents itself as you shift from leisure or vacation back into professional routine. It’s a psychosomatic condition—a powerful connection between mind and body—triggered by the abrupt loss of freedom, autonomy, and rest. It arrives at the crossroads of the sort of lifestyle you want more of, and what you’ve settled for to get it on occasion."
""
"This presents itselt through anxiety creeping in Sunday afternoon, deep irritability towards your inbox and calendar, trouble sleeping, mood swings, loss of motivation, fatigue, stomach issues and unexplainable avoidance. "
""
"There’s resentment toward the job you’ve chased but slowly outgrown, and cognitive dissonance—the internal tug-of-war between wanting to escape and feeling obligated to stay."
""
"RES isn’t a flaw. It’s a sign. The best sign the world can send."
""
"But you settle for the life you have rather than to accept this is a sign to chase something new. "
""
"What to do:"
"ACCEPT this is normal—valuable insight from within."
"PERMIT yourself to dream again, to evolve, and to explore something new."
"ACT on something new. Don’t do nothing. Find a tribe. Phone a friend. Book a ramble.  Put yourself on the hook."
""
"RES is a calling from future you—begging you to finally do it."

"The real question is, why did it move you."
""
"Why that story? Why that emotion? Why now? Why you?"
""
"There’s a downside to knowing the answer. It’s called your Responsibility to Act. Your responsibility to Act begs this question: What am I going to do with this?"
""
"Something?"
"Nothing?"
""
"Being inspired is easy. It’s entertainment. You hear a great story, feel something—and it requires nothing."
""
"But when you know why something moves you, you can no longer ignore it. Because now you see that you’ve been made ready for this moment. This requires more than nothing; it requires something."
""
"Something you’ve been built for. Something you’ve been made ready to do."
""
"And you will do something to test it, or you will wait."
""
"Waiting for the perfect time, the perfect plan, the perfect confidence, waiting for someone to make it obvious and easy."
""
"I waited for three years."
""
"But waiting is a thief—the thief of your life’s best days."
""
"If something has stirred in you—if you feel that pull—you have two choices."
""
"You can let it fade into someone else’s story. Or you can ask yourself why it mattered, and then answer the question:"
""
"“What am I going to do about it?”"
""
"It doesn’t have to be perfect. It just has to be more than nothing."
"It has to be something—something others can see."
""
"Make this your day, boss."
""
"Be up to something."
"Don’t do nothing."

The loneliest part of The Trade isn’t leaving your job. "
"It isn’t losing your title, paycheck, or routine."
"It’s leaving behind the person everyone else still expects you to be."
""
"The hardest part is that deep, quiet realization that no one—not your spouse, your kids, your colleagues, or your closest friends—fully understands the internal shift happening inside you."
""
"They’re still talking to, counting on, and expecting the person you were yesterday."
""
"But you’re not that person anymore."
"Most days, it feels like you’ve outgrown your own skin, your old story. "
""
"And you feel isolated because no one can yet see who you're becoming. None of them have stopped to consider who you are capable of being."
""
"In this loneliness, doubt creeps in. I promise you, it will."
""
"You second-guess. You wonder if you’re selfish, reckless, or foolish for wanting something different, something deeper. You wonder what might have happened if you had stayed. "
""
"It’s a brutal paradox: You finally know yourself enough to trade—but feel more invisible than you've felt in years."
""
"Here’s how you break through this loneliness:"
""
"Own Your Story: Speak openly about your trade. Be firm in who you are, what you do, and who you inspire and help. Remember who it is you are becoming, not who it was you became."
""
"Find Your Allies: A tribe, in this moment, can save your life. Connect intentionally with those who've walked this path. Community isn’t optional—it’s oxygen."
""
"Continue to trust the Quiet Voice: It got you from there to here. It won’t abandon you or lead you astray. It will however keep telling you to learn, to modify and to lean into what makes you, you. Stay committed to listening and acting on it."

"Remember:"
"The loneliness is real but temporary."
"The regret of never trying is real and permanent."
""

"Thank you for being up to something."

Costa Rica: Day Eight — The Great Irresponsibility"
""
"Three years ago, I thought quitting was for the weak. For people who couldn’t hack it—who burned out. Who lacked the courage to stay and fight for what they had built."
""
"I thought walking away was irresponsible."
""
"Today, I don’t think like that."
""
"I think people who quit on their terms, to chase their dreams and build something new, are inspiring."
""
"I don’t think betting on yourself is irresponsible."
"But never betting on the life you want certainly is."
""
"Three years ago, my life changed when I hit “Post.”"
""
"I was terrified. Who wanted to hear from me? I didn’t have anything others needed. And I didn’t have a voice—other than the one assigned by my company."
""
"I just spent nine days in Costa Rica. A trip that only happened because I once did something that looked irresponsible. "
""
"I spent the week with Jason Westlake , the owner of hundreds of acres of rainforest, and two people who started as clients and became friends—Dr. Patrick O'Malley  and Dr. Kirk Mahon."
""
"I worshiped with the Cabécar Tribe in their church, hiked to and swam under hidden waterfalls, drank tea with a Bribri family, crossed the river into Panama, and walked miles alone along a mostly abandoned beach."
""
"I met clients, made friends, watched The Trade impact people in ways I never imagined, and flew cross-country in a Cessna 206—because a friend thought it would give a better view."
""
"I ate things, smoked things, drank things, and consumed things that a more-responsible person might have avoided."
""
"But every day, I felt alive."
"Simple, purely, totally alive."
""
"I ignored emails. Turned off my calendar and missed meetings."
""
"And in doing so, I didn’t make myself invisible to others—I made who I was meant to be visible to me."
""
"This trip was, without question, irresponsible."
"This trip was, without question, a reminder of why I quit."
""
"To feel alive."
""
"Isn’t that what we all want?"
"To have a chance to feel alive?"
"To do something that connects us to who we are?"
"To do something pure without fear of consequence?"
""
"My life didn’t change in a moment of “eureka.”"
""
"It changed in a thousand little moments of, “Huh, that was interesting.”"
""
"It changed after I Accepted it was my time, "
"Permitted myself to start, and then "
"Acted on something new."
""
"It started by feeling … irresponsible."
""
"That’s the instinct that has brought me here."
"It has brought you here, too."
""
"If you’re here, you’re ready. "
"And I am, too."
""
"Book a ramble."
"Download the 14 Questions that Changed My Life."
"Join The Insider."
"Explore Vintage Coaching."
""
"Don’t do nothing. "
""
"Be up to something … irresponsible. "
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #jfds #inspiration

Costa Rica: Day Seven — Living the Life Unscripted."
""
"When you choose to live unscripted, if only for a week, don’t overthink the accommodations."
""
"Overthink the company. Overthink the experience. Overthink the wonder and the curiosity."
""
"Remove the comparison to what you’ve done in the past and embrace the adventure of not knowing how each day will begin, flow, or end."
""
"Because the thread count on the sheets won’t matter. The view from the room won’t matter. The five-star reviews won’t matter."
""
"What will?"
""
"Who you’re with. What you discover. The unexpected turns that happen when you trade plans for possibility. The presence you have to take it all in and let it unfold—as it was intended."
""
"It’s not the comfort of the bed that makes the trip—it’s the stories you bring home."
""
"So go. Be curious. Surround yourself with the right people. The rest will take care of itself."
""
"Live unscripted, if only for a week, and let it unfold."
""
"Be up to something."

"#normal40 #thetrade #jfds #costarica

Day 6: Costa Rica — The Stillness."
""
"Not silence—stillness."
"Not emptiness—space."
"Not loneliness—solitude."
""
"The kind you feel in a place where time slows to the pace of your own expectations."
""
"Where no one needs you right now."
"Where the world is big, and your worries feel small."
"Where the power you feel is the control over the moment."
"Where rain can’t dampen any plans, because there are none."
""
"A place where a chance encounter with a horse on a beach is enough."
""
"Find that place that gives you calm."
""
"It’s the kind of calm that reminds you:"
"You don’t always have to be somewhere."
"You don’t always have to be doing something."
"You don’t always have to be proving your worth to others."
""
"Beyond the noise, somewhere out there is a place that asks nothing of you. Nor does it need something from you. But it’s also a place that roots for you. A place that shows you what’s all around you, if you bother to look."
""
"Costa Rica is that place. But there are others. "
""
"Do yourself a favor this year: Find a place that allows you to be you—and go there. "
""
"Find a place that doesn’t demand a schedule or require a reservation. A place that doesn’t ask you to earn the right to be there. A place that allows you to be curious, off-grid, connected to who you used to be—and how you want to behave."
""
"Being still isn’t the problem."
"The inability to be still may be. "
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"#normal40 #jfds #thetrade #inspiration

Costa Rica - Day 4: The Quiet Voice Roars"
""
"The moment it became obvious: A bottle of 7-Up over ice at a beachside stand in Costa Rica."
""
"We all have a few voices. We have the one we use to communicate outwardly; it requires ears. We have a voice that communicates inwardly; it requires attention. "
""
"But there’s a third voice. It’s a quiet voice. It’s often covered up by the louder voice of your “could dos” and “should dos.” "
""
"This voice requires courage—odd, available, doable courage. "
""
"The third voice, the quiet voice, doesn’t shout. It doesn’t fight for your attention. It simply waits for you to slow down enough to hear it. When you do, you know it’s right."
""
"And that’s the terrifying part."
""
"Because if you accept what it’s telling you, it means change. It means letting go of something familiar. It means disrupting your pattern. It means others will see the change."
""
"It was after a long day of hiking—read slogging—through the jungle, then on a brief excursion into Panama and again on a hike near Manzanillo, that voice was there—a familiar one—the same voice I’ve spent decades ignoring and the past few years trying to trust."
""
"It was getting louder as the rest of the world became more still."
""
"Soon, the quiet voice started to roar."
""
"By the time I sat down with a 7-Up at a beachside pub, I wasn’t just hearing it—I was accepting that the voice was talking to me."
""
"Then, the hard part — I permitted myself to act. "
""
"That’s the moment everything shifted."
""
"For the next 100 days, I am going to quit alcohol. "
""
"Not because I have a problem but because it’s never offered a good solution."
""
"Since college, I’ve not lived 100 consecutive days free of alcohol. That’s not anything I’m proud of, nor am I ashamed of it. "
""
"It simply is."
""
"Maybe the same is true for you, too. Maybe a quiet voice is reading this and wants you to do the same. Maybe reading this is cracking open a door for you to try something…just to see what happens. "
""
"If you want to join me, you’re invited. "
""
"The clock starts today."
""
"I have one rule—you have to tell somebody. Doing so requires courage (you’ll see); if you don’t, the other voices in your head will destroy your chances of making it through the following weekend. "
""
"So, grab a 7-Up and join the club. "
""
"There is exactly zero downside."
""
"You are up to something."
"And I am a fan. "
""
"#normal40 #jfds #inspiration"

"Join The Insider if you want to do this with a tribe."
"Comment below if you really want to test your courage."
""
"Or, do nothing."
"It’s your ball.

Day 3 in Costa Rica — The Sound of Anxiety"
""
"Whenever I take a trip like this, something knocks me down."
""
"In Peru, it was my health. In Costa Rica, same story. Different place, same lesson."
""
"Day 3 was slow—a day of recovery. But more than anything, it was a stark reminder that the things that used to be effortless are getting harder—a reminder of time."
""
"It just slips away. Sometimes slow and romantic. Sometimes fast and wicked."
""
"For most of our lives, we use it like we have an endless supply—believing tomorrow will be the better day—tomorrow to make the call, tomorrow to take the trip, tomorrow to have the talk, seek the help, start the thing."
""
"But tomorrow is no guarantee. A reminder that every day you wait, it will be harder than it was yesterday."
""
"The same is true for your body. Your strength. The things you assume you’ll always be able to do. One day, they won’t be an option. They will be the things a younger you should have done. "
""
"This day forced me to slow down, to see what was right here, to immerse myself in it."
""
"And in that stillness, as I sat alone on the beach or wandered the streets, I noticed something."
""
"The sound of joy is the same everywhere. You cannot tell the difference between a child’s laugh in the tribe and a child’s laugh in the U.S. The language is different, but the sound is the same."
""
"The sound of sorrow and pain is the same, too—only the culture and customs change."
""
"But the sound of anxiety? It doesn’t exist here."
""
"There’s no walking and talking on phones and no sense of looming deadlines. There’s no open laptops, furrowed brows or general discontent in people’s faces. "
""
"There’s no rush in the traffic—no honking. People here yield to others. They yield their time, attention, and, if needed, resources. When they do, it’s given as a gift given to others, not as one taken by others. "
""
"And it’s not just in traffic—it’s everywhere."
""
"Even on a day when I couldn’t do what I wanted, the lesson was waiting: See yielding as a gift. Leave the anxiety to others."
""
"#normal40 #costarica #jfds #burnout

Day 2 — Lessons from Costa Rica."
""
"We had no agenda. No schedule. No one expecting us."
""
"Just three guys, making our way through the jungle, searching for a waterfall on Bribri tribal land. A waterfall that’s hidden from the rest of the world."
""
"The only way in was on foot. The only way to get there was with a guide. The only guide was one man, and we had no idea where he lived.  He doesn’t have a phone or an address, so we asked around."
""
"That’s how we ended up standing outside a small Bribri church in the middle of worship."
""
"As we approached, we tried not to interrupt. We failed spectacularly. "
""
"It turns out that three white guys stepping out of the trees during Sunday worship on tribal lands will draw attention."
""
"At first, only the pastor noticed us. Then, slowly, heads turned, one by one. Watching. Wondering. Whispering."
""
"We had no idea what to do, leave or stay. But before we could decide, several men stood, took our hands, and led us inside."
""
"They offered us their seats. Next to their families. They stood in the back."
""
"I didn’t understand a word of the service. I don’t know what the gospel of the day was or any words of the acapella hymns. But I understood what I saw."
""
"Mothers in bright dresses, swaying to the music. Children fidgeting, laughing, playing—because here, kids are allowed to be kids. Parents sharing knowing glances, passing a baby between them."
""
"It was community in its purest form."
""
"Two little sisters caught my eye. They’d sit through part of the service, then sneak outside to play tag with their friends. Back and forth, between worship and childhood."
""
"After the service, the pastor served lunch. Without hesitation, he ushered us to the front of the line—in front of the children and the women."
""
"Everything in me wanted to refuse. Everything about it felt wrong; rude."
""
"But here, turning down generosity is the real rudeness. Declining the invitation would have been rude."
""
"So, we ate. And it was incredible."
""
"Today, let’s be up to something."
""
"The next move is yours."
""

What have you already talked yourself out of this week?"
""
"The email you didn’t send. The conversation you didn’t start. The move you didn’t make—because this isn’t the week."
""
"What’s the thing you’ve been thinking about for a year but keep convincing yourself now’s not the time?"
""
"What is it? And why are you waiting?"
""
"Who are you waiting for? What are you waiting on? And what has waiting ever done for you?"
""
"We all get to this point and wait. We wait for it to get better, for the choice to be easy, for a sign to make it obvious, or for someone to de-risk it and make it free."
""
"That’s not how transformation works. "
"Ever."
""
"Change requires a trade—a trade from who you were to who you want to be next. "
""
"The Trade of what you used to want for what you want to do next. "
""
"The Trade of the way people know you now for the way people will need you next."
""
"And it will feel urgent long before it feels clear."
""
"Today, I’m packing for Costa Rica. A trip I’m taking largely alone, simply to see what happens. It is a trip that is happening because I made real connections in my new life."
""
"Every February, I do something incredible to celebrate the anniversary of The Trade. "
" — 2022, I quit"
" — 2023, I wrote The Trade"
" — 2024, I went to Peru"
" — 2025, I’m going to Costa Rica "
""
"There is nothing easy, obvious, or free about this trip. It is, in fact, a trade. A trade for the guy who wouldn’t, for the guy that would."
""
"The trade from a guy who worried about the price of doing it, to a guy who worries about the cost of avoding it."
""
"This I know: In two weeks, two months, two years, and two decades from this day, I’ll be glad I did it. And I will have long forgotten about the price."
""
"The longer you wait, the harder it gets. Confidence fades. Options shrink. Time slips. And suddenly, another year is gone."
""
"The thing you want? It’s not coming to you. You have to go get it."
""
"This window is closing. When it does, be on the right side of the glass. The side that is glad you did it. The side that didn’t settle. The side that made the bet. The side you’re hoping to connect with now. "
""
"This is what I do. This is who I am. This is how I act. And this is what I sound like—when I get to sound like me. And in a few days, I’ll be sleeping in the jungle with the Bribri Tribe, and I simply can’t wait to see what unfolds."
""
"Get some of this. "
"Get it from a friend, a sibling, a mentor—or get it from me."
""
"Tomorrow has more to offer than another candle on your cake."
"It has a life. It has energy. It has rhythm. "
""
"And it has you—if you bother to take it."
""
"Let’s be up to something. "
""
"You’ll be glad you did."
""

"PS—I’ll be posting from Costa Rica. You can follow me here, subscribe to my newsletter, or join The Insider, where I will do a few live calls while I’m there. "

"This is your invitation to something more."
"I sure hope you’re done waiting.

Jealousy gets a bad rap. A deadly sin, even. "
""
"Me? I listen to it. Jealousy isn’t a flaw—it’s a clue."
""
"Think about it. You don’t feel jealous of just anyone. You feel it when you see someone doing something you want to do. Experiencing something you want to experience. Writing something you want to write. Playing something you want to play. Leading something you want to lead. Chasing something you want to chase. Changing something you want to change and using the gifts you want to use."
""
"That’s not a problem; that’s an omen."
""
"Instead of ignoring that feeling, or resenting it, use it. "
""
"Listen to it. Study it. Dream about it."
""
"Pay attention to the people and experiences that trigger it. Then ask yourself, "Is that something I really want to do? "Is that something I really want to try?" "
""
"Then, if so, do something."
""
"Reach out. Ask questions. Get on a call. Learn from them instead of resenting them. "
""
"Jealousy isn’t there to shame you—it’s there to wake you up. It’s a raw, pure, hard-wired clue for what you want more of in your life. Don’t let old you tell you it’s not possible."
""
"Look, it’s frustrating to get this far in life and lack clarity on what you want to do next. But the things that make you jealous are a sign—but only if you see them."
""
"I leave for Costa Rica in two days. It will be just me, a bag, a camera, and a journal. I will hike, swim, journal, experience, camp with the BriBri, meet clients (who saw something in my story and called), and do whatever the omens tell me to do that day. "
""
"No calls. No email. No deadlines. No limits to what I create."
""
"Jealous? Good. I used to be jealous of this freedom, too."
""
"Then I did something about it."
""
"You should, too."
""
"It won’t be easy, obvious, or free. It will be a trade."
"The Trade of a lifetime. "
"But only if you make it."
""
"Today, let’s be up to something."
"You’ll be glad you did it."
""

Dear Past Self,"
""
"I know you are thinking about quitting your job—about leaving what is easy to chase what isn’t clear. I know you’re looking for a sign, wondering why you can’t be happy and waiting for what’s next to be obvious. "
""
"But most of all, you’re waiting. Tolerating. Observing …  "
""
"I know you’re worried about money and providing for your family. And if you leave, I know you’re worried about what happens if you get it wrong. "
""
"But right now, I know you have a dream—one that feels like a risk and doesn’t have a well-baked plan or a guarantee. One you can barely articulate, but you feel it."
""
"A dream that’s been building and brewing, but one that’s not got the attention it deserves, or needs."
""
"I know you’re worried about growing a following and questioning whether you have what it takes to fulfill your mission to inspire a change in a thousand lives."
""
"After all, how does one get paid for that? "
"Yes, that’s a legitimate concern."
""
"But I want to show you what’s possible if you listen to your instinct and pay attention to what you feel and hear bouncing around in your gut. "
""
"If you leave, you will have to do it before you have any proof that Normal 40 will work. You will have to start building an audience, and some of them will not like you—but that’s part of The Trade (oh, get used to those two words, by the way). "
""
"You will have to create your voice because you’ve forgotten what it sounds like when you get to sound like you. And you’ll have to give away all your time and gifts to build your craft into a process that will change lives. You will have to do the work."
""
"And then, and only then, will you find out if you’ll ever make a buck."
""
"But, dude …"
""
"From where I sit, just two years ahead of you, I can tell you this: What’s waiting for you after you start will blow your mind."
""
"You’ll discover that your gifts—and your craft—are needed and that others will be glad you are here. "
""
"You’ll come to meet people, travel to far-off lands, and inspire change that never, ever could have happened in your old life."
""
"You’ll come to find a voice, find a tribe, and live a life that you didn’t know was possible."

"#normal40 #jfds #inspiration #burnout

It was a Tuesday, five years ago."
""
"I was at my desk, staring at a calendar I didn’t control. Meetings. Calls. More meetings. A day packed with everything I once loved."
""
"But I felt… nothing."
""
"I had what I worked for—checked every box. Put in the hours, took the trips, did what others wouldn’t to get the title they couldn’t. I was living the life I wanted just a few years earlier."
""
"So why did it feel like something was missing? Why couldn’t I just want what I had? What kind of person gets this far and suddenly doesn’t know what he wants?"
""
"Well—me."
""
"That’s when it hit me: If I didn’t change something, I’d spend the best years of my life serving who I once wanted to be."
""
"That moment marked the greatest transition of my life—moving from decisions that were smart to those that were wise."
""
"It’s the shift from chasing what looks good on the outside—titles, offices, status—to building something that feels right on the inside. It's the shift from caring what others think to caring what you want. It's the shift from how to make more money to how to best use my time. "
""
"And that transition? It won’t feel like growth. It will be frustrating, confusing, and feel like loss. It will go against everything you know yourself to be. "
""
"But the part of you that got you here—the part you know and trust—has done its job. It has made you ready for what’s next, but it won’t be obvious what to do next. It strands you there—between what looks safe and what looks foolish. "
""
"When this transition finds you, and I promise there will be a day when it haunts you—day and night—it feel trick you into staying. Into stalling. It will convince you to wait, to toil, and to just get over the hump. But that day never comes."
""
"Right now, your smarts are begging you not to change."
"But your wisdom knows this is your time, that you are ready, and that these are the best days you have left to start. "
""
"So, you have a choice. The same choice I had five years ago."
""
"You can tolerate another yesterday, or you can do something today that gives you a shot at a new tomorrow."
""
"This is how life reshapes with you, around you and for you."
""
"The Smart will live another yesterday, and one day wish they had tried."
""
"The Wise will build a new tomorrow, and wonder why they didn’t do it sooner."
""
"Today, let’s be up to something."
""
"It’s your ball, boss"
"Pass or shoot."
""

Three years ago this week, I left my job."
"On that day, my last day,, I wrote this note to my son. "
""
"I wrote it sitting at my office desk—the one that would be occupied by someone else the following day."
"__________________________"
""
"Dear Son,"
""
"For everything in your life there is a first, and there is a last."
""
"Your life will be full of first days. These bring you experiences to be excited about, something to chase, and something that lies ahead."
""
"But in your life, because math being what it is, there will also be an equal number of last days."
""
"Your first and last day of work, your first and last day of school, of a relationship, of days with friends, of time in our house, and your time with your mom and me."
""
"A first and a last. Every start eventually ends."
""
"That is how life works, and it doesn’t discriminate."
""
"Son, in your life, live for the last days, not the first."
""
"For in your life, kiddo, it’s the last day that holds the score."
""
"A first day is about first impressions and fulfilling the aspirations of a person you may someday be."
""
"But your last day reveals the results; did you or did you not?"
""
"Your last day holds the score."
""
"Son, play for your last day."
""
"You see, today is my last day."
""
"And I choose to celebrate this day."
""
"I choose to walk the halls slowly, to give my thanks, and to be romantically nostalgic about how every day before this day has shaped me, while also knowing all that I did to help shape it."
""
"I choose to consider what these halls took from me, but also what I took from them."
""
"For 14 years, I’ve been building for this day."
""
"For 14 years, this day rested on a distant horizon."
""
"But on this day, my last day, we will share a sunset."
""
"Son, on this day, I choose to give heartfelt thanks for the friendships and the lessons I learned on a journey; time spent with some of the world’s best."
""
"On this day, I give thanks to those who took a risk on me and celebrate those who stay to carry the torch."
""
"In your lifetime, son, there will be as many last days as there are first days."
""
"On your last day, leave the company/friendship/relationship stronger, smarter, and better than you found it. "
""
"When you do, it will do the same for you. And when you do, you can take it all with you, wherever your path leads you next."
""
"And when you take it, you will have it."
""
"Today is my last day."
""
"I find myself nostalgic that my work here is done, but it is. I’ve completed what I came here to complete, and my future is no longer here."
""
"This is the finish for which I am prepared."
"And it’s a finish for which I am proud."
""
"Tomorrow, I will wake up to a first day."
""
"A day that comes before all of the rest."
"And I am ready. "
""
"This is my trade: Who I was for what I may become."
""
"I am in control, and choose to bet on me."
""
"And son, whenever you can, so should you."
""
"See you on the other side of today, kiddo."
""
"Cheers to the distant sunset."
""
"I’ll share a ride with you any day."
""
" — Dad"
""

“If I quit, what message am I sending my kids?”"
""
"I get this a lot, but it’s the wrong question. The better question: "
""
"What message are you sending if you never do?"
""
"Showing them how much you can tolerate isn’t the lesson they need. But …"
""
"Showing them how to do the math, draw the line, and chase something bigger before they feel ready—that’s a lesson they will use forever."
""
"In 2022, I quit a job I had worked for ten years to get. "
""
"In 2012, it was the job I wanted. "
"Ten years later, it was the job I thought I needed."

"There’s a moment in life when the ground beneath you disappears. You’re standing on what you thought was solid, and then it’s gone. Everything you thought mattered doesn’t anymore."
""
"Kevin Hoover knows that moment. He lived it."
""
"It was March 22, 2019, when he and his wife Suzanne found themselves sitting in a doctor’s office with their 4-year-old son. Baxley had been having stomach pains for a week, nothing out of the ordinary for a kid his age. The doctors wanted to run a few tests, just to be safe."
""
"Three hours later, the words "your son has cancer" shattered their world."
""
"Kevin shared his story on the Normal 40 Podcast this week, and it left me thinking deeply about how life has a way of forcing us to stop, to re-evaluate, and to face what truly matters. Kevin’s life wasn’t falling apart before his son’s diagnosis—by most accounts, it was thriving. He’d built a successful career in hospitality, transitioned into real estate, and was on a trajectory that many would envy. "
""
"But all of that became meaningless in an instant."
""
"And maybe you’ve felt it too—that moment when the suit doesn’t fit anymore, when the ladder you’ve been climbing suddenly feels like it’s leaning against the wrong wall. "
""
"This newsletter isn’t about cancer. It’s about clarity. It’s about the courage it takes to make The Trade—to trade what’s safe and comfortable for what’s real and meaningful. And it’s about the gift that sometimes, tragedy forces our hand."
""
"𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐜𝐞 𝐂𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐓𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐤 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐝𝐞"
""
"Kevin didn’t just face his son’s diagnosis—he and Suzanne decided to do something radical in the aftermath. Once Baxley was in recovery, they sold their house, bought an RV, and spent 18 months on the road with their son."
""
"They visited 12 national parks, saw the country, and even handed out ice cream to kids from a truck they converted into a mobile gift of joy. This wasn’t just a road trip. It was an act of healing."
""
"But here’s the thing: Kevin didn’t wait until life gave him permission to step away. He didn’t wait for a blueprint or the perfect timing. He looked at his family, at what they’d been through, and made the trade—comfort for clarity, routine for adventure, the expected for the extraordinary."
""
"He took the first step, not knowing what the second would be. "
""
"What Are You Clinging To?

"Leverage isn’t about waiting for the perfect moment. It’s about using what you already have today—to pursue the life and lifestyle you want tomorrow."

Climbing the second mountain is easy. It’s coming back down the first that scares people off. "
""
"Your life has windows. "
""
"They’ve been closing behind you for years. Your window for college parties is over (that was a good window). Your window for taking a nap with your 5-month-old is probably closed. Your window for a long talk with your grandparents is closed. Your window to get back to med school, law school, or to get a CPA is shut, or damn close. And the window where other people show up and take a bet on you is coming to an end. But you don’t see that one yet. "
""
"And, one day, your window to walk down the mountain on your terms with enough energy and passion to climb a new one will also close."

"It is, and I promise you, a window that finds us all."
""
"There is a time when the thought of never leaving becomes more irresponsible than the thought of leaving it for a bet on you. A time when staying on the mountain is more irresponsible than walking down with enough to find a new one."

"This is that time. This is that window. "
""
"To use all you have become and put it to work in a way you’ll forever be glad you tried."
""
"A window that offers no guarantee of financial success. "
"A window that guarantees you don’t arrive at the end of your life wondering what would have happened if you had tried."

"Nope, the hardest part isn’t starting something new. It’s accepting that it’s your last opportunity to leave something old—on your terms"

"But …"
""
"That’s what a guide does. They help you read your own map. "
""
"They see the clues you miss. They connect the dots you’ve been covering up. They prevent you from staying lost. They ensure you don’t stay until you get pushed off the mountain."
""
"There is a window. You’re half in and half out. "
""
"Before it closes, pick a side. Once closed, that part of your life is over. That’s just how it works."

"Whatever you’re frittering around with, be sure you’re on the right side of that window."

"Indecision is the quiet enemy of progress. It masquerades as patience, whispers that waiting is wise, and convinces you that doing nothing is the safest option. But indecision has a cost. "You’re not stuck because there aren’t choices. You’re stuck because you won’t make one. Waiting isn’t harmless; it’s a thief. It steals your time, your energy, and your potential. It lets the days pass while nothing changes except the regret piling up."

"Here’s the truth: waiting doesn’t solve anything. It doesn’t make decisions for you. It doesn’t bring clarity. All it does is delay the inevitable—until one day, you wake up realizing you’ve tolerated too much for too long."
""
"Indecision keeps you aging in place. Action moves you forward. The hardest part isn’t knowing what to do; it’s giving yourself permission to do it. Waiting feels safer, but it’s not. You’re betting your future on time fixing what only you can change."

"So, what’s the decision you’re avoiding? What’s the step you know you need to take? Today, stop tolerating. Choose to act. Because every day you don’t is another day you give to indecision—and it won’t give anything back."

It was 6:45 a.m., and I was staring at my phone, watching the spinning wheel as my flight confirmation loaded. Another week, another trip, another round of “I’ll make it up to you,” promises whispered to my kids before heading out the door. I told myself this was a success. After all, I’d worked my whole life for this."
""
"The corner office, the incredible bonus, the important meetings that made me feel indispensable—I was 40, and this was my harvest—I was reaping the rewards of years of grind and sacrifice."
""
"I had watched as others did it and dreamed of how it would feel. In precisely zero of those simulations did I think it would feel like it did."
""
"Hollow. Lonely. Frustrated. Never shut off."
""
"On the way to the airport, I thought about my son’s concert which would miss. He told me it was fine, but his eyes told a different story; kids are smart like that. I thought about my wife handling yet another bedtime routine alone. These things weren’t on my performance review, but they were the seeds I was sowing."
""
"I’d spent decades working toward this harvest, the first harvest, chasing an idea of success I created when I was 22. The grind made sense when I was planting the seeds. But when the harvest came, I realized the first harvest doesn’t always feed the soul."
""
"Weeks later, I was home in South Dakota for my family’s harvest. I’d been too busy to help with it for twenty years. But there I was, standing on the prairie, surrounded by fields and the wide-open sky. There’s a clarity you can only find in a place like that—a stillness that forces you to think."
""
"As I watched the combines rip seeds from stalks, the same thought kept running through my head: We don’t get to choose our harvest in the moment. We choose it in the planting."
""
"The seeds my dad planted in the spring were deliberate—he knew exactly what he wanted to grow before he ever started the combine. It hit me: the same is true in life. The harvest I was reaping that day resulted from decisions I’d made years ago. It was the first-half harvest from the first-half seeds."
""
"It was time to plan a different harvest, to find different seeds, the seeds of a second harvest."
""
"That was the moment I decided to make The Trade. I needed to do the work to find the seeds that created a second harvest, not a better résumé."
""
"The Second Harvest isn’t easier, but it’s better. The seeds you chose aren’t obvious; they’re elusive. The journey to find them is where I also found purpose. It’s where I realized the best things in life aren’t measured by metrics or bonuses but grown in the experiences we select and nurture with the people who matter most. It’s where I realized the greatest harvest I could ever chase is that of a ripple for others."
""
"Maybe this long note is that ripple."
""
"If it is, it’s time to be up to something."
""
"My mission is to inspire the change in a thousand lives."
"My metric is a thousand notes of thanks."
"My timeline is the rest of my life."
"My target is you."
"Follow me."
""

I am not a doctor—and this note isn’t for everyone."
""
"But I’ve been where you are, and I’ve felt what you feel. In the last two years, I’ve dedicated my life to understanding what I went through so I could put into words what you might be going through, too. "
""
"These are some of those words:"
""
"𝐀𝐬𝐩𝐢𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐟𝐥𝐢𝐜𝐭 𝐒𝐲𝐧𝐝𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐞 (𝐀𝐂𝐒) is a psychological condition characterized by a deep internal conflict in elite performers who have achieved significant external success but experience persistent dissatisfaction and an accelerating sense of misalignment with their current trajectory. ACS manifests as the tension between the desire to pursue something more meaningful, the fear of losing stability and identity tied to past achievements, a lack of clarity for what would be more fulfilling, and a growing urgency to make a change.  "
""
"ACS is fueled by four key factors:"
"	1.	Recognition: your current work no longer excites you."
"	2.	Awareness: your potential lies beyond your current role."
"	3.	Uncertainty: too much to lose—can’t get it wrong."
"	4.	Sunk costs: too much has gone into getting here."
""
"If this is you, then we have some work to do. I can’t make you do it, but I can show you what happens to most people when they do. I can show you that you’re not alone, that there is a better way, that you get to take all that you have with you and that they joy you once had for the work you’re able to do can come back."
""
"But it won’t happen on it’s one. It won’t start before you are ready. And you won’t be ready until you accept this note is for you—and that this is your time to do something."
""
"This is the most ready you’ve ever been and it’s the youngest you will ever again be."

On this day three years ago, I had made the decision to leave. I gave notice, transitions began, and the end of one of my life’s best chapters drew near."
""
"On this day three years ago, I didn’t have a resume to send out, a single client, a business model, or independent wealth. I did have my health, my family, and an explorer’s curiosity to discover who I could become with the gifts I’d spent years building."
""
"On this day three years ago, I had no idea I would create a community, start a podcast, and host calls with hundreds of people who wanted to share their stories. I called these meetings “Rambles.” And I could have had no idea that these rambles would spark a movement that continues to grow—a movement I decided to call “Normal 40.”"
""
"Because it’s normal, and it finds you when you have 40 years left to live."
""
"In that same year, I traveled to Hawaii with my wife, had epic vacations with my kids, spent time on the farm, celebrated my daughter’s graduation, picked up the guitar, and did the deep work of getting clear on how I wanted to live my best life."
""
"I also started writing The Trade, invested in two companies, and finished building Vintage One—my small-group coaching program that has changed lives. By year’s end, I had laid the foundation for financial, spiritual, and emotional wealth."
""
"I did it all from the sunroom of my home in South Dakota, and I did it all in a single year."
""
"I don’t know where you are in your journey, but if you’re here, it’s not an accident or an algorithm. It’s you. And if you’re reading this, listen closely:"

"You have no idea how different your life can look in one year."

""

""
"Let’s be up to something."
