Where are your blind spots? "
""
"No, not the one your boss tells you about. They focus on the blind spots they need you to improve upon. They focus on what they feel you’re not good at, what you miss, or what you’re not naturally wired to see. "
""
"Great. Thanks for that. "
""
"I’m talking about the other blind spot. The one/ones that don’t need to improve, they just need to be seen and defined. "
""
"In all of us, there’s something we’re really damn good at. Something we have a knack for. Something that just flows. "
""
"Often, this is so natural we don’t even know it’s a gift or skill. We assume everyone has the ability and are confused about why they don’t just do what you do. "
""
"You are the go-to person for things. Maybe you’re the one people delegate to have the awkward conversation with the boss. Maybe you’re the one who organizes the event. Maybe you’re the one who books the trip, closes the deal, sees the big picture, simplifies the real problem, or gives hope to those in trying times. "
""
"These can also be blind spots — the good kind. The kind we don’t stop and think about. The type that is hard-wired into who you are. The kind that should cause you to stop and ask: “What else should I do with this?”"
""
"Where are your blind spots? The ones that reveal the real you. The ones that you don’t get to use. The ones that fill you up when you do use them. The pure ones."
""
"Those are the blind spots you should work on. Those are the blind spots that will take you forward."
""
"Where are your blind spots? "
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"Go be more you."
""

It’s not about the Price or the Cost. It’s about the Margin."
""
"Every path you choose to take in your life has a Price. So, too, does it have a Cost."
""
"The sweet spot between is the margin you make along the way."
""
"Today, you will be paid a Price for the path you’re on. "
""
"You can see the math in your mind; the proof is everywhere."
" 	— The big projects"
"	— The never-ending meetings"
"	— The urgent emails only you can handle"
"	— The next trip to the next hotel for the next big deal"
"	— The push to meet quota or the quarter, and"
"	— The deposit into your account on Friday"
""
"This is the Price you’ve negotiated to live like you. It translates into dollars and things. "
""
"But what are the costs? "
""
"This math is harder. This math can’t be found on a day’s calendar, in your inbox, or a bank account. The proof is fleeting."
"	— The missed dinners at home in obedience to your email"
"	— The abuse of your physical and mental health"
"	— The time away from kids"
"	— The shallow friendships and constant grumpiness"
"	— The vacations that don’t get planned"
"	— The game of catch that you’ll have energy for tomorrow"
""
"This is the Cost you’ve negotiated to live like you. It translates into memories—the memories those closest to you will have, or miss, forever."
""
"Price — Cost = Margin"
""
"At some point, the Cost of this path outweighs the Price you’re paid to tolerate it. Your Margin will be gone. "
""
"Nobody fixes this for you. It is on you to deal with it."
""
"As you address Margin, start with the Costs. Change what it is you will tolerate. Make a boundary—one that those around you can see and feel. When you do, the Margin is immediate."
""
"If you can reduce the Cost, the Price you need to live like you becomes negotiable. And as the margin finds you, it finds those around you, too."
""
"This is the best day you have left to finally start. And waiting comes at a Cost."
""
"Need some proof? This photo feels like it was taken yesterday, but it was thirteen years ago, on the first day of Kindergarten."
""
"Last week, this kindergartener and a buddy drove my car 1800 miles to Florida."
""
"Thirteen years ago will be here before you know it."
" — Build the f*ing margin. "
""
"Let’s be up to something."
"It’s about time."
"Yours."
""

If you’re here, and this isn’t your first time, it’s probably time to quit chasing yesterday’s dream."
""
"The one that was authored by yesterday you, chased by you, achieved by you, and now lived by you. "
""
"Look, you are not the same person you were ten years ago. "
""
"You don’t act the same way. You certainly don’t look the same. You don’t love all of the same things. You’re not impressed by what used to impress you. What is important is different from what it was ten years ago. "
""
"But there you are. Taking up space in someone else’s dream, while also missing the chance to chase yours."
""
"This is not what you want to hear, but this won’t fix itself. Nobody will show up and do the work for you. Nobody will take the risk off the table for you. Nobody will put you onto the path you hope you one day find. That is up to you."
""
"So, if you haven’t also changed your life to mirror today’s you, you will feel zero control over your calendar, feel stale, sometimes bored, dependent on what you have, and frustrated that you’ve not really done anything to change your future."
""
"In short, you will be so out of touch with yourself that you forget that you have options."
""
"My friend, you have changed. And that change is fantastic—but only when you do something to live it. "
""
"This is the #normal40 highway. This is the road back to you. This is the time to take it. And if you need a co-pilot, you can find me here. "
""
"This is where we take the Beamer offroad, just to see what you might find. "
""

Quit managing your time."
"Start managing your energy."
""
"Time moves in a single direction. Time moves from the top of the hourglass to the bottom and never returns. You can’t control it, and every day it is taken."
""
"Energy is the opposite. It moves in two directions. You can give energy to someone or something, which can return to you later. "
""
"Everything changes once you know this and learn how to use it."
""
"Energy is an investment, and only you are in control of it. Over time, there is a return when invested with intention. "
""
"Your energy is entirely yours. Nobody can take it, and only you can give it away. "
""
"Energy can be squandered through tolerance by living yesterday’s dream or invested through the curiosity of creating a new one."
""
"You will decide, later today, who or what will get your energy. You can spend it doing what you did last week, or you can spend it building how it could look next week."
""
"You own your energy. You. Only you. Always, only you."
""
"The question is this: What will you do with it?"
""
"Where you place your energy matters; it is the single greatest predictor of the next year of your life."
""
"Your energy is your investment. Place it somewhere that slowly builds and then releases rewards through control, experiences, memories, adventure, and connection."
""
"Oh, and one more thing …"
""
"Wherever you choose to give your energy, make damn sure it will miss it when it’s gone."
""
"Let’s be up to something.  "
""
"Invest boldly. "
""

Everyone wants to change the culture, but nobody wants to change themselves. This one might hurt a little … But only if you bother to read it."
""
"There are thousands of definitions of culture. Most are paragraphs long. Mine is simple: What you consistently reject and demonstrate."
""
"That’s it: “People here don’t do things like that.”"
""
"You might work in a company with an excellent or lousy culture."
""
"You might be surrounded by people you trust and respect, be in the throws of politics, or be scolded today for something celebrated yesterday. "
""
"We love to talk about company culture."
""
"Why? Because it’s an easy target and allows us to point outward to the problem. It lets us off the hook."
""
"We can credit or blame company culture to fit any moment, and nobody is on the hook for it at that moment."
""
"But the culture of you?"
"You are on the hook for that every day. You can’t point outward at the problem."
""
"The culture of you isn’t about a boss, a coworker, or income."
""
"The culture of you is simple: What do you reject and demonstrate?"
""
"The culture of you needs nobody’s approval, permission, or explanation."
""
"But …"
""
"The culture of you needs a definition."
""
"Without a definition, there will be drift and tolerance."
"Without a definition, there will be no consistency."
"Without a definition, there can be no sense of control."
""
"Look, there will come a day when simply surviving the day will fail to be enough. It will finally get so bad that you move beyond thinking about change into actually doing something."
""
"There will be a day when cowering, tolerating, navigating, and reacting to matters outside your control will break you."
""
"There will be a day when you take inventory of all you are, all you have, all you have overcome, and all you are capable of being, and you will decide to REJECT how you have come to be treated, and you will find a new way to DEMONSTRATE what you are capable of doing and who you are capable of being."
""
"This is the day you will define the culture of you."
""
"At that moment, without the need for words, all of us will see what you consistently reject and what you demonstrate."
""
"The culture of me is right here — full display. I won’t apologize, change it for anyone, or modify it to fit everyone. But I believe my culture is for someone, and maybe my someone is you."
""
"I REJECT the notion that I have to do today that which paid me yesterday, and I will show up here, every day, to DEMONSTRATE that you don’t have to either."
""
"And while I’m rambling … I REJECT the belief that you have sold your freedom to be you, purely you. There is no greater feeling than knowing who you are and then mustering the courage to stand in it."
""
"Now, what will you reject, and"
"What will you demonstrate?"
""
"You are on the hook for it."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #culture #leadership #coaching"

Normal 40 Notes from Ramble #287"
""
"Rambler: “I stayed five years longer than I should have.”
"
"
"
"Lon: “What kept you around.”
"
"
"
"“I didn’t want anyone to question my loyalty.”
"
"
"
"“Tell me more.”
"
"
"
"“The culture changed as we grew, so I stayed there for my team. And my boss was good, too.”
"
"
"
"“How long were you there?”
"
"
"
"“Twenty-eight years.”
"
"
"
"“You were there for twenty-eight years?’
"
"
"
"“Yes.”
"
"
"
"“At the same company?”
"
"
"
"“Yes.”
"
"
"
"“When did you know you wanted to leave, to chase something more?”
"
"
"
"“Seven years ago.”
"
"
"
"“You’ve been working for the same company for twenty-eight years, and you have known for the last seven that you had a dream to chase, but you put your dreams on hold to avoid appearing disloyal?”
"
"
"
"“Yes.”
"
"
"
"“I admire loyalty, but it goes more than one direction. You can’t give all your loyalty to one thing—it turns into obedience. Over time, that type of loyalty becomes a thief. In this case, a thief of at least five years.”
"
"
"
"“What do you mean?”
"
"
"
"“Well, you have a dream — One begging you to take a chance on it. It keeps you up, excites you, and you know it is calling you to chase it. If all of your loyalty is reserved for the judgment of your employer, when does loyalty give your dream a shot? When does your loyalty show up for you? When does your loyalty finally show up better for your wife and grown kids? It won’t. You have to take it back and share it with what your future is begging from you.”
"
"
"
"Long pause.
"
"
"
"“I had never thought about that. My loyalty cost me five years - five years when I knew I wanted to be somewhere else.”
"
"
"
"“Loyalty is admirable, but it’s time to be loyal to you. It’s time to be selfish—for you, your family, and your dreams. It’s time to do things—for you. It’s time to be loyal to the dreams patiently waiting while you put everything else first. It’s time to bet on you. And now listen to this: You are ready.”
"
"
"
"Long pause.
"
"
"
"“Dude, what are you feeling?”
"
"
"
"Long pause.
"
"
"
"“I feel …”
"
"
"
"“I feel ... I feel like this is my time.”
"
"“I feel … light. Like a huge weight just lifted.”
"
"“I feel ... like this is possible, and this might be The Trade.
"
"
"
"“We have some work to do, boss. Next step for you, give yourself PERMISSION to feel good about taking a bet on you, and to ACCEPT that The Trade is the best loyalty of all; loyalty to yourself. Next week, let's start giving our loyalty to a dream; it’s time.”
"
"______________________________________________
"
"
"
"There will be a time in your life when the best thing you can do is become selfish.
"
"
"
"There will be a point when you quit chasing everything with a price and become more loyal to the things that are truly priceless.
"
"
"
"When that time comes, stare it in the face, and take it on. What’s next won’t be easy obvious or free.  It will be The Trade.
"
"
"
"When it finds you, be selfish. Be really damn selfish."
"When it finds you, find me. "
"This is what I do.
"
"
"
"It’s your ball, boss.
"
"Take the shot.
"
"
"

Day two — Florida. “But then I met Chris.”"
""
"Two days ago, I purchased a home in Florida. This move wasn’t for a job or to escape anything. This move is to build on an already full life—to write this chapter before I’m too old to enjoy it. This move is about avoding the regret of never trying it."
""
"Two nights ago was my first night in the new house. After a grueling day of new-home painting, installing, repairing, resurfacing, replacing, and asking myself, “Why the heck would they have done that?” when thinking about the sellers, I retreated, alone, to a local watering hole on the beach."
""
"That’s where I met Chris."
""
"Confession — I never know how to answer the question, “So what do you do?” when I meet people."
""
"I still fumble around that answer. Think about it—putting what I do into a tidy corporate box is hard. My wife loves it when people ask, mostly because she’s curious about how I’ll answer it…this time."
""
"For some reason, answering that question with, “I’m a best-selling author,” always seemed fake—like it was an act—as if I didn’t deserve it."
""
"After all, I’m no Simon Sinek, Arthur Brooks, Seth Godin 🌔 or Rich Roll. So, part of me felt like a “best-selling author” had already been taken—by people who weren’t me."
""
"Then I met Chris (back to the story)."
""
"When I walked into that beach-side tiki bar, I knew I would be asked, “So what do you do.” And this time, I resolved to have an answer that was true and accurate. An answer that inspires a conversation that goes deeper than the weather and a car show."
""
"Chris was working the bar at this appropriately humble place called Whisky Joe's. On queue, he took my order and we began to ramble. Before long, the question was on the table, “So what do you do?” This time, I had the answer."
""
"“I’m a best-selling author of a self-published book.” "
""
"This line, this answer, is 100% true. It’s completely accurate, and it was built by … me. But …"
""
"I was still a little sheepish when I said it to Chris. Then …"
""
"Two days later (this morning), an email from Chris found me—the bartender I knew for less than 45 minutes. It is, without a doubt, an omen."
""
"While Chris doesn’t know it, he just helped me own something I’ve been wrestling with for over a year. He helped me realize that you don’t need to be the most famous name in your field to belong there. He reminded me that you don’t have to be known by everyone to have an impact on someone. And you don’t need the most significant following or be able to spend millions to have an impact."
""
"Nope."
""
"Instead, you simply need to give a damn about someone or something worth a damn, and then you have to lean into it farther than what feels natural. And when you do, you find the people you are here to help. Even better, they find you."
""
"Chris—I’ll be seeing you again. I need to sign “The Trade” for you. After all, I am a best-selling author, and you are one of my people."
""
"Thanks for being up to something."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #coaching #jfds #bestsellingauthor

"My name is Lon Stroschein, A former public company executive who walked away from the title, the office, and the image—because it was time to bet on me."
""
"I’ve dedicated the last two years to researching what we go through while at the top of our game, and what we can do about it before it’s too late."
""
"Every day, I get notes of thanks from people I’ve never met. People who have read The Trade, joined The Insider community, or booked a free ramble with me. But also people who just show up here and then take an action."
""

"— Notes just like the one I received this morning. "
""
"Follow me for more. "
"Join me for change.

Wow. This is one of those days nobody can prepare you for."
""
"In one of the coolest traditions I know, the day before high school graduation, the seniors return to their elementary school one more time for their “walk out” into the world—the ultimate gesture of taking their past into their future. "
""
"The elementary kids and the teachers line the halls to clap and cheer these seniors into their life’s next chapter."
""
"This year, my son was one of those seniors."
""
"The same kid I dropped off 13 years earlier, in the same building and halls. Only this time, I wasn’t leaving him with his crooked backpack, untied shoes, and syrup-smeared eyeglasses, where I would pick him up later. This time, I was there to watch him close out an incredible chapter of his life and “walk out” into the world a fearless young man."
""
"I am, as you know, a bit of a sap. And there is no greater force over my emotions than moments of profound nostalgia."
""
"Nostalgia, to me, is the three-way intersection of living something for the last time, the vivid memory of experiencing it for the first time, while simultaneously wondering what the hell happened in the meantime."
""
"Nostalgia is that thin gray space in life where one chapter obviously ends, and the next hasn’t yet started. It’s one of life’s most pure moments, and it is beautiful."
""
"Just before he “walked out,” I grabbed this photo of my son hugging my wife, his mom, in the halls of the school he used to roam. They are surrounded by her class (she is a kindergarten teacher) and the staff and students are cheering both of them on—all sharing this part of this walk."
""
"The nostalgia of it all got me. My wife. Our oldest son. These halls. The graduation robe. The cheering—and the chapter for all of us so obviously coming to an end; it was nostalgia at its best."
""
"And I was given a front-row seat. I can not imagine a greater blessing. This thin gray space was short and sweet and will be with me for a lifetime. "
""
"So, in this mashup of weekends, let me say this:"
""
"Happy Mother’s Day to moms everywhere, but especially to Mindi Stroschein. "
""
"And also, happy graduation week to parents in the thick of nostalgia. The only emotion you can’t swallow away—nor should you. "
""
"May your graduation week be as heartfelt, tearful, beautiful, and memorable as mine."
""
"I can’t wait to see what the next chapter brings."
""
"Let’s be up to something … nostalgic."
""
"You’ll be glad you did."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #nostalgia #graduation #inspiration #mothersday #dads

There are two kinds of fear: The kind you wear on your face and the type that hangs on your heart."
""
"The first fear is outward—a phobia. A fear of snakes, spiders, flying, elevators, and being in closed spaces or around strangers. These fears are well-known by you and by others."
""
"The second fear is inward—an anxiety. This fear is lonely and often kept secret. The fear of losing a job, of not measuring up, of saving too little, of a failing marriage, of falling ill and aging, of being absent from home, of settling for work that isn’t your best, of getting older and never mustering the courage to try."
""
"These internal fears are often blurry and hard to label, swirling together relentlessly—and are lonely. Rather than knowing these fears deeply, we let them sit in our gut as a single ball of anxiety that grows and shrinks together as one. "
""
"But …"
""
"What if you got intimate with this fear—this inner anxiety? What if you could understand it so deeply you could name it?"
""
"Fear is a warning sign for danger, real or perceived. It often shows up as a ball of string in the pit of your gut, impacting your entire world as you try to hide from it or work around it."
""
"What if you pulled at the thread, with intimacy and curiosity? What if you could pull it into its parts and you could come to understand why it’s there? What if you pulled it out of the pit of your stomach, held each strand of fear individually in the palm of your mind, and named it? What would it feel like to relieve that pressure?"
""
"As a single strand, you can work with it. You understand it. You can APPRECIATE where it came from and what it is trying to do FOR you. You can see where it has use, and where it does not. Then you can DECIDE whether you still need to carry it with you or leave it behind."
""
"Maybe that fear is no longer needed. "
"Maybe its work here is done."
""
"You can’t outrun your fear. You can cover it up and tamp it down, but you can’t wish it away for a while. But this tactic adds to the ball of string."
""
"The best thing you can do is to get to know your fears individually, give your fears a name, APPRECIATE what your fears are trying to do FOR you, and then decide if you still need them for where you want to go next."
""
"Fear and anxiety are normal—we all have them. But when you get to know them individually, you also get to reset your boundaries. You get to test new limits and try new adventures, and you will feel like you are re-born into a new opportunity."
""
"New fears will find you. That’s normal. But these will be the appropriate fears for your new adventure of the new you. "
""
"One day, you will outgrow those fears, too."
""
"Get intimate with your fear and anxiety."
""
"Drop the fears you no longer need."
""
"Let’s be up to something…"
"Let’s change your horizon."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #JFDS #fear #imposter #thisday

The most often asked question among friends: “How are you?”"
"The most often given answer: “Living the dream.”"
""
"This is followed by some surface-level laughs as if the line was somehow original, and then it’s business as usual."
""
"Whew, genuine friendship and intimacy are avoided (again)."
""
"In my research, I get to ask that question to people I’ve never met. The people who ramble often wait a month to talk, and almost none of them miss the call. "
""
"Ironically, when I ask what’s led them here—to a call with some dude they’ve never met, none of them conclude they are living the dream. Most of them feel eluded by it. "
""
"In the rush to climb, we often leave something essential behind: our ability to dream. Our dreams are reduced to the next logical step in a career. And, eventually, even that is handed to us."
""
"Yet, as we ascend in our careers—to more prominent titles and more significant income, the pressure to conform, to hit targets, to fit what is needed erodes our dreams and replaces them with obedience. “My dreams will find me, so long as I follow the rules.”"
""
"In my research on Normal 40, I’ve asked hundreds of people like you, “What’s the dream?” "
""
"In less than 5% of the cases, people have clarity on their dreams. Most often, the answer is, “I have no idea, but this isn’t it.” "
""
"What I have learned in my research is a little deeper. Dare I say more disheartening. "
""
"For most who are here, reading a long post from some dude they wish wasn’t inside their head (hello!), they have forgotten how to dream. But even worse, they’ve lost the art in their life."
""
"Don’t skip this point — Not only do most of us forget how to dream, we forget that it’s even an option. We forget what our art even looks like or how to use it. We have become entirely mechanical, and art is a risk."
""
"Let me ask you: Where is the art in your life?"
""
"What do you do that doesn’t have to be done a certain way, played in a specific order, completed on a particular day, composed using a template, created with exact colors, made with certain ingredients, moves, or sequences?"
""
"When was the last time you did something you can’t be wrong—simply because it’s yours?"
""
"And when was the last time you shared it?"
""
"My friend, that is your art. YOUR art, and I miss it."
""
"So do you."
""
"This weekend, practice your art. Remember what it’s like to hit the keys, pluck the strings, stroke the brush, mold the clay, bend the steel, create the design, draft a concept, dance in the mirror…"
""
"This weekend, remember your art."
""
"When we stop dreaming, we stop innovating. We don't just lose the art; we lose our identity. And we lose our courage to bet on us."
""
"Today, practice your art—then share it."
""
"From there, let it unfold all around you. "
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"Show me your art."
""
"Live the dream."
"Yours."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #leadership #coaching #art #jfds

I write a letter to subscribers called The Rambler. It comes out when I have something to say. Today was one of those days. Here's a little of what I shared, but you should subscribe. "
"--- "
"There's a lot of chatter about Sunday Scaries. While I can appreciate that it is bringing a larger problem to light, I have come to detest the term."
""
"Why? It’s a misuse of words that tells the wrong story."
""
"At best, it’s a cute term that brings a real problem to the surface. But at worst, it gives you something to hide behind."
""
"So, what’s my problem with it?"
""
"First, it’s not only applicable on Sunday. In fact, it’s worse after an extended leave, like vacation or illness."
""
"Second, what you feel is not fear; it's visceral frustration."
""
"So, other than being cute, the term is useless, and it is distracting from the greater problem."
" "
"Let’s call it what it is: Re-entry Syndrome."
" "
"Re-entry Syndrome is a condition characterized by a cluster of emotional, cognitive, and physical symptoms that individuals experience when transitioning from a period of leisure, vacation, or extended time away from work back into their regular work routines."
""
"The condition arises from the contrast between the freedom, control, and relaxation experienced during personal time off and the structure and demands of the workplace. The condition has an element of "re-entry creep" that sets and escalates several hours, or even days, before the actual return."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40 Highway."
""
"If you're here, it's not an accident or an algorithm."
"It's you. "
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #reentry #JFDS #leadership #ramble

Three years ago this week, my company initiated a process that would lead to a $2.1B transaction. I was the M&A lead on the deal. It was supposed to be the crowning event of a career. And for a while, it was."
""
"Two years ago, I left: My work there was done. My passion for the job was gone. And pay was the addiction to stay. I had given all I had to give, and I had taken all there was to take. I knew my life’s best days were upon me, and that to live them, I would have to make a change—a trade."
""
"I resigned in February 2022, I was 46, and this was my Normal 40 Moment."
""
"I left work on a Friday, had a typical weekend, and then posted about my decision to resign on Monday."
""
"By that Friday, my life’s next chapter was starting to unfold."
""
"I discovered that we all get to a place where we want to break the rules and explore something new. We have what we chased and earn more than we once thought possible. But something is different—we feel it in our gut."
""
"Our kids and our parents are getting older. Our friendships are growing more shallow, our passion for work is gone, our marriage is at a low point, our age is suddenly a factor, and nobody knows how you truly feel under that thin smile and worn joke. After all, to anyone else, it looks like you have it all."
""
"That triggers guilt and shame—for having so much and feeling so little."
""
"One year ago, I wrote the last line of my book, The Trade."
""
"It took months of hard work, deep exploration, and courage to share what I had to share. There are stories in this book that I can’t take back—but stories I knew, if heard, might change the lives of those who need it."
""
"I self-published The Trade with my friend Jeremy D Brown. I aspired to sell a hundred thousand copies to change the world instantly. "
""
"That’s not how it worked."
""
"Instead, with no ad spend, no retailers, and only my little reach, The Trade sold thousands of copies, leading to another few thousand … and another."
""
"The little book I wrote for people like us had become a fixture in thousands of homes—and lives."
""
"It’s been a gift from a wife to their spouse, from financial advisors to their clients, and from friend to friend. The Trade has slowly, steadily, and consistently done the hard work of inspiring change."
""
"A $2.1BN transaction wasn’t my life’s best work; it was The Trade."
""
"A lifetime’s best work is never about the money—it is about the passion for the journey and the curiosity about where it might take you. For me, it’s about creating a community wired for change and inspiring YOU to inspire others."
""
"Today, I am plugging my book. It’s too good not to. It’s available on Amazon, and it has the power to change lives."
""
"The $3/book I make is laughable, but the notes of thanks I get from those who read it … will be with me for the rest of my life. These notes and photos are my new income, and they are priceless."
""
"Thank you for being up to something with me."
""
"On your life’s last day — "
"Be glad you did it."

"I used to admire the toughness of relentless conviction. "
"Now, I admire the bravery of relentless curiosity."
""
"Clinging to an opinion is easy. It requires no work. It allows you to use the same lines of mental code to defend your thoughts, day after day. If you hold a conviction long enough, it becomes a habit, not an idea."
""
"If your default is to defend a conviction by getting pissed off at anyone who questions it, you have a habit, not an opinion."
""
"Rethinking your convictions requires courage—and it needs work. Hard work. The work of renegotiating a belief that no longer serves you—a belief that once helped to build you."
""
"Nothing is more immediate, powerful, and actionable than renegotiating a conviction that no longer serves you."
""
"In a single hour, on a walk in your neighborhood, you can alter your life's trajectory slightly. "
""
"Think of a conviction you locked into years ago and have defended ever since, then ask:"
""
"1.) Is the evidence of this belief still on my side, really? "
"2.) Is the conviction still pure, or is it getting hard to defend?"
"3.) Is this conviction still worthy of my name and this extreme?"
"4.) Is there anything about this belief I no longer need?"
"5.) How might things change if I let this go today?"
""
"Conviction for a cause is great, but conviction for a habit that worked yesterday is dangerous. "
""
"It turns people into drones who say again today that which they said yesterday. "
""
"It turns people into politicians. "
"And I think you’re better than that."
""
"Let’s be up to something … curious. "

""
"Be more unreasonable."
""
"Over the last 20 years, you’ve been molded to fit perfectly into a tidy little box we know as you. Your box consists of the walls in your office, the expectation of your title, the inbox you are a slave to, and the Outlook calendar that owns you."
""
"This is your box, and you will obey it."
""
"But it wasn’t always like this. You used to be able to push a wall or bend a norm. You used to be able to create, to test, and to do it a new way. You used to be celebrated for being unreasonable enough to try."
""
"Now, unreasonableness is a one-way street, and your employer owns it."
""
"On your drive home from work, you will rehearse all the moments in your day when your reasonableness won out, again. All the times when you wish you had said this, or you wish you had done that. You will recall all the times you were tamed and returned to your box."
""
"Once again, your reasonableness is yielding to the unreasonableness of your job."
""
"Look, if saying no to an idea, a meeting, a project, or a timeline feels like a risk, that’s unreasonable."
""
"If the thought of telling a colleague, a team member, or a boss that you won’t take a call on Sunday or over the dinner hour feels like a career-limiting move, you have a problem, and it’s time to be unreasonable."
""
"Being unreasonable isn’t disobedience or disrespect. Usually, it’s not even unreasonable. You’ve just let the power of the culture erode your understanding."
""
"It is NOT reasonable to work 65 hours a week and get paid for 40. It is not reasonable to have a project move up from next week to tomorrow morning. It is not reasonable to be on a conference call from a line at Disney World. It is not reasonable to feel a pit in your gut on your way to a meeting. It is not reasonable to give the best days of your life to something that neuters your joy."
""
"All of that shit is entirely unreasonable. And you shouldn’t tolerate it. Heck, fifteen years ago, you made fun of the person who did."
""
"When it feels risky to speak your mind, to give an opinion, or to choose date night over a client, you are not the one being unreasonable. You’re the one tolerating it."
""
"Getting your life back starts with knowing what you will no longer tolerate. It starts with saying “no” and then living with the results."
""
"So, today, be unreasonable enough to create a boundary. To take a day off. To shut off your email after 6:00 PM. To forward emails on vacation. To have a date with your spouse. To be you."

Drip. Drip. Drip. "
""
"The problem isn’t that it’s Monday."
"The problem is that you don’t like the score."
""
"Two years ago, I used to hate Monday. It wasn’t always like that. For most of my professional career, Monday didn’t bother me."
""
"Slowly, the Sunday Pit started to find me, and Monday became something I began to dread. At the time, I didn’t know why—but I do now."
""
"Monday was not the problem. My Mondays used to be pretty great. My boss wasn’t the problem—we were hanging out this week. The work wasn’t the problem—I was good at it."
""
"How I felt on Monday was an inside job."
""
"Monday represented the start of another week where I had given up on the dream. Where I had let another week and another weekend come and go and did nothing to deal with how I was feeling. "
""
"Monday was a reminder that my future was my own, but I had become too attached to my image, title, and W2 to do anything about it. Monday made me feel alone, defeated, and sometimes like a fraud."
""
"Drip. Drip. Drip."
""
"Monday was a reminder that I was waiting and that I had failed to act. It was a reminder that I had done nothing to bend my future in my favor. A reminder that nobody was going to do this for me, and that I was starting to question if I would ever have the courage to do it on my own."
""
"How I felt on Monday wasn’t anyone else's fault; it was my own. And it was lonely as hell. After all, most people around me would give anything to have the position I held. But that made it even worse."
""
"Monday was a reminder that I had quit pushing the boundaries, quit chasing the next rung, and I was quietly starting to hide in my previous success. I was slipping into the middle of the pack while trying to make it appear as if I was still leading it. "
""
"Drip. Drip. Drip."
""
"It turns out, Monday was never the problem. I’ve survived every Monday I’ve ever met. "
""
"The problem was that Monday kept score. Monday was the proof that I had failed to act—and how I felt on Monday was the frustration. But blaming how I felt on Monday, instead of myself, was usually the only medicine I needed to make it to another Friday."
""
"The problem isn’t that it’s Monday."
"The problem is that you don’t like the score."
""
"If you want to change that …"
"Let’s be up to something."
"That’s what we do."

“My dad and I didn’t always get along.”"
""
"It was November, 1992. My grandpa and I set out for an afternoon drive around the farm; I remember it like yesterday."
""
"We were often together during family events, but something about this setting — the cool air and warm sun, the land, and the company — moved my grandpa to tell stories he had never before told. Stories I knew he wouldn’t likely tell again."
""
"We talked about everything: his wife (grandma), my dad, playing baseball, and all the changes he has seen in his life.  "
""
"We were driving along a homesteader’s tree claim on the banks of a creek not far from home — on the land his dad purchased in 1912 and lost in 1929. On the land my grandfather repurchased back from the bank in 1942 when my dad was three."
""
"It was in that stretch of land, on that cool fall day, when Grandpa looked off to his right and said softly: “My dad and I didn’t always get along.”"
""
"For the next 45 minutes, as I drove slowly along the trees that would eventually lead us home, I clung to every word."
""
"My grandpa attended a rural one-room school on the South Dakota prairie. He began farming with his dad as soon as he could catch a horse. But his dad, struggling with the bank, and probably with providing meals, was not an easy person to be around."
""
"The summer after my grandfather’s 8th-grade year, my grandpa and his dad had a falling out over a team of horses that escaped their stable, and who would get blamed over an untied gate gate."
""
"While my grandpa didn’t leave the gate open, he set out for most of the night to find, collect, and return the team to the barn. While feeling proud of his contribution, his return home was not a happy one with his father."
""
"The unfairness of the affair was more than my grandpa wanted to tolerate. According to him, after crying most of the night, he picked himself up, kissed his mother goodbye, and set out alone down a dusty road."
""
"Then he said something I will never forget: He left home that day with the clothes on his back and the two things he loved most: his well-worn baseball glove, and his .22 rifle."
""
"My grandfather left home at 14 with a baseball glove and a small .22 rifle to have something to eat. It was the height of the depression, and he was determined to make it on his own — to bet on himself. And he did it because of the blame for an untied gate."
""
"Grandpa didn’t quit that day — he started. He started walking. Alone. And he knew he would figure it out."
""
"This school photo from 1930 found while at the farm earlier this year. In the back row, there’s a kid holding a baseball and bat. "
""
"That is my grandfather."
"He’s 14, and it’s likely his 8th-grade year."
"That’s the kid—and that’s the bat—from my grandfather's story."
""
"My grandpa knew more about The Trade than I will ever know; it’s beautiful."
""
"Thank you, Grandpa. Your decision to set out on your own is still paying dividends to me and my kids."
""
"Thanks for being up to something."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #inspiration #farmers #agriculture #jfds

The other One Percent."
""
"“I quit my job this week after 31 years in the industry….”"
"  — A bank president."
""
"Yesterday, I sent an email to my mailing list. It was my ramble about knowing when to write off the sunk costs of yesterday’s life."
""
"I didn’t expect what happened next."
""
"One percent of the readers sent me a note to tell me they resigned in the last 30 days."
""
"One percent."
""
"One percent of readers who wrote me a note of thanks to tell me they finally mustered the courage to quit might not impress you. "
""
"But one percent of people who show up here deciding to take their life back — makes me proud."
""
"Some will mock my One Percent. Some will think it’s a lousy performance. And some may wonder why such a low number could carry such high value to me. "
""
"But the answer is easy:"
""
"My mission is to inspire the change in a thousand lives."
"My metric is a thousand notes of thanks from people who have done it."
""
"My mission has never been to change the world."
"My mission has been to finally reach you so we can change yours."
""
"My metric has never been about how much I make; it’s about the proof — for my someday great-great-grandkids that this mission had an impact. And here are the notes to prove it."
""
"This week, One percent of you did it."
""
"One percent of you quit tolerating who you were, and decided to live out the best of what you have to offer, next."
""
"One percent of you did the math, had the awkward conversations, and took action on who you were here to be next."
""
"One percent of you just ‘insured’ that you would never look back on this day and wish you would have tried."
""
"One percent of you are waking up today, unlike yesterday."
""
"Turns out, One Percent a month is all I need to be on my mission."
""
"At some point, while at the top of your game, it will find you. This moment where you can feel deep appreciation for all you have, and deep concern about never having the courage to try something new. "Unreasonably, purely you."

"But the truth …"
""
"I was afraid of the consequences. It wasn’t that I couldn’t budget the financials. It was that I couldn’t budget the courage."
""
"I wasn’t afraid of the workload when I returned. I feared that nobody would miss me when I was gone."
""
"So, at most, I would take a week. In twenty years, I never took more than five consecutive working days off."

"Looking back, I would spend anything for a thirty-day experience with my kids while all were still just kids."

"You need this."

"That’s why you are here."

Dear Lurker,"
""
"Hey, thanks for being here. While I cannot know who you are, there’s a great chance I know what you feel and why you are here. After all, I talk to you every day around this time."
""
"And for me, that’s cool."
""
"You can be here—or there—as long as it takes."
""
"But here’s a little secret: You are who I’ve been talking to all along."
""
"Sure, there are a lot of others who are in a position to post, share, comment, and tag. And you’re glad they do because you need this shit. "
""
"But you’re unable to post, like, share, or comment. It’s simply too risky. I was there, so I get it. "
""
"Look, I know being here sucks. I understand that you’ve only told your spouse most of what you feel, and that your friends and colleagues all assume you’re living the dream—but it lives a bit like a rich person’s prison cell: to have so much on the outside and feel so little on the inside."
""
"I mean, who would leave a job like yours? Who would put in all of the travel, all of the weekends, all of the late nights, all of the schooling, build all the connections, create all of the influence, build all of the skill, climb to the top, and then want to do something else?"
""
"Well. You."
""
"And for the record, me."
""
"Look, you know why I’m here. My intent isn’t to get you to quit. My intent is to inspire you to do the math. Not that financial math, the self-made “F-it” net-worth spreadsheet you keep on your desktop does that. I am talking about the math of your time—the math on the good days you have left, and the decision for how to live them."
""
"Sometimes, we start this process in the wrong place."
"I did. Maybe you are, too."
""
"Try this hack: Quit thinking of yourself as the product."
""
"In your life now, you are the product. You assume your compensation is in return for you—your work, your output, your production. Today, you see yourself as the product your employer is paying for. "
""
"But where you’re going, you will not be the product."
""
"You can’t find yourself when you view yourself as “the product” someone else can buy. It cheapens you. And isn’t that why you’re tired of the grind?"
""
"You are this:"
"— You are the solution. "
"— You are the answer."
"— You are the wisdom that the person right behind you needs."
""
"You need to come to grips with who you can help, and what you can do to get started."
""
"I believe that you can't confidently take someone where you haven't been yourself."
""
"But I also believe YOU can be a guide for anyone who has never traveled the road you know."
""
"You might not think today is a good day to start."
"I simply think you’re full of shit."
""

The incredible illusion of “not yet.”"
""
"In two words, often uttered only in our minds, we convince ourselves that tomorrow will be better—that tomorrow it will be more obvious, less risky and perfectly responsible. Tomorrow you will be ready."
""
"And in the same trick of the mind, by deciding not you, you give ourself the illusion of having taken an action, while having done nothing but wait."
""
"Eventually, without notice, your “not yet” erodes into “I never did” and eventually into, “I wish I would have tried.” "
""
"And regret for never trying is not an illusion. "
""
"Not yet …"
""
" — After this quarter"
" — When this project is done"
" — When I have enough saved"
" — When my boss retires"
" — When my kids get a little older"
" — When something better comes along"
" — When the next set of options vest"
" — When the company gives me a package"
" — When my team no longer needs me"
" — When I don’t feel like I’m abandoning them"
" — When the job market picks up"
" — When something else finds me"
""
"I will do it when ____________, but not yet."
""
"There is a vast and deep chasm between not yet, and I’m glad I did."
""
"There are months of toil, bouts of fear, hours of doubt, fits of guilt, concern over optics … and a lifetime of reward in crossing that chasm."
""
"But crossing it is not easy; it’s not obvious, and it won’t be free."
"Crossing it will be The Trade. "
""
"But as you stare at the other side, as you’re inspired by it, as you’re made curious for what it might hold for you, and even as you long to make the leap to it, you will likely spend years hiding behind two words that will put all of it on hold—not yet."
""
"Nothing can start until you remove the phrase “not yet” from your mindset."
""
"Not yet isn’t really waiting—because it won’t be easier tomorrow. Not yet is worse."
"Not yet is stalling, and it’s hiding under the guise of an informed decision."
""
"How long have you been saying to yourself, “not yet”? "
"How long have you been wishing you had started yesterday?"
"How long are you going to wait before you start?"
""
"What if you change your “not yet” to “what if”?"
""
"Every time you have a dream about something more, something else, something greater … what if you responded to that urge with “what if…” "
""
"What if I made the call?"
"What if I joined the group?"
"What if I told a friend?"
"What if I asked for something more?"
"What if I got the training?"
"What if I took on the client?"
""
"What if I never do? "
"Not yet. "
""
"You can read this post and decide … not yet. Or …"
""
"You can read this post and ask yourself, “What if …” "
""
"What if you start? "
"What if you make the call?"
"What if, by this time next month, your life looks different?"
""
"Or what if you wait? Not yet."
""
"What you do from here is on you. In the next 30 seconds, you will once again answer the question:"
""
"1.) Not yet, or"
"2.) What if I did it?"
""
"Choose wisely. "
"Future you is counting on it. "
""
"And time is not your friend."
""
"It’s your ball, boss."
"Pass or shoot."
""

""
"What do you want to do with it?"
""
"If you’ve rambled with me, you’ve heard the question. I use it to pull people out of hiding—especially those who confuse their searching for what I suspect is actually their hiding."
""
"Yes, it is annoying. But I’m good with that; it forces you to stand face to face with what you are trying to avoid—the full story of you. And it forces you to give it a name."
""
"It is, without question, a bitch of a question."
""
"So — What do you want to do with it?"
""
"The shit that happened to you a few years ago; the pit in your gut on Sunday afternoon; the story you are ashamed to share; the conversation you waited until it was too late to have; the shots you never took; the thing you endured to preserve your image; the decision you got wrong; the guilt you feel about having so much and feeling so little; the troubles in your marriage you’re ashamed to talk about—that shit…."
""
"What do you want to do with it?"
""
"Nothing?"
"Something?"
""
"Today?"
"Later?"
""
"What do you want to do with it?"
""
"I’ve dedicated two years to cutting into this with people. After 585 calls and thousands of coaching hours, I’ve learned how to do more in an hour than what most people believe possible in months."
""
"This is my craft, and I know what I want to do with it."
""
"Your life has two resumes: The one you talk about here—your First Resume. And the one that did more to build you than anything, but you don’t share with anyone—your Second Resume."
""
"The Second Resume of your life—the shit that made you who you really are, not what you can have—the story you would prefer to leave buried under your stirling image…"
""
"That story."
"That shit story you want to run from."
"That one."
""
"What do you want to do with it?"
""
"I want you to play the shit story in your head today. All of it—write it all down. A full-on pity party for the shit you keep to yourself."
""
"Capture the shit that held you back, taken your game, and left you feeling alone and empty. The problems at work, the issues at home, the lack of control you lament, the guilt of having so much and feeling so little, and the shit you don’t want anyone to know about the real you."
""
"This, all of it, this shit is your Second Resume. But it is also your paid-in-full tuition. It’s real, and you own it."
""
"With this tuition, you have a curriculum."
"A curriculum that someone needs."
""
"All that shit is no longer a liability—it’s an asset."
"It’s yours, and someone else needs it."

"All you have to do is be found."

"So …"

I leave for Peru’s Sacred Valley tomorrow. But this morning, as I think about the trip, there are a few habits that led me here, and I want to share one of them with you."
""
"But first, this direct reminder: The discontent you feel today, at this moment, in your life, in your feeling stuck and without control — yes, that feeling. Yes, I’m talking to you. Looking around here won’t help. Scrolling for magic doesn’t happen. You are, right now, wasting your time wishing. The only way to feel different tomorrow is to act differently today. You don’t need the answers, but you do have to Just F*ing Do Something (#JFDS)."
""
"Okay, if you’re still reading, albeit with a slight annoyance for my candor, then maybe you are ready for this little drill that changed my life over a few months in 2021. "
""
"1.) Go for a walk—alone. You need two hours. (Yes, you can)"
""
"2.) Capture everything you are feeling and thinking."
" - I use the Notes app…I have hundreds of pages of “me stuff”"
" - Don’t edit, spellcheck, or try to understand it; free flow"
" - If you don’t have 20 lines an hour, you’re not trying"
" - If you need inspiration, listen to an early N40 podcast"
""
"3.) When you get home, walk away from it for several hours."
"  - If you do it right, this will be harder than it sounds"
""
"4.) Return to what you wrote, re-read it, and add to it."
" - Build out what this line means when you re-read it"
" - Write about where it came from and why you wrote it"
" - Be open to what you are writing and where it is going"
" "
"5.) Ask yourself the Normal 40 questions:"
" - What are the two common elements causing this feeling"
" - Am I ready to do something about them"
""
"If the answer is no, you still had a nice walk."
""
"If the answer is yes, join The Insider. I guarantee it will be the best $25 you spend on yourself all month. This is doing something. This is acting differently."
""
"This is where change starts — with people like us."
"The next call is Thursday."
"I will be on the call."
"From Peru."

""
"This post from Molly Dixon just described the journey of the countless majority."
""
"Often, while we are at the top of our game, when our life from the outside looks like a dream come true, we find ourselves in a box we are unable to escape."
""
"A box built from our own success. A box that leaves you looking successful, but feeling stuck. A box that others dream of living inside, and one you can’t figure out how to escape. A box nobody else can see, and one you don’t want anyone to know you feel."
""
"One day, the person you have become will stand face-to-face with the person you are capable of being. When that day comes, we have a choice about The Box: Stay in and live another yesterday. Or break down a wall and chase a new tomorrow."
""
"Molly, and thousands of others, have broken down that wall."
""
"The Mission of Normal 40 isn’t to show you what I am capable of; it to show you what you are capable of. "

"And people like Molly simply show up and help convince you."

"Lon -

Two years ago this week, I left my job as a public company executive. That week, two years ago, I had a calling—an urge I could feel in my gut—telling me to hike Machu Picchu, alone."
""
"I didn’t do it. The optics were terrible—after all, I just quit. And a solo trip like this looked irresponsible and selfish. So, I ignored the urge."
""
"A few weeks ago, the call came back."
""
"Out of nowhere, the urge to go to Peru’s Sacred Valley, alone, found me. But this time, instead of wondering, I listened, and then I acted. This time, I’m going."
""
"In 72 hours, I leave for Peru’s Sacred Valley and Machu Picchu."
""
"I am not going as part of a tour. I will not attend a conference or meet up with a friend; I am going alone."
""
"I have an airline ticket in, and an airline ticket home. And, thanks to LinkedIn, I may have a connection living in The Sacred Valley who has offered to “Help me experience what nobody else ever gets to experience.” So, I’m letting that unfold."
""
"The truth is, two years ago, I didn’t know what was calling me."
""
"Today, I do — My Third Harvest."
""
"My life to this point in three harvests: "
""
" - The First Harvest is our first 22 years: a quest for fun. "
""
" - The Second Harvest, our next 22 years: a quest for wealth and status."
""
" - The Third Harvest, our next 24 years: a call to nurture the soil. To use all we have become to live out who we are capable of being for others."
""
"For me, this trip is a simple quest to fill in the gaps of my Third Harvest. It’s a quest to prepare the soil from which others will live."
""
"I leave on Monday."
""
"I don’t yet know where I will be staying, nobody will be waiting at the airport, and I don’t speak the language. Nor do I have a meeting to catch, a company to tour to attend, a presentation to give, a brief to prepare, or a sales call to make."
""
"I simply have to show up with my senses, lead with curiosity, listen for what has summoned me, and use it in my Third Harvest."
""
"As I prepare for the journey, I am reminded that there is something extraordinary about wonder that is pure. I have missed it, and so do you."
""
"The time away is selfish. But I’ve discovered that this selfishness, if avoided completely, eventually erodes the selfless. When done right, the selfish act becomes the fuel, the cause, and the motivation to carry out the selfless acts that will define you."
""
"What I will get out of Peru remains a mystery. But what it will get out of me will be fascinating."
""
"As I travel, I will check in here from time to time."
""
"Why?"
""
"To remind you that following what you feel matters. Even when you don’t know the language."
""
"If this interests you, follow me over the next two weeks, even only to see where it takes us."
""
"Welcome to your Third Harvest. "
"Remember, it starts with the soil. "
""
"In fact, it always has."
""
"See you in Peru."
""
"Let’s be up to something."

Two years ago today, Normal 40 was born."
"Two years ago yesterday, I wrote this note to my son. "
""
"I wrote it from my office desk—the one that would be occupied by someone else the following day."
""
"I stand by this advice. This time, smiling."
""
"__________________________"
""
"Dear son,"
""
"For everything in your life there is a first, and there is a last."
""
"Your life will be full of first days. These bring you experiences to be excited about, something to chase, and something that lies ahead."
""
"But in your life, because math being what it is, there will also be an equal number of last days."
""
"Your first and last day of work, your first and last day of school, of a relationship, of days with friends, of time in our house, and your time with your mom and me."
""
"A first and a last. Every start eventually ends."
""
"That is how life works, and it doesn’t discriminate."
""
"Son, in your life, live for the last days, not the first."
""
"For in your life, kiddo, it’s the last day that holds the score."
""
"A first day is about first impressions and fulfilling the aspirations of a person you may someday be."
""
"But your last day reveals the results; did you or did you not?"
""
"Your last day holds the score."
""
"Son, play for your last day."
""
"You see, today is my last day."
""
"And I choose to celebrate this day."
""
"I choose to walk the halls slowly, to give my thanks, and to be romantically nostalgic about how every day before this day has shaped me, while also knowing all that I did to help shape it."
""
"I choose to consider what these halls took from me, but also what I took from them."
""
"For 14 years, I’ve been building for this day."
""
"For 14 years, this day rested on a distant horizon."
""
"But on this day, my last day, we will share a sunset."
""
"Son, on this day, I choose to give heartfelt thanks for the friendships and the lessons I learned on a journey … time spent with some of the world’s best."
""
"On this day, I give thanks to those who took a risk on me and celebrate those who stay to carry the torch."
""
"In your lifetime, son, there will be as many last days as there are first days."
""
"On your last day, leave the company/friendship/relationship stronger, smarter, and better than you found it. "
""
"When you do, it will do the same for you. And when you do, you can take it all with you, wherever your path leads you."
""
"And when you take it, you will have it."
""
"Today is my last day."
""
"I find myself nostalgic that my work here is done, but it is. I’ve completed what I came here to complete, and my future is no longer here."
""
"This is the finish for which I am prepared. And it’s a finish for which I am proud."
""
"Tomorrow, I will wake up to a first day."
""
"A day that comes before all of the rest. And I am ready. "
""
"This is my trade: Who I was for what I may become."
""
"I am in control, and choose to bet on me."
""
"And son, whenever you can, so should you."
""
"See you on the other side of today, kiddo."
""
"Cheers to the distant sunset."
""
"I’ll share a ride with you any day."
""
"Dad -"
""
"#normal40 #inspiration #careerchoach #motivation #JFDS #thetrade

The Paradox of a Life Lived Later. Sometimes, it never comes."
""
"Tomorrow, we tell ourselves, is the ideal day for change. It's a seductive lie we buy into every year around this time."
""
"Every day, I talk to people—the relentless providers, who believe that change right now is not right; it feels irresponsible. But just under the surface of that nonsense rests the nagging wonder of where you might be had you mustered the courage to start yesterday."
""
"This is the Normal 40 paradox: Believing tomorrow will be easier, while simultaneously wondering where you might be had you started when you could have."
""
"In the meantime, here you sit. Hoping for tomorrow, and wondering about yesterday."
""
"Eventually, every tomorrow turns into today, and change feels just as hard as yesterday. Change is hard—because we don’t want to get it wrong. So we freeze under the weight of a single question about change that haunts us: “Will I be successful?”"
""
"Here’s the truth: There is no guarantee you will be."
"Here’s the irony: You’ll regret never finding out."
""
"Next week will be two years since I did the most irresponsible thing imaginable; I left an incredible job without a plan. I left after getting what I chased, and I left for one reason: My future wasn’t there, and I knew it. So the only wrong thing to do would be to wait, to toil. To wish. To wonder. Instead, I did what was hard—I made The Trade. And I’ve never looked back."
""
"I’ve dedicated the last twenty months of my life to learning about what we all go through in this stage of life. I have rambled with 561 incredible men and women who feel like I used to. I’ve written books, hosted podcasts, and led masterminds, all to build a tribe, to pull people from the shadows, to let them know they are not alone, and to invite them into a community I call The Insider."
""
"Doing this was never about money and how to make it. It is about time—and how I get to spend it."
""
"Making a change while at the top of your game is not easy; it’s incredibly lonely, and there are no guarantees. Change does not wait for you to feel ready. Change is not patient, and it’s judgy as hell. It nags. It steals. It bites, and it compares."
""
"But change cares. It cares that you show up and deal with how you feel."
""
"Change might be your one true fan. Change might be the only thing truly pure in your life. The call for change might be the one thing you should trust."
""
"The change you feel in your gut is not doing the retirement math of money; it’s doing the legacy math of the time you have left."
""
"It's not hiding in the folds of tomorrow. It's here, in the relentless march of today."
""
"Nobody else will walk this path for you, but I will do it with you."
""
"It’s your move, led by your moment. If you missed starting yesterday, today is your next best shot, and this is the youngest you will ever again be to finally start."
""
"When you’re ready, join me."
"Then, let’s be up to something."
""
"It’s your ball, boss."
"Pass or shoot."
""
"#normal40 #careercoach #lifestyle #JFDS

Farm kid wisdom 101: When you’re stuck, quit spinning the tires. Allow yourself to feel stupid, and then make the call for help. In 100% of the cases, in the history of farming, someone shows up to help—no matter how long you let the tires spin."
""
"Look, I know why you’re here; you’re stuck. And if you are, you’ll keep reading. If you’re not, you’ll find some cool quotes a little further down the feed. "
""
"I know you’re feelings: Restless, Frustrated, Fearful. Alone, Rutterless. Annoyed. Caged. Yearning for more. Disconnected. Older than you used to be."
""
"What you want: Mission, Purpose, Control. Confidence, Peace, Connection, and to do something pure. "
""
"What you need to do next: Accept what you feel. Reflect on what you have. Explore what you want. Create the space. Get selfish.  Connect, Quit stalling. Do something. "
""
"Bad news — you live an outside life, but this will be an inside job. The fact you’re here and hope nobody knows is proof of that. "
""
"Go do the oddest thing in the world: Stare at yourself in the mirror. See the lines that have formed. See the person everyone else sees, then reconsider what it feels like from the inside."
""
"Ask that person, the person who looks just like you, only older than you remember, “Are you ready to start exploring how we will live the best chapter of my life?”"
""
"You’re going to think this is a stupid exercise, and there’s a reason for that: it is. But if you do it, and I know you will, when nobody is looking obviously, you will find something, or something will find you, that you didn’t expect. "
""
"Look, you don’t have forever. And what’s next starts at the moment you decide it will. This shit won’t be easy, and it certainly won’t be obvious or free. It will be a trade."
""
"The Trade starts with a mirror, and it presents itself through action. It will be the hardest, deepest, and most rewarding work of your lifetime. "
""
"I take groups of six through this process in Vintage One. It is the most powerful investment of a lifetime. Ask those who have done it. Join the waitlist at Normal40."
""
"Need something today? Book a time to ramble and join The Insider Maswermind—we meet twice monthly. "
""
"You alone can do it, but you don’t have to do it alone. "
""
"This journey you’re on—it’s a calling. "
""
"And I suggest you answer."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
"It changes everything."
""
"#normal40 #careercoach #inspiration #JFDS

To chase or to be chased. One is happening to you now. One of them brings you here. "
""
"Give some thought to the times in your life when you felt incredible, when you felt alive, in control, full of energy, and excited—truly excited for the day, week, or month."
""
"Give some thought to when you were brimming with confidence, flowing with hope, following your curiosity, and excited about what might happen—even if you got it wrong."
""
"Give some thought to when you cared more about where the journey might take you and who you might meet than what it might buy."
""
"Give some thought to when you knew you might not get it right, but you woke up every morning excited to see where the day might take you—and that it made you feel alive."
""
"Give some thought to when you put it on the line to chase something more significant—something almost ridiculous, somewhat wreckless, and to others looked irresponsible. The time you took the shot when it was yours to take."
""
"Give some thought to who you were with, what you were doing, who you were helping, and how it eventually changed your life."
""
"Give some thought to how it makes you feel now, reflecting upon it."
""
"Every day, you wake up with two options: To chase what you want or to be chased by what you don’t."
""
"When you quit chasing, you start preserving."
"You move from a life on offense to a life on defense."
""
"Are you chasing your next chapter or protecting your last?"
""
"If your reason to stay is that you don’t have a good enough reason to leave, who is to blame? "
""
"There’s nothing more powerful and inspiring than someone who has reconciled the end of one chapter, and set out with passion to write the next."
""
"Doing so will be some of the hardest work of a lifetime. "
""
"But closing the chapter is on you. Defining it is on you. Starting it is on you. Doing something about it is on you."
""
"Chasing what’s next is on you. "
""
"Today, I am chasing."
""
"Let’s be up to something."

Dear the LEGO Group,"
""
"It’s me, the dad of two boys who may have single-handedly funded LegoLand. I like you, but I have to share an awkward observation about who you were, and who you have become."
""
"Of Legos: Once, you were a canvas for the imagination. A medley of shapes and colors; indestructible, brimming with potential to become whatever you could dream."
""
"In my childhood, I treasured a powder-blue pleather suitcase, a trove of countless Lego pieces. Then, buying Legos was simple: a set, a few hundred pieces perhaps, and a nudge of inspiration and a dose of imagination, and I was set. It was me and the Lego, and that was enough."
""
"Now, Legos have morphed. You teach something else. Buy a set, and we're buying a mission—to recreate the Millennium Falcon, for instance. We're handed a 100-page manual and a roadmap from start to finish. Legos arrive in pre-sorted bags, numbered in order of need. Each number associated with the page of instruction."
""
"Legos are no longer a gift of the imagination; you teach obedience, process, and following a path laid out for you. About making mine like everyone elses—because we all have the same pieces, the same shapes, the same colors and the same instruction. Worst, dear Lego, you have taught us there’s only one way to build it, and the other way is wrong. Now we can fail at Legos."
""
"Lego used to teach the incredible value of failure. About having something in your mind and buidling it with whatever you have in front of you. About how to get it wrong a hundred times before you got it right, and that the change along the way is where the magic lived. That mine will not look like yours, and that my way was better, and why."
""
"Today, Lego teaches that failure happens when you don’t follow the script—when it doesn’t look like the box. When mine isn’t exactly like theirs. You teach conformity, not creativity. You teach that doing it right can make me like everyone else. And if you get it right, you can put on your shelf and stare at it, just like everyone else. A race to the middle."
""
"I miss lessons of imagination, freedom, and independance. I miss the car with three crooked wheels, the triangle spaceship ship without a canopy, and the floatless boat that could also fly. Most of all, I miss the pleather suitcase full of jagged dreams waiting for me to tug at the zipper."
""
"In this chapter of my life, I’m going “old Lego.”"
""
"I’m finding my powder-blue suitcase, tugging at the zipper, and I’m going to build what’s next from all I have inside. No instruction, no failure, no putting it on the shelf; all me and my imagination."
""
"My first top with my suitace, Machu Picchy, Peru  and the Sacred Valley. I’m going alone, simply to see what I can build from it."
""
"I will go again later this year. If you want in, send me a note. Or, if you want to talk today, join my mastermind; we’re on a call today. No instructions—just chasing dreams. It’s called The Insider."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"#normal40 #leadership #careercoaching #JFDS

Journal Entry—Normal 40 Ramble Note #542:"
"Topic:		The Leap from Comfort to Courage"
"Rambler:	Niko"
""
"In my incredible ramble with Niko, we dove into the heart of change we all wrestle with, no matter our title. A reminder that change is hard enough, but it is compounded when it feels irresponsible to yourself to remain where you are—but appears irresponsible to the rest of the world to make the bet on what’s next."
""
"Highlights for the N.40 Journal:"
""
"- Comfort zones are well-trodden paths to nowhere. If you can see the path clearly, it’s someone else’s path. Know when it’s time to finally make your own."
""
"- Behind every successful person is a trail of uncertainties and fears they dared to confront. But, for some reason, we discount our past performance as we move up."
""
"- Seeking help isn’t a weakness; it’s often a breakthrough. Niko was relieved to have someone to talk to, and to connect with a community who felt what he felt. This alone changed his courage."
""
"- The biggest risk isn’t failing in the new, but stagnating in the old. That brings Niko and so many others here: the fear of wasting their life’s best days—trading comfort for courage, routine for revolution, the certainty of yesterday for the wonder of tomorrow."
""
"Niko is up to something. "
"And I am inspired."
""
"#normal40 #careercoaching #lifestyle #JFDS

Success isn't an accident. You are where you are for a reason. Your seat at the table is yours. It's the result of a deliberate, relentless pursuit over decades. You’ve made it. But …"
""
"Three common beliefs still hold you back. "
" - The yearning for something seemingly unattainable,"
" - The fear of losing our current self and all we have, and"
" - The notion that it was easier for others and harder for us. "
""
"These are your shackles. They are invisible yet powerful. They keep you coming back here. "
""
"Consider this: "
"What if your biggest risk is the one you never take? "
"What if you always wonder what might have been? "
"What if you get let go and you’re forced to leave?"
"What if you never let go, and you’re left to wonder?"
""
"The financial hurdles are easy to hide behind—more is always better. But more isn’t a number. Tomorrow is always easier, but tomorrow always moves. "
""
"But take another look, an honest look—you’re more ready than you think. Money isn’t usually the problem; it’s something responsible to hide behind. It’s tangible, and it is easy to explain why what you have isn’t enough … and never will be."
""
"You have enough money; you lack the path and a community to start something—to find the courage to explore something new, on your terms. Something purely you, before you have to."
""
"Normal 40 isn’t an age; it’s a feeling, and it will find you. "
""
"Normal 40 isn’t for everyone, but it is for someone. Maybe, just maybe, that someone is you."
""
"You will end this day exhausted. Where will you apply your finite energy today? What if a little of it went to your next chapter? Just 20 minutes a day to the new you. Today, you have a choice:"
" —Build more yesterday, or build a new tomorrow?"
""
"Whichever you choose is up to you. After all, success isn’t an accident. You are here for a reason. The seat at the table is yours. The results … yet to be lived."
""
"When you’re ready, join my closed private Mastermind."
"It’s embarrassingly underpriced."
"It’s called “The Insider..”"
"Be up to something."
""
"Our next call is Thursday."
""
"#normal40 #leaderhip #careercoaching #careeradvice #JFDS

Getting to where you are has a cost, but …"
"You’ve already paid the price."
""
"When was the last time you truly tested your freedom? The deep, exhilarating freedom of living life on your terms. Freedom that’s not just a concept at work, but is a life practice. Living your freedom requires practice, but you’ve let it go."
""
"When was the last time you tested your courage? Not the courage of leading a company initiative, but the courage to face yourself in the mirror and to try something new. The courage to push into the quiet instances where you speak your truth, stand up for your beliefs, and take a leap of faith despite the uncertainty. Moments of courage shape your future, but it requires practice, or it withers into tolerance."
""
"When was the last time you lived an adventure that made you feel truly alive; wide-eyed, and full of joy? Not just entertained or momentarily excited, but deeply, viscerally, emotionally alive. Adventure is the soul’s way of reminding you that life is a vast, incredible journey. It’s a reminder that what we do is not who we are. It’s in these adventures that you find yourself, your true passions, and your unbridled joy. But seeking healthy adventure requires practice."
""
"And when was the last time you did something that was purely you? Something that didn’t have a deadline, an ROI, or needed to satisfy anything other than you. In a world where conformity to a blended strategy is the norm, being yourself feels like an act of rebellion. Doing something purely you requires a search for something more significant than you alone. It’s about being unapologetically you. Over time, the purity of you can get lost, and bringing it back requires practice."
""
"In February, I will head to Peru’s Sacred Valley and Machu Picchu alone. I don’t have an agenda, I don’t know a soul in the country, and I don’t speak the language. "
""
"I am coming up on the 2nd anniversary of my departure from my former corporate self, and this is how I test my freedom, courage, and adventure.  Knowing that I’m doing it, trying it, with the belief that I will lead a Normal 40 retreat there later this year, makes it feel … pure."
""
"If a journey with me (and a small group) to Machu Picchu sounds like an adventure, you are invited. The list is already building, and something keeps calling me there."
""
"Today, practice your freedom. Test your courage. Dream about an adventure and feel something pure—then do something to practice it. "
""
"This is a life up to something."
"It costs less than you think, and …"
"It lives better than it looks."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"#normal40 #careercoaching #personaldeveloment #JFDS

Step 1 in Normal 40 Coaching: "
" — “The Deep Personal Inquiry” "
""
"If we’ve ever rambled, you’ve had a taste of it. If I’ve ever hired you, you’ve also had a taste of it. If you’re my friend or colleague or someone who makes me curious, you’ve probably had a taste of it."
""
"I call it The Deep Personal Inquiry', or DPI. And it is, without question, the most complex and essential aspect of who I am and what I do."
""
"DPI is the cornerstone of transformative change. It sets the foundation for everything that is to come next. "
""
"On my free rambles, I always take a run at it. And in 40 minutes, I usually know more about the person I am face-to-face with than 99% of people in their life. Getting people to open up isn’t easy, and it’s part art and part science. But, without fail, I’ve never had anyone refuse to answer a question. "
""
"Why? Because there is a process, I’ve spent a thousand hours crafting it in the last 18 months. "
""
"My rambles tend to be with people I’ve never met. At best, we know someone of the same people, but usually not. I don’t do anything in advance to prepare—that would create a bias. I simply hit “join meeting,” and carry out a process that establishes a bond, builds a connection, ensures we’re safe, demonstrates they are not alone and that to make progress we have to work a process. And that process starts here: Deep Personal Inquiry.”"
""
"This process isn't about skimming the surface; it's a deep dive into who you are at your core. "
""
"It's about unearthing your authentic desires, fears, and the narratives you've told yourself. The ones that are true, and the ones that are bullsh!t. You have both."
""
"Withing 30 mintes I’ll have a feel for the truths that are haunting you, the narratives that are holding you back and the patterns that keep bringing you back."
""
"Spoiler alert: It’s the same pattern that brought you back to this post."
""
"It's intense, but it's where real growth begins. If I may be so bold, it is where I have spent my time sharpening my craft, and it is where I start the process of rebulding who it is you are capable of being next. "
""
"When you’re ready to start, I’m ready to ramble. If I’m not your guy, no problem. But if you need a guy or gal, find one. "
""
"Becuase I know your pattern. And by 4:00 today that pattern is going to be sitting on your chest asking you why don’t do something about how you feel."
""
"Do something, or do nothing. "
"It’s your pattern—follow it, or break it."
""
"My name is Lon Stroschein, and I am up to something."
"I have a community for you. We meet again this Thursday."
"It’s called The Insider. It’s easy to find on my site."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
""
"#normal40 #careercoaching #personaldeveloment #JFDS

"But you’re not here because I’m doing anything special. You’re here because you are not."

"You’re not here to get anything out of me. You’re here hoping that maybe, just maybe, this is the day we finally get something out of you."

"But the fact that you’re here is a sign, if you see it. The fact that you’re here is a start, if you allow it. The fact that you’re here is an invite, if you take it."

"It’s been two years for me. In that time, I’ve learned that we all go through a process, and it finds you right where you are. "
""
"How you feel right now, as you read this, is part of that process. The fact that you seek it out, is part of your process. You're so curious about what might come next that you’re still reading … process."

"Your annoyance, appreciation, frustration, and inspiration from being here are part of that process.  It’s the process of awakening. And it takes time."
""
"Maybe this is your day, and maybe it isn’t. But if you’re reading this and thinking it will be easier tomorrow, while wishing you dared to do it yesterday, you have arrived at your time to quit making excuses. "
