Dear Outlook Calendar,"
""
"Wow, we have been through a lot together."
""
"As I write this, I'm filled with a mix of nostalgia and resolve. You've been my steadfast companion through each moment of my professional life. From the early days of juggling travel to the pivotal meetings that catapulted me into the C-suite, you were there. Your reminders and alerts were my lifeline, keeping me on task, on time, and always available to others, never letting me miss a beat in the relentless rhythm of corporate life."
""
"Every appointment, every scheduled call, every blocked-out hour for deep work that was constantly interrupted when a second and third meeting took the same hour of my life, you've held me accountable to the anxiety of it all."
""
"You were there as my days started before dawn and ended long after my kids were in bed. You joined us for dinner, interrupted dates, and on more than one awkward occasion, you joined my wife and me when we thought we were alone. Not to worry, your gentle buzz was just what I needed to get back to what was most important: my job."
""
"But dear calendar, I’ve reached a place where we should just be friends. As I reflect on our pursuit of success through relentless availability to my company, I lost sight of something equally precious: Time for me, my family, my autonomy, my joy, and my ability to do whatever the hell I want, whenever the hell I decided I want to do it."
""
"Dear Calendar, in your quest to organize my life, you commandeered it. My family's presence became just another entry among countless others. My wife, resorted to “inviting” me into our shared life. And on most days, my kids quit asking."
""
"As it turns out, the tool that helped me organize my time was working for someone else all along."
""
"You didn't just hold my schedule; you began to hold my freedom—my independence and joy were slowly handed over to the company, one new meeting reminder at a time."
""
"Soon, my days blended into one another until there was nothing but a series of double-booked hours, blocks, deadlines, and alerts, each beep a reminder of something I once had, but had since lost."
""
"This letter is a reclamation—a reclamation of my time, my choices, my dreams—on my terms. We will still hang out, but I will no longer let other people fill you with their needs. I am going to only fill you with mine. I am taking back the most precious thing in my life — my time."
""
"Yes, we will still be friends. I'll visit you for the occasional reminder in my new life and to stay organized. But you'll no longer be my constant companion and no longer the gatekeeper of my days. I'm stepping out into a world where time is not a series of slots to be filled by others, but a life to be lived by me."
""
"Here's to living the best chapter of my life."
""
"From your corner, "
""
"Lon - "
""

"This was recorded in September — weeks after he separated as a VP from a household company. Our ramble is a reminder that our prime years are the optimal time for change—but that leaving while at the top of your career will be the most courageous work of a lifetime."
""
"Here are four key insights:"
""
"1. Self-Belief Propels Transformation: Dan’s journey from a stand-up comedian to sales executive to entrepreneur was fueled by an unwavering self-belief. It’s a story of channeling your courage to start rather than waiting for it to be “safe” to start."
""
"2. Personal Evolution Needs 'Beneficial Selfishness’: Balancing familial duties and personal ambitions, Dan demonstrates that focusing on your growth and your joy isn't selfish—it's crucial. Embracing 'beneficial selfishness' is a critical action for pursuing your own Normal 40 moment."
""
"3. Vulnerability Unlocks Possibilities: Revealing personal challenges and embracing vulnerability, particularly in professional contexts, can yield unforeseen opportunities and stronger relationships."
""
"4. Image vs. Ego: I stress the importance of recognizing the difference between image and ego in your life’s call for more. Your image is manufactured by your yesterdays. Image urges you to stay—to maintain what you already have. But your ego is thinking about tomorrow—it is telling you there’s more, and it is urging you to act. It’s time to listen to your ego. Imagine life as a grand game. You only get one shot to play it; it's your move."
""
"In this game, you've had years of success, financial stability, and dedication to your work. You’ve amassed wins, climbed higher than you thought possible, and played fair. But getting here also comes with a cost — to you and your family."
""
"But the game just took a turn. For the first time, the game now has an ending you can see."
""
"This game has only one set of rules—yours. You can keep them, write them, or give that control away."
""
"This game has only one way to win—yours. You can define what winning looks like for you, or you can give that control away."
""
"In the game of your life — the rules and winning will be defined by your mind."
""
"But the spirit of the game? That’s done for you, and it happens in your gut."
""
"The spirit of the game is a compass—a mission, and a calling. And right now, in this moment, your gut can tell you if you are playing up to the spirit of the game. It knows if you are ahead or behind, winning or losing."
""
"The spirit of the game doesn’t care what it looks like on the outside. It only cares what it feels like to be living it from the inside. The spirit of the game doesn’t score assets, read titles, or give a rip about the image you have built for others. It cares only about the purity of your work, and how well you’re using the gifts you were given."
""
"At some point in this game, your rules will put you face-to-face with the rest of your life. At that moment, you will have to reckon with what the spirit of the game is—for you."
""
"Is the spirit to end with control over all of the assets, or does it end with control over all your time and passion?"
""
"The spirit of the game challenges you to avoid giving your best years to something you no longer love, and it challenges you to take who you have become and build something pure."
""
"At some point, the spirit of the game shows up and calls into question all that you thought was important, and it causes you to rethink what winning is, and what it is not. This change is often confusing, frustrating, and lonely."
""
"The Trade is easier to avoid than it is to face. But every game worth playing is hard-fought, and there is no greater game than yours. Thankfully, you’ve never been more ready to be you than you are today."
""
"I’m here for one reason: to get you off the sidelines of your life. My game is pure—to get you to play yours. "
""
"My mission is to inspire the change in a thousand lives, and my metric is a thousand notes of thanks."
""
"These are my rules, and this is how I win."
"Every day, I get a little closer."
""
"At your game’s end, it will ask each player a single question:"
"“Do you have any regrets about how you played?”"
""
"You are answering this question right now."
""
"If you’re still reading, it’s time to start. I have a community called The Insider. I’m in there every day, and we host calls most weeks—calls with people like you, who do hard things like this."

""
"You will do something to start, or you will not. And whatever you choose, you’re on the hook for. So, what needs to change?"
""
"The Normal 40 Paradox: To believe a change will be easier next year, while simultaneously wishing we would have done it last year."
""
"We can all look back and judge the year; how it treated us, and how we treated it. Last year is in the books."
""
"The past year may have been great or terrible. It may have been happy or sad. It may have been for richer or poorer, or lived in sickness or in health."
""
"But for all of us, 2023 ends today. The story of how you lived it is ending, and the story of how you will live in 2024 is starting. You are on the hook for what you do from here."
""

The Third Harvest. The one you give away. "
""
"In our twenties, we're drawn to cleverness—and we aspire to have it. We seek the allure of those who navigate life with effortless charm—those who float, seemingly without care, garnering friends and having fun. We seek the harvest of feeling free and living without limits. This is our life’s first harvest."
""
"In our thirties, we’re drawn to smarts — and we aspire to gain it. We admire those with big titles and prestigious jobs. Those with luxury cars, nice houses, and the jet-setting lifestyle. Those with the image we aspire to one day have. To us, they represent success, the tangible harvest of being smart and using it. This is our lifetime's second harvest."
""
"In our forties and fifties, what we admire evolves. It's no longer about wit and cleverness, and it ceases to be about the spoils of being smart. We give less of ourselves to garner more things, and we aspire to have more time to share with others."
""
"You have come to value those who are kind; those who seek deeper connections rather than wider networks. You crave meaningful experiences with family, over material possessions for family. You are no longer driven by what you can reap from the seeds, but by how you can improve the soil using only your chaff."
""
"This is your third harvest."
""
"It’s the harvest nobody else can offer, because this one is yours. It’s a pure harvest, and it contains the wisdom someone else needs."
""
"If you’re here, maybe you’re preparing for your third harvest. The one nobody can do for you. The one that won’t wait forever. The only harvest the world will remember when you’re gone."
""
"In your lifetime, your harvest will change. No longer will you reap what you sow, but you will, finally, be blessed enough to freely sow into others that which you’ve spent a lifetime learning to reap."
""
"This harvest doesn’t happen suddenly, nor does it offer a loud arrival. And if you never bother to look for it, the third harvest may go regretfully unharvested. "
""
"Maybe there’s nothing wrong with how you feel."
""
"Maybe you’re simply preparing for your third harvest. "
""
"The harvest that builds the soil."
"The harvest that is purely you."
"The harvest of your lifetime."
"The longest harvest."
"The third harvest."
""
"Be the soil."
""

Everyone wants to change the culture, but nobody wants to change themselves. This one might hurt a little … But only if you bother to read it."
""
"There are thousands of definitions of culture. Most are paragraphs long. Mine is simple: What you consistently reject and demonstrate."
""
"That’s it: “People here don’t do things like that.”"
""
"You might work in a company with a great culture or a lousy one."
""
"You might be surrounded by people you trust and respect, or be in the throws of politics, or scolded today for something that was celebrated yesterday. "
""
"We love to talk about company culture. "
""
"Why? Becuase it’s an easy target. And it lets us off the hook."
""
"We can credit or blame company culture to fit any moment, and nobody is on the hook for it at that moment."
""
"But the culture of you?"
"You are on the hook for that every day."
""
"The culture of you isn’t about a boss, a coworker or income."
""
"The culture of you is simple: What do you reject and demonstrate?"
""
"The culture of you needs nobody’s approval, permission, or explanation."
""
"But …"
""
"The culture of you needs a definition."
""
"Without a definition, there will be drift and tolerance."
"Without a definition, there will be no consistency."
"Without a definition, there can be no sense of control."
""
"Look, there will come a day when simply surviving the day will fail to be enough. It will finally get so bad that you move beyond the temptation of action into actually doing something."
""
"There will be a day when cowering, changing, navigating, and reacting to matters outside your control will break you."
""
"There will be a day when you take inventory of all you are, all you have; all you have overcome, and all you are capable of being, and you will decide to REJECT how you have come to be treated, and you will find a new way to DEMONSTRATE what you are capable of doing and who you are capable of being."
""
"This is the day you will define the culture of you."
""
"At that moment, without words, all of us will see what you consistently reject and what you demonstrate."
""
"The culture of me is right here — full display. I won’t apologize, change it for anyone, or modify it to fit everyone. But I believe my culture is for someone, and maybe my someone is you."
""
"I REJECT the notion that I have to do today that which paid me yesterday, and I will show up here, every day, to DEMONSTRATE that you don’t have to either."
""
"And while I’m rambling … I REJECT the belief that you have sold your freedom to be you, purely you, in the lines below or in the hours outisde of work. And I hope that at least one of you will DEMONSTRATE that for all of us in the comments below."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40, boss."
"This is a place where you are on the hook for you."
""
"Now, what will you reject, and"
"What will you demonstrate?"
""
"Let’s be up to something."
"Let’s do the work."

My son, who is eleven, asks me to play “Would you rather.”"
""
"He asks me questions like:"
""
" - Would you rather have laser eyes or be able to time travel?"
" - Would you rather be able to fly or breathe underwater?"
" - Would you rather be locked in a cage with apes or snakes?"
""
"We can all agree that the first two are easy: time travel and fly, but the last one requires more thought. And maybe a few follow-up questions, a move forbidden by my son."
""
"There is something fascinating about the game. He loves playing it simply to see what I will say. But some of his questions elicit a reaction in me that is, well, confounding."
""
"If you are like me, the first two questions were probably easy. You may have chosen different answers, but you could cite your justification — a justification we could believe because you could play it off as a fact. "
""
"But the third question was a stumper."
"The third question caused you to stop and think."
"The third question is different."
""
"Why is it different? Because it’s real — or at least our minds tell us it could be. It’s not probable, but it is real. So we apply actual logic, as we see it, to our answer."
""
"We run this question through a different part of our brain. The part of our brain that considers failure and calculates risk. The part of our brain that considers consequences."
""
"So, while our kids await an answer, we are doing the math. This is usually around the time we say, “That’s a good question. Let me think about that.” as we walk off. Simply, it’s easier to avoid it than to get it wrong."
""
"We’re good at deciding what’s best for other people, about matters we can’t possibly impact, and about fictional powers that won’t come about in our lifetimes. There are no consequences."
""
"But when faced with a decision that has virtually zero probability of happening, we stop, or we avoid facing it—to avoid a consequence."
""
"Ironically, the same is true of our career. Every day, in our mind, we construct our own game of “Would you rather.”"
""
"Would you rather: "
" — Sleep in or go to your workout"
" — Try to catch up on email, or work on this report"
" — Work on employee reviews, or write your self-assessment"
" — Stand out in the meeting, or blend into the middle and nod"
" — Tolerate ten more years here, or chase something you can’t yet define."
""
"This leads to the ultimate ‘Would you rather’ question of your lifetime, should you choose to play."
""
"It’s the question that leads you here."
""
"The question has no obvious answer, but will have an obvious consequence. But unlike apes and snakes, this question has a 100% probability of finding you, and it will, one day, force an answer. So …"
""
"Would you rather:"
"Bet on yourself and live with the results, or never leave and live with the regret of wondering what might have happened if you had?"
""
"I made the trade. And, as it turns out, betting on yourself is also a fantastic way to fly and time travel."
""
"And all it required was a start and a guide."
""
"It’s time to fly."
""

"The only thing that makes you special in your position is that it’s your name on the door. If you were gone, your company wouldn’t miss a beat. In 6 months, it would barely remember you were there."
""
"Like it or not, this is how it works. Like it or not, this is how companies survive."
""
"But -"
"The same is not true of your family; that’s not how your family works, and it is not how it survives."
""
"If you were removed from your family today, it would devastate them. The void would not be filled in a week, month, or year. And maybe not in a lifetime."
""
"And -"
"If your family left you today, it would launch you into a tailspin you may never recover from. The void would not be filled in a week, a month, or a year. And maybe not in a lifetime."

"So what if, this year, you changed the currency of your life from income to time?"

"What if you started to guard your time with the same zeal you defend against your tax dollars?"

"What if you gave equal effort to time investment in others as you offer to the financial investment of things?"

Will you do something today that you will remember in a year?"
""
"Will you be part of something today that someone else will remember, and maybe even thank you for, in a year? What about a decade or generation from now?"
""
"For the rest of your days, your life will be the story you get to tell. It’s the culmination of your experiences, your friendships, your achievements, and how you used the gifts you were given. Your pen, your canvass, your decisions, your story."
""
"A lifetime, your lifetime, is a story. A story you write in the moment, one you tell to others reminiscent of the past, and one you take with you or leave behind when you’re gone."
""
"Today, you will write your story of today. The question is, will it matter when you’re gone?"
""
"But in the moment after you’re gone, the survivability of the story of your life — all our lives — will dim. Not because it had little meaning to others, but because you’re no longer there to share it or build from it. You simply become part of someone else's story. Until one day, it’s a story lost to time. This happens every day."
""
"If you think I’m wrong, what was the first name of your mother’s, mother’s father? No doubt he was important. You wouldn’t be here without him."
""
"But most won’t know his first name, how he met your great-grandmother, or what his favorite stories were to tell. He is known best as the somber-looking man in the black and white photo that used to hang in your mom’s house. An image of an un-expressive man who looked a lot like your kid brother lookes today. But that’s not his story."
""
"Why am I rambling on this? Well, for two reasons."
""
"First, maybe it’s time for you to start writing a better story. It is your pen, your canvas, and this is as young as you will ever be. So, is this the best story you have to tell? Is it a story that doesn’t require you to be here for others to hear it or use it?"
""
"Second, maybe your story needs a book. Not a journal you hide, a book to leave behind forever. A book that turns your work into your works."
""
"— Your WORK is what you do, but your WORKS are what you leave for others. And maybe your works need a home that can outlive you."
""
"Look, your work will end one day, but your works, if captured appropriately, can live forever. Your works will be the story you will tell to your great great grandkids, even if you’re not there."
""
"Your works matter."
""
"I created Normal 40 to inspire people to write a better story; their story on thier terms."
""
"And I created Normal 40 Publishing, a company I share with Jeremy D Brown, to help capture your life’s best WORKS for the generations who will follow."
""
"As my mom’s dad said just before he died: “A lifetime is a long time, but it didn’t take me long to live it.” These words were true to him, true to me and will be true for you."

"If you want to ramble on what Normal 40 Publishing could do for you, send me a note: Lon@normal40.com. I have a dream of inspiring your life’s best works."

For more than twenty years, I was told I was a lousy writer. "

"Time and again, I was told my grammar was flawed, my sentence structure was at a 7th-grade level, and my spelling was so poor it distracted the reader. I was told to find a job — a career — where my writing would not create a problem."
""
"But that isn’t the worst part."
""
"The worst part is I believed them."
""
"So much so that I nearly didn’t take a job with the United States Senate because I knew it would require much writing. I feared my poor writing would be discovered and that I would become an example of who not to hire and how not to write. I was petrified. "
""
"After only a few months, my greatest fears came true; my writing was discovered, and I was singled out. Publically. "
""
"It was discovered to contain emotions and clarity helpful to others on staff. It was useful in conveying what constituents were saying, what help they were seeking, what injustices they were enduring, and what sentiments they were sharing. "
""
"My writing was called out. Not because it was great but because it was mine — my voice, my style, and my words."
""
"I’ve used my writing ever since to build all I have. It was never a weakness; it was simply misunderstood by the wrong audience, and I allowed it to be judged by the wrong people."
""
"What feedback have you received that you think is wrong? What have you been told you’re not good at, but you love it anyway? What have you been told to quit because there’s no future in it? "
""
"What if you are allowing yourself to be judged by the wrong people? What if you are asking the wrong audience? What if the greatest gifts in your lifetime can’t be used where you are now? "
""
"Finding your voice and audience is some of the most challenging work of a lifetime. It takes time, energy, several reps, and much courage. But finding your own music is worth the chase."

We are all wired to follow the well-trodden paths — we call them org charts, succession plans, down-lines, chains of command, and corporate ladders. Everything around you is built to keep you in line…for someone else."
""
"If liking this post feels out of line for you, I rest my case."
""
"To get what we hope to have next, we seek the familiar; to stay within the boundaries of what's already accepted. To separate our performance, we resolve to do better today that which was done well enough yesterday."
""
"But just underneath the surface, lurking in your mind’s courage, are these relentless "What ifs." Questions that defy convention and take you off the worn path. But there they are: The whispers of unexplored territory, a new path, the sparks of creativity that refuse to be extinguished. And it is calling you out."
""
"Pondering "What if" is not a curse; it is a sign of life and the genesis of progress. It's the compass of a curious mind. A stubborn voice that refuses to believe this is all there can be — for you."
""
"But the grind of life—and the illusion of loss—has conditioned you to silence your "What ifs." People in your upline may deem them as distractions, naysayers might label them as unrealistic, and our own fears can cast them aside as fanciful daydreams — available only to others."
""
"But what if your “What ifs” were a gift from your future self? What if they were a sign of things to try, people to connect with, and new adventures to lead you to your own path? What if you listen to that voice?"
""
"What if you never do?"
""
"I’ve dedicated eighteen months of my life to talking to people wrestling with their own personal “What if.” I have learned that they all feel a pull to something: They all wish the answers were more obvious, and they all feel like chasing it now leaves them with something to lose, and they feel the clock now works against them more than it used to."
""
"And each of them is correct."
""
"I have also learned that the “What ifs” that find you also have a life. Over time, these ideas and challenges from a future you, if ignored long enough, also go to rest — forever. Taking with them a piece of you you’ll forever miss."
""
"There is another side to “What if.” A regretful side. The side that asks: What if you never start? What if you never chase? What if you never find out? What if you leave nothing behind?"
""
"The “What ifs” that find you are a gift. But if ignored long enough, they too become obedient and lost to the path—forever."
""
"I answered my what-ifs when I left my corporate job. What if others have felt like me? What if I can build a community to inspire change? What if I build it, and it changes a life? What if I build something to leave behind, forever?"
""
"Normal 40 is a movement whose mission is to relentlessly inspire a true accounting of yourself and what you are capable of, then beg you to answer a single question:"
""
"Is today the day you start?"
""
"This one is on you."
"But you can join us."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #motivation #jfds #coaching

This is not a post about quitting. "
"It’s a post about starting."

"This is not a post about a product."

""
"This is not a post for other people."
"This is a post for you."
""
"Today, what’s more exciting:"
" — The work you get to perform and it’s impact, OR "
" — That you’re one day closer to vesting?"
""
"Welcome to the power of the golden handcuff."
""
"Golden handcuffs serve one purpose: To keep you longer than you would logically stay—if your life wasn’t for sale."
""
"Golden handcuffs don’t exist to reward those who lead best. Golden handcuffs are awarded to those who can tolerate the longest."

""
"The matters of your life matter. "
"The moments you’re living today also matter."
""
"There are many ways to live a golden life, and most don’t come with cuffs."
""
"Busting the cuffs and taking back your life is wicked hard. Umm, that’s why you’re there — and why you’re reading this."

"Staying won’t give you more options; it just takes your days."

"In 2021, I didn't know much about digital assets, blockchain, or crypto. But I knew if I could find people who did and sit eyeball-to-eyeball with them to drill them on platforms and potential, I would allocate a small portion of my portfolio to blockchain."

I hear it constantly: “I wish I would get let go with a severance, then I would finally start.”"
""
"That makes a great headline, but a lousy story."
""
"Severance comes up in a lot of rambles. The severance someone got or didn’t get. The severance they would get if they were let go. And the severance they would lose if they quit on their own terms."
""
"Nobody dreams of spending severance on a new car, a boat, or a 40-day cruise around the world."
""
"You think about how to use it to finally build the life you’ve always wanted. To try the thing you’ve always wanted to try and to chase the dream you’ve always wanted to chase. You don’t think about things you can buy with it; you think about the impact you can have with it."
""
"Most believe the money will make it easier to finally start. But if you’re being honest, that’s not it."
""
"You have enough money — as evidenced by all the things you already have that you no longer use. You don’t need the money — that’s the headline."
""
"For you, severance is not to compensate for a lack of reserves."
""
"For you, severance is to compensate for a lack of courage, the courage needed to do it on your terms. You hope someone forces your hand to act. You need someone to kill your excuses. That’s the severance you seek."
""
"Sure, that paragraph will annoy some, but that doesn’t make it untrue."
""
"Here’s the deal — you are not alone. And you’re still reading because you know I’m talking to you or to the person you used to be."
""
"Look, you don’t need the severance money to get started. That makes a good headline. But three months' pay, in the grand scheme of your life, will be invisible and untraceable; it will be lost to the real story."
""
"The story is that you are waiting for someone else to decide when you start, or if you start. The story is that every day that passes without action eventually becomes a mountain of regret resting atop the deepest part of your aging soul."
""

An open letter to the company that knocked off my book:"
""
"Dear Vickie Press,"
""
"A few fans of my book, The Trade, contacted me this week about a companion workbook they were excited to find online. "
""
"You can imagine my confusion. You know, because I hadn’t yet published one."
""
"But I see you have."
""
"And that’s what brings me here."
""
"When I heard about the unauthorized workbook that bears my name, I did what every self-respecting author would do: I purchased one."
""
"Hey, don’t judge. Part of me was flattered. But then I received it."
""
"Look, Vicke, I dedicated one year of my life to writing a book that I thought maybe, just maybe, could inspire people to bet on themselves and to finally accept and chase what’s been chasing them. I aspire to help people to do the math in their lives and answer an honest question: Am I capable of more, on this day?"
""
"A high bar, admittedly."
""
"To do this, I used my story, all of it. But I also used the emotion, challenges, and inspiration of more than 450 people I interviewed along the way, and I share their stories."
""
"Then I wrote it. Every word. Every line. Every story. Every page."
""
"I wrote it."
""
"Then, I gave it a title, and I dedicated my work to the people I would give it all away to save: My family."
""
"Last, and only then, did I put my name on it."
""
"See, there’s a lot that went into The Trade for me. I am so proud of the work the Normal 40 community has created."
""
"The Trade is working. It is inspiring change around the world."
""
"When I first discovered your workbook, I expected to be upset. But I wasn’t. My publisher and attorney urged me to reach out and ask you to cease, but I won’t. My wife encouraged me to do what I think represents the Normal 40 community, so I will."
""
"Vickie, or whatever your name is, it would be nice to connect with you. See, the Normal 40 is more than me. It’s more than a book or knockoff content. It’s a movement—one that expects more than what you have offered."
""
"I love the idea of a workbook. I plan to offer one. It will be something built by and for the Normal 40 community, and I can’t wait to share it."
""
"In the meantime, I have some ideas on how you can make your product better. You quote me a lot, and you repeat many of my thoughts and teaching, but your writing prompts are, well, tragically weak."
""
"The name of my book is called The Trade, not The Take. And while I’m not going to put any energy into stopping your take, I hope you would put some energy into making this something the Normal 40 community should trade their money to own."
""
"And, if interested, I will help you."
""
"So, the ball is in your court. Well, I guess my ball is in your court…whatever. As long as you have it, use it to create something incredible, something worthy of Normal 40’s name."
""
"Vicki, there are a lot of things I’m willing to trade. But my name is not one of them. If you want to make something that helps this community…"
""
"You know where to find me."
""
"Lon -"
""
"Author of The Trade, and"
"Founder of Normal 40"

Sunk Cost: An expense or investment already incurred that cannot be changed or recovered."
""
"We think of sunk costs as a financial term. But there are sunk costs of your time, too. "
""
"Your calendar is likely filled with the things you initially invested in last week, last month, or last year. It may have remnants of the good work that is part of your future, but it also may have the sunk work you hope someone will muster the courage to kill for you. "
""
"Maybe only a portion of your day is sunk, but tell that to someone with a terminal illness. "
""
"Every day you tolerate working in the wrong place doing the wrong thing, working on the things that no longer fill you up, you are adding to your sunk costs, and you know it. "
""
"Once you discover your future isn’t where your feet are—that the work you’re doing today is to get a paycheck while you hope tomorrow delivers you a new mission—your time is sunk. As the definition says, the day will be “an investment incurred that cannot be changed or recovered.” A day when you could have, but didn’t. "
""
"Sunk costs are real, but they yield an asset. The asset is you: The skills you have built, the friends you’ve made, the gifts you want to explore … all of it is an asset that moves with you. "
""
"I turned my sunk costs into a life-long asset in February 2022.  I loved what I did, and I did it well.  But when my future was no longer where my feet were, when my job was not going to deliver anything more than income, my time was, in fact, being sunk into something for which I had no passion. "
""
"So, I started something else. I did the hard internal work of getting clear on a mission, and then I chased it. That change has built the Normal 40  and has brought be here. And, ironically, it brought you here, too."
""
"Today, in every writing, you can see my passion."
"Today, in every moment, I can see the results."
""
"Some days are better than others."
""
"But none are spent chasing something that isn’t part of my future. I have no more sunk days. And neither should you."
""
"A bad day on my mission is 10X better than a great day on someone else’s."
""
"Let’s be up to something."
"Life is better that way."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #jfds #inspiration #motivation #beuptosomething #coaching #lifestyle

Keeping it real, here are the top ten topics we’ve rambled about over the last 90 days. I thought you might be interested in seeing what goes down inside Normal 40. "
""
"1. Belief: The importance of knowing more is possible for you. If you didn’t think there was more, you wouldn’t be here."
""
"2. These are your best days: This is the exact right time to start. Yesterday’s gone. Tomorrow will not be easier; it will be harder."
""
"3. Honest Self-Reflection: You have to know clearly what you want more of and less of. What are you great at, and you know it? What do you no longer wish to do, and you know it? What will you no longer tolerate — really? "
""
"4. Relationships: The work will start face-to-face but then transition to shoulder-to-shoulder. People will come from all over to help those on a journey, but walk right past those hiding in plain sight."
""
"5. Pay attention to the Tension: Where is the tension in your life, really? Where is the tension in what you are doing and what you have left undone? What is causing that pit in your gut, really? Give it a name so we can fight it. "
""
"6. Finding a Guide: We all need one. Nobody ahead of you claims to have done it alone. We all try to do it alone (I did), and then we wish we had asked for a guide sooner (I did). For the record, if you didn’t need one, you wouldn’t be here. Find one you admire, and ask."
""
"7. Your Clock: Today is the youngest you will ever be. What do you hope to have in your life that you don’t have yet? What do you hope to see in your lifetime that you have not seen yet? What do you hope to do in your life that you have not done … yet? "
""
"8. The Freedom to Choose: Nobody shows up to do this for you. You will do something, or you will wait. Last week, you did something, or you did not. Last year, you did something, or you did not. This week, you will do something, or you will not. But that’s all on you. You are really waiting for you. "
""
"9. The Incredible Power of Your Second Resume: We all have a First Resume — yours is your LinkedIn profile. BUT — When you turn the things you hide from (your failures, losses, and shortcomings), the things on your Second Resume, into the wisdom from which you teach, they all become servants to you as they become your strengths."
""
"10. Purpose and Action: Knowing who you are and mustering the courage to stand in it — at all ages — will be the hardest thing we are challenged to do every day. Those who do are those we admire. Who are you, and what would you give it all up for if you were ever asked to produce it? "

"Most come to this canyon and never cross it and surrender their dreams to the canyon. Some wander in unprepared and never make it across.  And others find their clarity and use it as a map. "
""
"We all can list the names of people we know who have done each."
""
"If you’re here, you are at that canyon’s edge—gazing out at its vastness, hoping you find the courage to set down the path. "
""
"Know this: You won’t have the answers before you start the journey; your answers will be found along the path. The path you’re staring at as you read this."
""
"As you stare at that path, don’t do so with the wrong question in mind. "
""
"Don’t ask: “What do I want in this life?”"
"Ask this: “What is needed from me in this life?” "
""
"It’s not what you want that awaits you on the other side; it’s where you will find that what you have is also what is needed."

""
"You are in control. "
""

In February 2022, I left the safety of my W2 income because my future wasn’t there, and I felt it. I left with no guarantee, no safety net, and no backlog of clients. But I left anyway."
""
"I thought the accomplishments recorded my LinkedIn profile would get me to where I was going next."
""
"It turns out I was wrong."
""
"Today, people worldwide hire me to help them through a personally confusing time. They come to me because I am safe; I don’t know them, but I understand them. "
""
"They arrive feeling confused and frustrated. They are living under the illusion of great joy from yesterday’s achievements but feel empty, alone, and frustrated as they are slowly ground down into something lower than they thought they would be. And all of this leaves them feeling guilty for wanting something other than what they already have. So, in desperation, you find me."
""
"They don’t come to me because I worked for the United States Senate or because I can help them invest, grow a business, or sell their company. They don’t come to me for anything on my LinkedIn profile."
""
"They come to me because I learned how to use it, and simultaneously live without it."
""
"I’ve dedicated the last 18 months to studying, understanding, interviewing, and coaching the world’s highest achievers. I didn’t find a single one of them; they found me. Why? Because they needed a teacher, and they didn’t know a class like this existed—or that anyone else might need the same help."
""
"After 575 calls with people from around the world, I discovered none of them found me for my First Resume — all of them have found me because of my Second Resume, the one that owns the fear, the misgivings, the mistakes, and the failures. The Resume that went against convention and placed a bet — on me."
""
"They found me because the Normal 40 gives them hope — hope that what has become possible for me can one day be possible for them."
""
"My First Resume helped me build an impressive living. But my Second Resume has taught me how to build a life. "
""
"You’re not here because of my First Resume. You don’t even know who I am, where I live, what I’ve done, and how good I think I used to be at making money for others."
""
"You are here because of my Second Resume. You are here because I left it all, took a bet on myself, put the chips on the table, and then had the guts to talk about what I got right and what I got wrong. That’s why you’re here. And you’re not alone."
""
"There will be a day when we should talk. That day was probably six months ago. There will be a day when you get tired of hearing my words bounce around your head and ricochet through your chest."
""
"Look, I can wait for you forever. The problem is, you don’t have that much time."
""
"So, when you’re finally ready to join my class and start working on your Second Resume, it’s best to quit wasting your time."
""
"This is your Normal 40: Let’s be up to something."
""
"Start here: https://lnkd.in/g7RxxAHs"

A Normal 40 #Ramble with Bill:"
" - Age 45:"
" - Sales Executive"
" - Exceeding goals"
""
"Feeling:"
" - Bored"
" - Experiencing “Pay-only joy”"
" - In a no-growth zone at work"
" - Boxed in — wishing he had left two years ago"
""
"Dream:"
" - Flexibility with the satisfaction of worthwhile work"
" - Wants to see people again; travel and make connections"
" - To be the star of his own story"
" - Creatively solve problems in pursuit of helping others"
" - To get laid off for the severance, so he can"
" - Start my own company using my core gifts of connectivity"
""
"“When you’re done faking the answer to how good things are in your life, you might be in your Normal 40.”"
"___________________________________________________"
""
"Each week, I ramble with people I’ve never met. And I never know who I will come face-to-face with every time I hit “Join Meeting.” "
""
"If you’re reading this, you’ve already judged Bill. Right now, you have an opinion of him. Some of you will want him to suck it up and quit complaining, and others will find yourself rooting for Bill — hoping that he takes a bet on himself and wishing you could somehow help."
""
"And, for a select few of you, you will bounce back and forth from one option to the other, depending on your own mood at the moment — mimicking the indecision you have within your own life."
""
"Me? I’m rooting for Bill. But based on what he shared, his work there is done. His future isn’t where his feet are, and he knows it. "
""
"Waiting a few years to get a few months of severance is a decision that is delaying the inevitable — and it is wasting time. Waiting for someone else to make the inevitable dicision for you is the problem, not the solution."
""
"His work there is done. I know it, he knows it, and you know it."
""
"But that doesn’t make it easy. It will be the most challenging thing Bill will face in a decade, if not a lifetime. Just like it was, has been, or will be for you, too."
""
"But hearing this perspective from an outsider changes things. It can change everything to hear someone else say, “You are ready, and this is the best day you have left to finally start.”"
""
"I talk to a lot of Bills. Some of them stay. Some chase a promotion. Some leave to join something different. And some leave to start something new."
""
"There is no universal correct answer for generic Bill, but there is always a correct answer for you. Even if you do know what it is."
""
"Okay, so this got a little long. But the key is this:"
""
"You don’t have many “Best day to start” left. After this weekend, you’ll have a few less. When you want to start, you have to act. When you don’t know how to act, book a #ramble with me and download “The 14 Questions That Changed My Life” from my website. Join my free, closed and private LinkedIn Group or join The Insider on my website."
""
"I don’t know what Bill will do. But I can tell you this: He has started — and he is up to something."
""
"Let’s be up to something, too. Life is more fun that way."
""

Loss Aversion (Normal 40 Edition)"
""
"loss a·ver·sion /lôs əˈvərZHən/ (NOUN)"
""
"A mindset that haunts individuals in their prime years, causing them to fear losing what they've amassed more intensely than they experience the joy of gaining something greater."
""
"Symptoms include clinging to safety nets, unbalanced loyalties, living on yesterday’s achievements, believing next year will be easier (one more set of options), low intimacy, weakening friendships, uncertainty over who they are without their title, self-medication, declining self-confidence, isolationism, frustration, false moods, and guilt."
""
"Remedies include betting on oneself with small calculated risks. Exploring small trades and building anticipation for an adventure. Accepting this is normal and acknowledging that others can help. Surrounding yourself with others who are on the journey with you and take action."
""
"Outcomes take two distinct courses: To stay and protect, dedicating the balance of a lifetime to professional defense where you run out the clock. Or reorient your mind towards life on offense — chasing what you are here to chase and using all the clock you have left to play."
""
"Overcoming N40 Loss Aversion starts at the moment you accept you have it, and then take action against it. It happens when you get back on offense and lean into your courage. "
""
"You will or you will not."
"Offense or defense."
"You decide."
""
"It’s your ball, if you want it."
"Maybe this is the sign you’ve been looking for."
"Maybe you should finally book that #ramble"
"Maybe you need a little offense. "

After 464 calls from people I’ve never met, I’ve concluded that Corporate Anxiety is real, and it might be killing you — really. "
""
"I don’t know that there’s an actual definition, but here’s mine:"
""
"Corporate Anxiety (noun): "
""
"A condition characterized by persistent stress, unease, changing priorities, competing deadlines, and increasing apprehension experienced within the corporate environment. "
""
"It arises from the pressure to meet demanding expectations and the constant need to navigate complex and competitive corporate and political structures — even when home or on vacation."
""
"It blossoms in isolation and the absence of intimacy where those suffering don’t feel they have any right to complain, don’t want to appear as if anything is wrong, and don’t want to introduce problems into an already stressed home. Most feel guilty and ashamed for having so much but want something else, so they persist, again. "
""
"If this is you, do something. You are not alone, and this condition is stealing your life. Find a friend, find a coach, or find me."

"The good news is — There’s help everywhere."
"The bad news is — You have to ask for it."
""

"What you feel is normal."

Corporate Anxiety: Do you have it? "
""
"You know the feeling: You have a never-ending loop of work scrolling through your mind, and then you overthink and fixate on everything relating to employment and the consequences that might accompany your incomplete tasks. "
""
"You’re constantly annoyed at home and take it out on other people or things — or you avoid everyone and self-medicate. "
""
"You have low-level self-doubt swirling around your mind most days. And, worst of all, the dreadful pit in your gut that finds you late into every weekend and virtually shuts you off from anything that could give you joy. "
""
"Add a few layers of guilt for having so much and feeling so … frustrated, and voila —"
""
"You have Normal 40 Corporate Anxiety. "
""
"I know, because I used to have it."
""
"How did I respond?"
""
"Well, first, I hoped I would wake up and feel better. But that didn’t happen."
""
"Then, I hoped my past achievements would be enough to buy me some time — but in reality, it wasted my best years. "
""
"Then, I hoped nobody would notice when I quit seeking big assignments. "
""
"Then, I hoped that someone on LinkedIn would pluck me out of the 900 million LinkedIn users and give me the perfect job, even though I had no idea what that would be. "
""
"Then I discovered something harsh: "
" - Nobody was going to fix how I felt,"
" - Nobody was going to come do this for me, and"
" - Nobody was going to appear and bet on me magically. "
""
"So, I made a plan. I created an exit strategy from my rich living, and I traded it for a chance to live a rich life. "
""
"One year later, I resigned. One year after that, I wrote a book about it. "
""
"Now I help others navigate their path through Corporate Anxiety. And, let me say, I’m good at it. "
""

"The Harvard Study of Adult Development, initiated in 1938, follows participants' lives (and lifetimes) to uncover what leads to healthy and fulfilling lives. It involves two groups; college students and underprivileged youths, aiming to reveal insights about physical, mental, and emotional well-being, relationships, and personal development across the lifespan."
""
"To date, it is the longest-running study (ever) dedicated to adult development, and the study remains ongoing. And it is, well, fascinating. "
""
"The central question of the study is: "What factors contribute to a happy and fulfilling life?""

"While there are several factors that contribute, there is one factor, available to you within the hour, at no cost, that changes everything. So, I wanted to write about it. "

If you’ve never heard of the Harvard Adult Development Study, you’re missing out on something mind-blowing."
""
"The Harvard Adult Development Study, launched in 1938, has attempted to unravel (or explain) the factors behind a “fulfilling life.” "
""
"For the last 85 years, the study has followed two cohorts of people from adolescence to adulthood, to old age and death — to answer this seemingly simple question: "
""
""What are the factors that contribute to a fulfilling and successful life across adulthood?" "
""
"For 85 years, the study has followed the same people to every corner of their existance. They used in-person surveys, interviews, medical examinations, and analysis of personal records to uncover the interplay between genetics, environment, relationships, personal choices, and life experiences that shape individuals' well-being and overall life satisfaction."
""
"For 85 years, researchers have gathered data on various aspects of participants' lives, including physical health, relationships, career trajectories, and emotional well-being — across generations. "
""
"So, what is the study's standout revelation? "
""
"“Quality relationships reign supreme.”"
""
"85 years of research, multiple generations, and millions of dollars well spent comes down to this: "
""
"Are you a friend?"
""
"I have more thoughts on this incredible study that I will share in my newsletter this weekend. I call it “The Rambler,” you can sign up on my website or the Normal 40 page. "
""

"NEW EPISODE: NORMAL 40: THE PODCAST"
""
"In March 2022, I was only a few weeks past my “Normal 40 Moment.” I had recently left my corporate job and was exploring the groundwork for what I would do next. Somewhere in that fog, a dude named Paul Reynolds emailed me and asked for a ramble. We had never met, never talked, and he was sitting seven time zones away. But that note led to this call, which you will hear, and it shaped my Normal 40 Story.  "
""
"When we connected in March 2022, Paul was just emerging from a storm; one he wouldn’t wish upon anyone. He had been working long hours and traveling four days per week while climbing the ranks of a sizeable pharma company.  Paul was successful at work, leading big teams and pushing large projects. He was also doing his best to raise his two young girls, who were in their teens — as the minutes between Zoom calls would allow. "
""
"He was at the top of his professional game, but then it happened: A Diagnosis."
""
"His wife of more than 20 years, Judy, was diagnosed with liver cancer. The prognosis was dim, but the fight was on. And they fought it, together. Then, weeks later, COVID-19 began taking over and shutting down the world. "
""
"This was Paul’s Normal 40 Moment, and he took it."
""
"In a moment, what was once important — was not."
"In a moment, who he had become — didn’t matter."
"In a moment, where he was going — no longer made sense. "
"In a moment, he discovered who he needed to be next mattered most."
""
"Paul did what we all hope we would do in that situation: He quit. "
""
"His only regret is that he didn’t do it sooner."
""
"This podcast was recorded in March 2022. We never intended to make it a podcast, but the lessons Paul shares are lessons we all need to hear. "
""
"I never know who I’m going to meet every time I hit “Join Meeting.”"
""
"In this case, it sparked a friendship seven time zones away. Best of all, it gifted me with some omens for where I need to go next. "
""
"Enjoy this episode of Normal 40: The Podcast. "
""
"Next week you will hear an update from Paul on what he’s doing now."
""
"When your Normal 40 Moment finds you, find me. "
"This is what I do."

Maybe your work here is done. "
""
"Maybe you’ve given all you have to give, and you’ve taken all there is to take. Maybe your work there is done."
""
"You can find work to do every day. That is called productivity. You can write emails, draft memos, run meetings, and complete tasks. But if productivity isn’t leading you curiously forward — towards a future you that is exciting — that is called tolerance. "
""
"Look, everything you will ever do has a beginning, a middle, and an end. That includes your job. So, are you at the beginning, the middle, or the end of it? "
""
"Maybe that pit in your gut is not disdain for what you have in front of you this week; maybe it’s the smoldering frustration for what you have allowed yourself to tolerate last week."
""
"In 2022, after fourteen years of serving as a public company executive, I left my job. In short, I quit. "
""
"Quitting is easy. But knowing when to quit took me years. "
""
"If what you are doing today isn’t part of who you hope to be tomorrow, your work there is done. "
""
"Know what? Being done is okay.  In fact, it’s liberating. "
""
"If you’re wondering if you’re work there is done, it probably is."
""
"If it is, you might like this little book I wrote called “The Trade.” It’s written for people who are ready to be done tolerating."
""
"When you’re finally ready to start, find me here."

"This is an open note to dads:"
""
"For every first, there will be a last."
"Often they meet on the same day."
""
"I've shared a roof and a permanent address with Grace for the past eighteen years, five months, and three days."
""
"In that time, we've shared thousands of firsts."
"In that time, we've shared an equal number of lasts."
""
"Today is the another last. But it is also another first. "
""
"Time is real."
""
"The time you have left with kids in your home is moving from a list of firsts to the balance sheet of lasts. Before it's all over, it will balance perfectly."
""
"Your life has a clock. It will be cruel, relentless, and unwavering. It is not emotional or apologetic and offers no guarantees. You'll come to learn the only thing you can trust is that it will take the moments you have available to live today, and place them permanently into yesterday."
""
"Eighteen years, five months, and three days ago will be here before you know it."
""
"- Don't arrive at your future and wish you would have done more."
"- Don't arrive and wish you would have cared less about your car and more about our kids."
"- Don't arrive and regret you didn't take the trip; book the date, give the hug, or chase the dream."
"- Don't arrive and wish you would have tried harder, done more, or loved better."
"- Don't arrive with regret for a dream you never chased."
""
"I haven't got it all right in my life. Nobody does. But if there's one thing you ever take from me, let it be this:"
""
"Looking back from your last day, it will be easier to accept that you tried and failed than to admit you failed to try."
""
"You own that, dad."

“I want to change my story.”"
""
"Over the last year, I’ve had 451 conversations with people I’d never met. They arrive at the call from a million different paths — through healthcare, law practices, accounting firms, military service, and executive leadership. On the surface, they share little in common. But one layer beneath that hard exterior lies sameness."
""
"While who they are, what they do, and who they help are different, how they feel about who they have become is identical. In most cases, after a spouse, I’m the first person they’ve told."
""
"They arrive to me traveling their own unique path, but they come to me feeling the same way and wanting the same thing: A new challenge, a new way of feeling, and to be part of something bigger — something that lasts. They are searching for a new story built from the old."
""
"I know these people because I used to be one of them. Today, I have a new story I’m writing. It’s the story of the rest of my life, it will one day be bigger than me, and it started the moment I said: “My work here, in this chapter, is done.” "
""
"When you’re finally ready to write your new story, when your work there is done — when you have nothing left to give and nothing left to take — you will be ready. "
""
"You’re not quitting; you are finishing — and you are making room for the best years of your life. "
""
"And to start what’s next, you have to finish being what you have become, and then use it for what’s next."
""
"At that moment, the moment you decide your work there is done, new energy finds you. The energy you have been looking for."
""
"If you’re looking for that moment — for that energy, you will need a guide. If you think that guide might be me, we should talk. "
""
"If you ever want to ramble, I’m game.  "
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40 highway."
""
"Let’s write a new story."
""
"#normal40 #thetrade #ramble #inspire #motivate "
""

“I just want to put on my boots and go outside.”"
""
"Meet Normal 40 member #2944 — "
""
"Most of my calls have a typical flow. We start with a great get-to-know-ya, then there is some version of “If you’re here, there’s something brewing; tell me about it.” Before long, I will know the spouse and the kids, where they’ve been, and who they have become, and I will have a pretty good feel for what is tearing a hole in their chest."
""
"This guy, #2944, was frustrated. I could see it in his face, and I could feel it in his body movements. But I didn’t need to guess how he felt; he shared it in full detail. "
""
"He was frustrated by his reality. Frustrated that he was getting older, frustrated by his hours, and, in my opinion, frustrated that it looked so good from the outside but lived so empty on the inside. He summed up how he felt by this, “At the end of the day, after all of the good work, with all of these good people, I still bring home an empty cup of fulfillment. My heart is no longer there.” "
""
"When I asked #2944 what he wanted, his first reply was, “I don’t know.” But when pushed, he shared three more things:"
""
"1.) A chance to breathe and enjoy my blessings without guilt,"
"2.) A chance to be me and to be on myself without worry, and"
"3.) I want to put on my boots and go outside to do work that matters."
""
"Today, this dude has a lot of titles: Dad, son, ex-husband, husband, provider, vice president, boss, and friend. But he doesn’t feel like he has any options. He has worked himself into The Box. He feels trapped by his success and is wondering how to get out."
""
"We worked a plan, walked through some scenarios, and then I left him with some homework that might give him some clarity — and some room to breathe. "
""
"For two dudes who have never met, we covered three months of ground in 68 minutes. Before we were done, he looked different. He looked like a dude who had some control back, a dude who could breathe, and a dude who might be able to get his boots on sooner than later."
""
"#2944 is not a regular client. He simply needed to talk with someone who had been there. He needed some help to think about what’s next — and what’s in his control. He booked a ramble with me, and in 68 minutes, we put him back in his boots."
""
"When it’s your time to finally start, you’ll know it. I’ll be here when you need me. But don’t wait too long; you’re running out of runway, and I’m running out of openings."
""
"Welcome to the Normal 40. Nobody drives this one for you."
""

"It’s not about speed; it’s about trajectory."

"It’s not about quitting; it’s about starting."

"It’s not about having; it’s about chasing."
"It’s not about grinding; it’s about lifting."

This is the note I wrote to my two sons when I resigned from my position to chase what was next. "
""
"Dear son,"
""
"For everything in your life, there is a first, and there is a last."
""
"Your life will be full of first days. These bring you experiences to be excited about; something to chase, and something that lies ahead."
""
"But in your life, because math being what it is, there will also be an equal number of last days."
""
"Your first and last day of work, your first and last day of school, of a relationship, of days with friends, of time in our house, and your time with your mom and me."
""
"A first and a last. Every start eventually ends."
""
"That is how life works, and it doesn’t discriminate."
""
"Son, in your life, live for the last days, not the first."
""
"For in your life kiddo, it’s the last day that holds the score."
""
"A first day is about first impressions and fulfilling the aspirations of a person you may someday be."
""
"But your last day reveals the results; did you or did you not?"
""
"Your last day holds the score."
""
"Son, play for your last day."
""
"You see, today is my last day."
""
"And I choose to celebrate this day."
""
"I choose to walk the halls slowly, to give my thanks, and to be romantically nostalgic about how every day before this day has shaped me."
""
"I choose to consider what these halls took from me, and what I took from them."
""
"For 14 years, I’ve been building for this day."
""
"For 14 years, this day rested on a distant horizon."
""
"But on this day, we will share a sunset."
""
"Son, on this day, I choose to give heartfelt thanks for the friendships and the lessons I learned on a journey … time spent with some of the world’s best."
""
"On this day, I give thanks for those who took a risk on me and celebrate those who stay to carry the torch."
""
"In your lifetime, son, there will be as many last days as there are first days."
""
"On your last day, leave the company/friendship/relationship stronger, smarter, and better than you found it."
""
"When you do, it will do the same for you. And those are all things you can take with you, wherever your path leads you."
""
"And if you take it, you will have it."
""
"Today is my last day."
""
"I find myself nostalgic that my work here is done, but it is. I’ve completed what it is I came here to complete."
""
"This is the finish for which I am prepared. And it’s a finish for which I am proud."
""
"Tomorrow I will wake up to a first day."
""
"A day that comes before all of the rest. And I am ready."
""
"This is my trade."
""
"I will own this. And I will bet on me."
""
"And son, whenever you can, so should too."
""
"See you on the other side of today, kiddo."
""
"Cheers to the distant sunset."
""
"I’ll share a ride with you any day."
""
"Dad -"

Last week I wrote a poem for a lone tree that stands one half mile from the farm where I was born. Today, on #farmkidfriday, I’m sharing it."
""
"THERE’S A TREE I KNOW:"
""
"There's a tree I know, standing all alone,"
"Decades I passed by, its presence unknown."
"Awakening in spring, where birds sweetly sing,"
"A timeless witness to what life may bring."
""
"There’s a tree I know:"
"Limbs starting to break, leaves sparse on its frame,"
"Yet that tree I know remains steadfast, the same."
"Its height towering, shading sun's golden glow,"
"A prairie road’s sage, a friend, in times of woe."
""
"There’s a tree I know:"
"I used to drive by, with speed and haste,"
"But now I'm glad, I've found its faithful embrace."
"Like that tree, I've changed, taken different forms,"
"Farm kid and businessman, wise or foolish norms."
""
"There’s a tree I know:"
"From tractors to cars, passing by in my youthful prime,"
"That tree observed it all, through the passage of time."
"I wonder what that tree would say of me,"
"If it could share its thoughts, so wise and free."
""
"Would it see resilience, in every mile I faced?"
"Or moments of weakness, when I felt out of place?"
"Would it speak of growth, as seasons of life came and went?"
"Or caution my speed for the time, and how it’s been spent?"
""
"There’s a tree I know:"
"Obvious now, my companion, it was there all along."
"Guardian of secrets, so silent and so strong."
"In its unwavering presence, I now find solace and peace,"
"A constant reminder, that time does not cease."
""
"There's a tree I know, in its wisdom it sees,"
"The evolution of a kid, transformed like the breeze."
"And as I pass by this day, in the tree's knowing gaze,"
"I'm grateful for the lessons, its silent whispers now rsise."
""
"- Lon Stroschein. June 12, 2023."
""
"PS - Mom and dad - Thanks for making me a farm kid."

Normal 40: The Podcast - Episode 30"
""The Unimaginable Loss" - A Journey of Grief, Resilience, and Embracing Life's Trades with Larry Ambrose."
""
"I never know who I will meet every time I hit "Join Meeting." Today, it was Lawrence Ambrose."
""
"Tragedy struck Larry when his wife, Christine, died suddenly two months after the birth of their eighth child — while Larry was away on a work trip."
""
"Now a single parent of eight children under 15, he faced the unimaginable reality of being the family's sole provider and sole parent."
""
"Larry shares the heart-warming details of the day he met Christine and the heart-wrenching details after the loss. Learn how he coped with grief, worked through what felt like an impossible task of being a provider and the dad his kids needed, and how he was supported by a community that loved them."
""
"One day, Larry decided to make #thetrade, to ‘invert his retirement’ to give the kids what they really wanted - a dad. "
""
"This episode is a reminder of how life offers no guarantees and is best lived without regrets. And a reminder that nobody shows up to do it for you, but people will show up from everywhere to do it with you — but you have to ask for it."
""
"Larry will be the first to tell you that this was the most challenging work of a lifetime — and he is now thriving again. "
""
"He is excited to share the full details of his story in a book he is putting the finishing touches on — so follow along, and follow Lawrence Ambrose, for all the details."
""
"Finally, Larry is excited to launch his new business with his partner Dawne Jacoby, to use their experience to help people get unstuck, find their true purpose, and live a life of joy. "
""

Who are you … really?"
""
"One day you will wake up and meet yourself for the first time."
""
"One day, something will jolt your life into perspective. You will be forever changed and reduced to two halves: The person you were, and the person you are becoming."
""
"The hardest thing you will do in your life is finally discovering who you are capable of being, and then muster the courage to stand in it. "
""
"The feelings you have right now about work — the frustration, discontent, and lack of control — maybe it’s not about work."
""
"Maybe it is frustration with what you are settling for at this stage of life, but you’re blaming it on work."
""
"Discovering who you are never comes without a fight. It’s not easy, it’s not quick, and it won’t be free. It will require “The Trade.” "
""
"Dude, where will you be in one year from right now? Are you even the slightest bit curious about what you’re made of? "
""
"It is incredible how much your life can change in a year."
""
"For you — the stage is set, the slate is clean, the time is now, and the choice is yours: Start or tolerate. "
""
"The sun is just rising for you, boss."
"What will you do with your gifts?"
"Who will you be in a year?"
"I can’t wait to find out."

“I don’t know what happened. This place no longer inspires me.” "
""
"Normal 40 Ramble #289 — Midwest Accounting Firm partner. They continued …"
""
"“I don’t know if I changed and it stayed the same, or if it changed, and I stayed the same. But my work no longer fits. I used to show up to work happy and inspired, but now I show up and feel discontent and stale. Every quarter gets worse.”"
""
"“I just want to be inspired by my work and the impact I can have, and it’s no longer here. If I stay, other than a nice 401k, what legacy will I have? Who will care?”"
""
"“Worst of all, nobody knows how I feel, so my daily routine is starting to feel like an act — like I’m going through the motions. My best decade is in front of me, and I don’t know how to live it.”"
""
"Can you relate? Have you told anyone about how you feel?"
""
"This feeling will find you while you are at the top of your career. It will creep in slowly and nestle in tight. You assume it’s a funk - but it lingers. You have started to withdraw socially, become short at home, and live with a churn in the gut that never stops. You are perpetually annoyed and secretly miserable. "
""
"You feel trapped by your success, and it feels like you have no options. After all, who would walk away from all you have now? "
""
"Yep, I get it. You didn’t expect to feel this way, and it sucks. "
""
"Welcome to your Normal 40. I’ve dedicated the last year of my life to understanding and coaching it. Dude, you are not alone."
""
"There are things you can do. Many of them are obvious. Most of them are helpful. None of them are easy. And all of them require you to quit tolerating and start exploring. "
""
"I’ve written a book about Normal 40 called “The Trade.” It’s about all the things we go through at this stage of life; the things we see, the things we feel, the things we tolerate, the things we miss, and the actions we can take to get our life back."
""
"The book launches on July 19, 2023. The endorsements have been incredible (I’ll share them soon), the process has been humbling, and the impact will be fantastic."
""
"I have a ridiculous goal for this book - New York Times Best Seller list. I have no business being a NYTimes best seller. It’s a moonshot for a guy whose never written a book, has no marketing budget, and lost Oprah’s phone number. "
""
"But I’m going for it anyway."
""
"I want this little book about how to navigate and own this transition in life to be the thing that pulls this conversation out of the minds and into the homes of millions. "
""
"We’re announcing pre-orders in the coming week. If you’re interested in learning more, join the free community in the link below, or sign up for “The Rambler,” my free newsletter, from my website."
""
"Welcome to my dream — to inspire change."
"Welcome to your Normal 40."
"Nobody does this for you."

"So, let’s ramble."

"I was at the farm this week working on something that sounds absurd. But I’ve got a feeling I can’t shake. So, I’m exploring it."
""
"This photo is of an old gravel pit located on some of the most remote lands in Eastern South Dakota. There’s no real road to it, it’s best used by cows for grazing, and yard lights are almost invisible in all directions."
""
"The stars at night leap from the sky. The sunrises will stop you in your tracks. The fireflies float along the cool shores of “Preachers Run,” and it’s buffered by 800 acres of private property."
""
"This land has never been much more than a pasture … But something is gnawing at me. "
""
"Something is telling me to build a small lake on the prairie. Something is telling me to turn the old pit into something more, and something is telling me that it will one day heal."
""
"So … We hired a crew to make the pit larger and deeper, and I went home to groom the shores. "
""
"I still don’t know where this will all go, but I can’t wait to find out."

"I’ve dubbed this the Normal40 Reservoir … and maybe there’s something in it for both of us."

"And dude, I can’t wait to find out."

When I quit my job, people thought I was crazy. Six months later, most called and asked me how I did it."
""
"Leaving a job after years of investment isn’t like the movies. There’s not any well-rehearsed shouting or a march out."
""
"But there is emotion. There are friendships. There are mentorships. There is a history. "
""
"Wanting to leave — that becomes easy."
"But actually leaving — that is wicked hard."
""
"Before I left, I wasn’t sure I was ready. But after being gone, it became clear that I was. The same will be true for you."
""
"Two years before I left, I created a simple tool I used to help build clarity for my second-half story. It’s a day planner I created to help prioritize my days, simplify the relationship with my boss, identify overwhelm triggers, create more time with family, and catch myself in the act of dreaming about what might be next."
""
"If you’re interested, I put a copy on my website under “Executive Planner.” I don’t post about it much, but I talk to people every day who use it — and love it."
""
"The planner is not a silver bullet — those don’t exist. But you might be amazed at what it will do for you over a few weeks or months. There’s more in you than you think, and one of us can see it."

Bad news — The universe will do little to help you discover who you’re here to be. Rather than provide answers, it will confuse you with unlimited options, give you a mind that is hard-wired for comparison, and cultivate the fear of losing what you already have. And it will leave you to believe you’re all alone."
""
"Good news — When you step a single foot off the worn path of yesterday and into the wilderness of tomorrow, the universe brings you everything you will ever need to see a new future, and it will conspire to help you get it. A path will slowly present itself, joy will return, and others will show up to support you."
""
"If you’re wondering what’s next, if you know your best days are in front of you, and if you know that your time to take a shot is upon you, then I want you to take that step."
""
"The Irony — If this finds you, on this day, in this chair, with these feelings, and these concerns and emotions, then maybe this note isn’t an accident. Maybe this isn’t an algorithm. Maybe, just maybe, this is the universe staring you in the face, hoping you take one step off the worn path of yesterday."
""
"There is a trade looming before you. It is staring you in the face, sizing you up, and wondering if you will have the courage to make it, or if you will tolerate never finding out if you had it in you."
""
"I’m here as your insurance policy against that regret. "
""
"Take the step. This is your tomorrow."

Last week I posed about the #normal40challenge."
""
"A challenge to spend 40% LESS over the next 100 days, and…"
""
"To get rid of 40 pounds of stuff — The stuff lying around your house that you haven’t looked at in years. The stuff that’s bought, paid for, and holding you in place. The stuff taking up room with no use in sight. The stuff you cling to for memories of who you once were—the stuff you can see from where you’re sitting right now. "
""
"I’ll be honest, cutting 40% of my spending hasn’t been hard, but it’s only been a week. However, it has made me think twice before purchasing, and my spending is way down."
""
"But here’s the surprise …."
""
"Finding, boxing, and donating 40 pounds of stuff has been fantastic! I did it in a few hours this weekend, and it was great. It wasn’t easy to let some of this stuff go, and I don’t know why. But once it was gone and I could see all the new space I had made, I wanted to find more to get rid of."
""
"From books to boots and suit coats to suitcases… I donated 60 pounds of stuff I’ll never use, to a charity that will put it to good use this week."
""
"How are you doing on your #Normal40 challenge? "
""
"Maybe it’s time to make room for something more in your life."

"It’s how much you spend that owns you. "
""
"We’ve all been there — all of us. "
""
"BUT WAHT IF … For the next 100 days — "
" "
"What if you spent 40% less on material items you usually buy without even thinking about it? Check your Amazon purchases for the last 100 days if you wonder what I’m talking about. This is the shit you don’t need, but you’re able to buy - so you do. This is part of the problem - your problem. I know that because, well because you’re here. "
""
"What if you put that 40% in an account and called it your “Freedom Account.” "
""
"What if you invested part of that “freedom account” on experiences with people you love, to make memories you’ll never be able to make later, to live a life you enjoy today?"
""
"What crap will you cut out of your life, and what will you put in its place? You own this, boss. "
""
"I’m going to do it. From now until September 1 - I will spend 40% less on shit I don’t need … "
""
"- AND - "
""
"I will also DONATE 40 pounds of crap I’ve already purchased, the stuff lying around my house that I never really needed, to someone or someplace that genuinely needs it. "
""
"THIS will finally return some joy to the purchase."
""
"This is the #normal40challenge"
""
"For 100 days:"
"1.) Cut 40% of your spending on material items,"
"2.) Donate 40 pounds of things you own to a cause."
"3.) Spend a portion of the savings on an experience."
"4.) Share the photos/stories under #normal40challenge"
""
"Comment below: What will you be willing to trade to get your freedom back?"
""

"For me - I want more days like this. They won’t be free; they require a trade. And I am in control of it.

The social norm is changing."
""
"Working 60 hours a week and getting paid for 40 is dying. Free work and quarterly performance are the problems, not the solution."
""
"The old normal:"
"Arrive one hour earlier. "
"Stay there one hour longer. "
"Eat one more meal at the desk. "
"Take one more trip to save the deal. "
"One more email, one more text on vacation …"
""
"Then you arrive home to crash for the weekend and start all over Sunday afternoon. "
""
"Dude, this is the norm for your family, too. Ask them."
""
"There is a new normal, and it’s why you are here. "
""
"There is a different way."
"There is a better balance. "
"But there is a cost to live it … your image."
""
"When you’re finally sick of how you feel, we should talk. "
""
"I’m in Florida today with my family—parasailing, jet skiing, scuba diving, deep sea fishing, and lying by the pool in a house we rented for the week."
""
"I am spending a fraction of the money I thought I was saving for 25 years from now —"
""
"And I’m trading it to create memories I’ll be glad I have 25 years from now."
""
"In 25 years, I’ll still be able to make a buck. But I won’t be able to re-live this day."
""
"This is The Normal 40. "
"This is The Trade. "
"This is your day."
"Be glad you did."
