Keep some offense in your life."
""
"We’ve all heard it: “Defense wins games.” But earlier this year, during a 3rd grade basketball game, my son shot back with: “Defense doesn’t win games, scoring more points does.” Then he capped that off with: “And it’s more fun.”"
" "
"Nailed it."
""
"In our 20’s, we architect our offense: We’re living free. We take risk and we pursue whatever suits us. We draft our life’s first blueprint, then we take whatever shot we want, whenever we want to take it."
""
"In our 30’s, we grow and deploy our offense: We leverage our blueprint to build our family and our friendships. We work long hours to galvanize our personal brand. We lock into what feels successful and we use our scoring to buy things we want."
""
"But in our 40’s, we change. We start thinking about protecting the title and reputation we’ve already built, and we stop adding to the blueprint. We quietly stop building our brand, but actively try to preserve it. We stop taking the shots we used to take because we don’t want to risk a miss. In our normal 40, we start to convince ourselves that defense does win games. And that’s when we start feeling empty."
""
"Today, go play some offense. Take the shot. Make or miss it; who cares. When you do, you’ll remember that offense really is more fun & fulfilling!"
""
"#normal40

Those of us who grew up on a farm, or in small communities, know one thing for sure: There’s not many left."
""
"As these once prrofitable farm sites are returned to farm land, and as these once bustling towns are gently pulled to the ground, we all slip further away from a way of life that built so many of us."
""
"There’s something about an abandon farm site that captivates my imagination. I ponder, for hours, what it would have been like to sit at thier kitchen table, if only for a day."
""
"The kitchen table is where the family gathered each day, and where they prayed together before each meal. The kitchen table is where friends and neighbors gathered for fellowship and shared life. It’s where the decision was made to plant what crops where, and where to put the new barn. The table had the clearest view of the holiday decorations, and the best chance for a scent of bread. And if you look close enough, the cracks in the table held the tears of joy, tears of regret, tears of fear and the tears of loss."
""
"The table is where life happened. The life before the weeds and the wonder."
""
"I sure hope somone, sitting at a table somwhere, finds time to capture these stories of the American dream, and the American decency, before we too are gently pulled to the ground."
""
"#normal40"

Enough."
""
"Many high performing, impressive and successful individuals simply don’t feel as successful as they used to."
""
"Many look the same from the outside, probably act the same at work, but they feel completely different inside. While the family portraits still carry the same wide smiles, many have become more isolated, lost their drive and put thier work on autopilot."
""
"Some no longer motivate themselves to grow or take risks, nor do they invest in their health like they used to. Others have let friendships slide and allowed the relationships inside their home to weaken. For each, the change happened over time, but it’s created a new you to live with."
""
"Worst of all, most won’t talk about it with anyone; they don’t want anyone to know. Confessing to these emotions is admitting defeat. Over time, this mask grows heavy, the mind gets lonely and the way out feels harder to achieve."
""
"Enough!"
""
"This is normal. It’s all around us. If it’s you, welcome to your normal 40."
""
"When you’ve had enough, take it back. It’s your clock, and you are in control."
""
"Enough waiting. Enough hoping. Enough coasting. Enough masking. Own it and take it back."
""
"Disarm the autopilot and grab the yoke. Lets get back to where you used to be going."
""
"Today is enough. Own your clock."
""

Sometimes we need a wake up call."
""
"I had a client ask me about fulfillment. He indicated that he used to feel fulfilled at work, but that his feeling of fulfillment had started to elude him. Then, after a long pause, he confessed he had been on autopilot for more than 5 years."
""
"As he went on, he described all of the success he'd had, even in recent years. He shared the initiatives he'd led, the friends he'd made and the great leadership he'd provided. Yet, all of that success had simply created even more layers to the walls of the box that he had worked himself into."
""
"Something was missing, and it mattered at his core."
""
"At the same time, his success made any thought of change seem like a crazy risk. He would look like a fool leaving what he had in order to take a risk on something else. So he stayed; he put in the hours, and continued his search for fulfillment."
""
"As dudes, we need an adventure. Being successful isn't an adventure, but becoming successful is. For many, it's when we stop taking the risks, when we stop saying yes, when we stop taking the shot, when we settle - that steals our fulfillment."
""
"Many dudes in their Normal40 simply want to feel like they once did. And it's totally possible."
""
"Step 1: The Awakening. It's okay to want more, and it's time to respect the clock.

Can we address the 1100 pound steer in the room?"
""
"It happened every August; the county fair. Today a fair is about rides, concerts and delicious artery-clogging foods. But for farm kids, where I grew up, the fair ticket came about differently."
""
"The price of a fair ticket started in April when we pulled the best calves from the herd, and ended after we fed, tamed, groomed and broke the beasts to lead."
""
"Sometime between April and August there was the excitement of “the first lead.” This was the day we would try, for the first time, to lead the calves in an open pen."
""
"Once that gate flew open, we never knew if the calf would stand there, let you pull them around, or if it would take off running & bucking and drag you around the pen."
""
"They usually did all three."
""
"Worse, if we let go of the rope, the steer could never be broke."
""
"What 75 pound kid wants to tether themselves to a 1100 pound steer and throw open a gate in an effort to break them to lead? Well, we did."
""
"This stunt taught farm kids a lot: 1.) Respect the calf and to connect with them. 2.) See & mitigate risks. 3.) Expect a surprise and plan to pivot. 4.) Experiencing pain may be necessary to achieve the result. 5.) This was my responsibility. 6.) The reward was incredible."
""
"Cheese curds, anyone?"
""

It’s the unreasonable decisions we will remember most."
""
"We all aspire to be reasonable. We can reason though every part of our life - when looking backwards. We have a reason to live where we live. There’s a reason to have the friends we have, to love who we love and to work where we work. "
""
"Yep, totally reasonable."
""
"Ironically, where I live today, the friends I’ve chosen, the love I share, and the place I work are all the outcome of a series of unreasonable decisions I made along the way. Our lives become the result of the risks we’ve taken, and those we’ve chosen to let pass."
""
"This creates our life’s endowment."
""
"As we have success we make decisions that are more “reasonable” and “responsible.” It’s reasonable to de-risk everything. Maybe this is why we’re the target demographic for extended auto-warranty robo-calls?"
""
"But this learned aversion to risk slowly steals our joy."
""
"If you’re in your Normal 40 and you feel something is missing, something you used to have, do something unreasonable."
""
"I unreasonably became a pilot, learned to play guitar and became a certified executive coach. Oh, and I started #normal40."
""
"When you’re judged by others for doing something unreasonable, this will be the proof that you’re on a path you’ll remember."
""

It was 1988 and I saw it all on the AM radio."
""
"There’s no single technology that had a greater impact on my first 18 years than the AM radio."
""
"It was in a John Deere 4230 when I spent time with Herb Carneal and John Gordon, the voices of the Minnesota Twins, as they put me on the field for every pitch. Every day I listened until the game was done, it was dark or the hay was up."
""
"It was in my best friends 1977 Mercury and we would drive the township backroads with a deep and raspy-voiced friend named Larry King. It was his voice that remnided us it was after our curfew."
""
"It was in a 1981 F250, with doors flung open, when dad and I heard: “Hello Americans, I’m Paul Harvey. You know what the news is, in a minute you’re going to hear The Rest of The Story.” That voice meant it was 3:15PM, and it meant we were going to set down our fencing tools and listen."
""
"The crackle of the AM radio playing in concert with distant lightening was magical. No sound spawned more hope of overnight rain. The crackle birthed great optimism to our crops, but also to my social life."
""
"The AM radio had 5 buttons. Two of them, for some reason, were for the same station."
""
"Thank you AM Radio. Thank you KKAA, KSDN, KFYR and WNAX. "
""
"You helped shape a generation. "
""
"Good day -"
""
" #normal40"

I wonder who Sam W. Coe was?"
""
"My maternal grandpa, William Raetzman, passed away when I was 5. I have very few memories of him, but I took some time this week with my mom and dad to get to know him a little better."
""
"While diggin through some old photos that my grandma left for my mom, I came across this note from Sam Coe and I was taken by it."
""
"My mom remembers Sam. She remembers the friendship, and the conversation. She remembers Sam’s generosity, his philanthropy, his decency and kindness."
""
"I never knew Sam. This note was the first time I’d ever heard his name. He probably passed away more than 35 years ago. But this note from 1960 gave me a glimps of the type of person he was. This note enabled me to witness the type of person my grandpa must have been. The message is succinct, personal, and just enough to leave me longing for another day with Sam Coe of Springfield, IL."
""
"If you’ve dropped into my posts before, you know how much I cherish the effort placed into a note; it matters. This one did. It mattered to my grandpa. Then it mattered to my grandmother and mom who kept it. Now it matters to me."
""
"While it’s more than 50 years later, I’d like to stop in on Sam’s family someday. And should this find the Coe family, consider this your invite to visit mine."
""

"Let that sink in."

"If you’re in your Normal40, the life happening around you right now is the chapter that will best define you. It’s also the chapter we allow to blur past."

This is the chapter when our family needs us to be the best moms & dads. This is the chapter when we let our work and our fear of loss distract us from chapter we will cherish most."

“Get your jersey dirty!”"
""
"My dad was my baseball coach. He had 100 wonderful one liners for the team. One of my favorites was his requirement that we get our jerseys dirty."
""
"I remember the day my phone rang; I was tangled in ball of yarn and it began to unwind. Once it started to unfurl, it went fast. It was bad."
""
"I was the leader. It didn’t matter that I didn’t know it was happening, it was mine."
""
"I did, and I do, own it."
""
"I took my hits over it; professionally and personally. And I’m forever thankful for the gift this experience gave me."
""
"I learned it early enough in life to put these lessons to work. Failure has taught me more than any classroom or mentor, but it has also left scars."
""
"The scars are the reminders. The scars are the diploma. The scars are ... romantic. "
""
"The scars are the proof that you can take a risk and face challenge, and survive. The scars show that you can battle, that you were willing to personally sacrifice yourself for your team."
""
"The scar shows that you were in the game, that you gave a damn and that you could get your jersey dirty."
""
"The dirt on a jersey won’t tell the full story of any single play, but it does tell you a lot about how fiercely someone plays the game."
""
"My jersey is dirty. What about yours?"
""
"Thanks, Coach Dad."
""

The last few months have been pretty fun living the normal 40."
""
"I been asked: What is N40? Who is it? What does it mean? What’s the goal? Where is it going ... etc."
""
"Two years ago it felt as if I’d worked myself into The Box, so I wrote and shared what I was feeling with friends who are very successful, impressive and climbing - the people who had made it. They wrote back and said: “YES, I feel the same! I’m in The Box!”"
""
"Wait, what? You?"
""
"Let’s be clear, The Box isn’t all bad. In fact The Box is safe. The Box is consistent, financially comfortable and it looks really good on the outside, and you got yourself there."
""
"But The Box is also a place where we unknowingly transition from risk taker to risk avoider. It’s where we slowly move from climber to base-camp operative. It’s where we somehow shift from architecting a greater legacy into preserving the one we have. The Box is where we work to convince everyone, including ourself, that we’re still as impressive as we used to be. The Box steals from us, one hour at a time."
""
"So..."
""
"What is N40? It’s your life’s halftime."
""
"Who is N40? We are."
""
"What is N40’s goal? Writing your second-half story."
""
"You are the Normal 40. So am I."
""
"Our legacy is counting on us to break out of The Box."
""
"Own it."
""

This week, try yes."
""
"You are a big deal. No matter what your brain tries to tell you, YOU are a big deal to someone awesome."
""
"As we get into our midlife (what I call the Normal 40), we change our default from “Yes, that sounds like fun.” into “No, I can’t commit my time to that.” I don’t know why, but maybe it is to preserve the already low fuel in our tank. After all, being us is exhausting, right? "
""
"But as I think about it, only my life’s “yeses” have ever put fuel into my tank. Saying no, at best, only ever delivered more of the same. Isn’t “nothing” the only thing no can offer?"
""
"Saying yes is where adventure and potential live. Each of my life’s greatest unfoldings started with a yes."
""
"This week, say yes. Meet the friend who has been asking - or ask a friend you’ve been missing."
""
"This week, take the meeting you’ve been putting off. Make the call you’ve oddly avoided."
""
"This week, write the note you’ve been contemplating."
""
"Whatever you say yes to, take time to ride it. Let one yes lead to another. Just roll with it and follow it."
""
"Say yes because you are in control. Take your yes back."
""
"This week, say yes. Then, remain open to where yes takes you. Ride the yes just like you used to...before we went on autopilot."
""
"Tell me how it goes!"
""

I’m a professional, a husband, a dad, a coach, a friend, a volunteer board member .... bla, bla."
""
"There’s an age band from 35-55 where we become so driven by our hourly outlook calendars that entire years become a fog."
""
"Literally, years."
""
"If you’re in this age band, a band I call “The Normal 40,” you don’t have to wonder if this is you. For many, this is us. We spend our days racing the watch. This is normal."
""
"Take a look at your calendar for today. Or next week. Even better, look at your calendar from last week and last month. Most of it will be a blur, but it sure seemed urgent at the time. That’s normal."
""
"We all try to give our time to the most important things (that’s normal). I sure do. But measuring “time invested” isn’t the right instrument to lead with. Time doesn’t move the needle. Time invested is an output. How you choose to spend your energy is the input; enerrgy moves the needle."
""
"How we allocate our energy fulfills, or fails us."
""
"You allocate your energy. Own that!"
""
"Giving time is important, but time without energy is just exhaust. "
""
"Go back to your calendar and own which matters you will gift your energy?"
""
"When you do, how you spend your time will take care of itself."
""
"Stay normal!"
""

My LinkedIn posts probably don’t fit the mold; I’m good with that."

"I’m inspired by those who take time to comment or DM me after a post. Many can relate my inventory of life-shaping moments to their own."

"I’ve concluded ..."

"We’re all on a journey, and we all get to decide how we navigate it. Sometime we put our professional journey on an autopilot. If you’re a high-achieving executive between 35-55, and you have or are raising kids, you’ve spent time (or are currently on) autopilot."
""
"I have."
""
"Autopilot isn’t a bad option. In fact, simply achieving a place where you earned the luxury of an autopilot means you’ve accomplished a lot - more than most. But the longer you’re on auto autopilot, the harder - and scarier - it is to break out of it. This is real."
""
"Autopilot is a place of no growth; a place where any sort of change feels like a professional risk. If you stay here too long, even the highest achievers find themselves trapped in a box with the clock ticking. I’ve been there."
""
"If you want to read a bunch of good quotes from great leaders, go ahead and keep scrolling on LinkedIn; they’re everywhere. But getting you back into life, out of the box, requires you to knock yourself out of autopilot."

It was the spring of 2001, and I was falling in love.  "
""
"I can walk you, blindfolded, to the exact place on the face of the earth where I took this photo. It was my wife’s (a self-described city girl) first trip to our farm. In fact, her first trip to any farm. On this spring day she met one of the farm’s newest arrivals. And thanks to the contribution of a 20-year-old Canon AE-1, one of my life’s greatest memories became shareable to a family that was yet to come."
""
"While wildly biased, this photo, for me, sums up the grace of the world around us - when we take a moment to grab it. It’s the newness of the world, it’s the face of wonder, the simple beauty of the prairie, the mud on the tires, and the rush to grab an opportunity with the door left open. It is a moment’s grace."
""
"In 2021, I’d like to do more to capture more of these moments. I want to capture more of the moments I love; the moments that shape me. I want to harvest the moments my kids need to hear about, and the moments my great-great grand kids wished they could ask me about."
""
"For it’s in these moments that we curate our legacy."
""
"As this comes together, I hope you will join me. I also hope you do more than simply pass by, I hope that that you will stop in and be part of the story."
""

After closing three acquisitions in 2019, 2020 presented me with the opportunity to deploy into Raven Engineered Films division to build and align a word-class sales team and strategy. The results will speak for themselves. It was, without a doubt, a blast. Thank you #ravenefd. Keep solving!

It was 1976 and it was a big day on the farm; new tractor day!"
""
"My Grandpa Lester, after several months of waiting, is pictured here with his new John Deere 4630. While he had several new tractor days in his lifetime, this would be his last."
""
"That little guy on the yellow toy tractor was also celebrating his new tractor day. That little guy is me, and I was about three years old. While my memory is faint, I recall seeing the semi pull in the yard and I remember darting out the door to greet it. In fact, I was so excited that I didn’t bother to change out of my pajamas."
""
"Through the years I would spend many hundreds of hours, probably thousands of hours, behind the wheel of that tractor. We were a two-tractor farm, so it didn’t get many days off. I changed the oil and used it to disk, chisel, cut, stack, chop, harvest, blow, feed ... One time even put a coat of wax on it."
""
"It’s been well over 40 years since this photo was taken. My grandpa passed in 1999. And while I don’t know where that little yellow toy tractor may be, I do know that red shed and that 4630 are still on the farm today. "
""
"And on many days, so is the heart of that little blond-haired kid who still loves to spend time in the cab."
""
"I miss new tractor days."
""
"My inheritance is a gift."
""

Dear Craven Corner,"
""
"The irony of 47 years."
""
"Thank you for the armature baseball sponsorship in 1967."
""
"These two photos were not a recreation, but sheer coincidence."
""
"53 years ago a roadside supper club called “Craven Corner” sponsored an armature baseball team. My dad played on that team for a few seasons. The photo on the left is of my mom and dad on game day; it was 1967. It was most-certainly a Sunday afternoon, because that was game day."
""
"I had never seen the photo of my parents when I found the uniform, 47 years later. It was tucked in a drawer in the basement of the old farm house and decided to try it on."
""
"The two photos randomly surfaced when I was home recently and I was taken by a few observations. It’s interesting that we each married 5’ 4” blond-haired teachers who like the color pink. It’s also worthy of note that the uniform only needed to survive one move, about 150 feet, from an old farm house to a new one. And Craven Corner was the site of my parents first date."
""
"Craven Corner, you may not know it, and even though you’ve been closed for decades, your sponsorship continues to leave a legacy."
""
"Thank you, Craven Corner!"

The dreams of a bygone era."
""
"The prairie landscape is dotted long vacated, quiet, lonely and fragile farm sites. These farms, vacant for a generation, stirs deep wonder."
""
"Today I see the same things differently. Deep within these homesteads, I see the under-appreciated struggles of yesterday through a somewhat romantic lens, and with much-deeper admiration. From the driveway, I will sit for hours trying to relive how things were on this very site 60 years ago. And I wonder how they changed over time to arrive where they are today."
""
"Abandon farmsteads are not about the failing buildings that will soon melt into the earth. Instead, they represent the hopes and dreams of a family who risked it all to build a barn to protect their animals, THEN a house for the family. I hear the laughter of children and the hope of a plentiful harvest and the trips to the well. I also feel the gut-wrenching devastation of drought, the loss of loved ones and the decision to auction the life they built. I feel it all from the driveway. "
""
"Somewhere in my wonder, I wonder if anyone told them thank you. Thank you for taking the chance and for rolling the dice. Thank you for placing the fence, squaring the barn, taming the prairie and for building a legacy. So, thank you."

There’s a rhythm of the prairie. A rhythm where the clouds, the soil, the sun and the seed all have jobs to do, and they all count on one another to get their job done. And after the seed is placed, the farmer is left farming his optimism in the trust that each will do their part."
""
"Some years the clouds, soil, sun and seeds find a rhythm and the farmer is rewarded financially. In other years they find conflict, causing a search for purpose that runs deeper than a bank balance. This builds the optimists heart and the pessimists mind."
""
"Perhaps the optimists heart can be best captured by the majesty of a harvest’s sky. For on some nights, as the sun sets low on the prairie, it casts orange and red rays that bounce off the clouds and onto the prairie."
""
"During harvest, farmers work relentlessly to harvest the season’s rhythm in the form of new seeds from each spear of wheat, ear of corn or pod of bean, as the slow-moving harvest wafts grain dust into the air."
""
"On a calm evening during harvest, as the sun presses through the atmosphere, the harvest dust that gently hangs in the air scatters the sun’s light just enough to wring out the blue spectrum of light, and leaves the red and orange light paint the sky into a moment."
""
"I do love the rhythm of the prairie."

Can you get the gate?"
""
"Growing up, I heard my dad ask me that question hundreds of times. In retrospect, it was more of an instruction than it was a question. After a while, it became implied that when we stopped at a gate we both knew the expectation. Being the gate-guy was not hard, but it sure felt somehow disruptive and unfair."
""
"When moving from field to field, we always listened to baseball. I think that’s what I would have chosen to listen to, but our pickup only had a AM radio with a single speaker in the center of the dashboard. It was enough."
""
"It was always the Minnesota Twins. I can still hear Herb Carneal, the voice of the Twins, coloring my mind with visions of the park and the crowd, while also taking me on a pitch-by-pitch journey of my ears that drew a sharp contrast with that of my eyes."
""
"Sometimes we arrived at a gate as the game would be drawing tight. While dad didn’t have to say a word once the truck stopped, we both knew what would happen next; I would get the gate. But when the game was tight, as Herb drew our ears and our eyes from the pastures around us into a keen focus of the single, crackly AM speaker inside the dash, it also went unsaid that the gate would wait a minute. Some things were simply more important than others."

"In this photo you can see the farm where I grew up. The fence was put there by family. Even the road itself, after being vacated by the township, is now under the care of our family. After all, and according to the township, it doesn’t really lead you anywhere."

"On this day, on this mile, I chose to run with my head up. In the miles before I may have been distracted by the uneven ground or a nearby sound. But on this mile, the last mile, I ran with my head up as I couldn’t wait to reel in the distance between where I was, and the place that built me."
""
"The four-mile trek around the section has come to represent a lot in life. The aggressive start down the road as we leave home. Rounding first mile and entering the second; uncharted territory as we move further from our roots. Then in the far distance, in our self-made world somewhere in mile three, the outline of home in the distance starts its gentile tug. "
""
"As I move closer, step by step, the memories of a thousand laughs and a thousand struggles romance in my mind. In the final mile, I give thanks for each of the miles behind me for all they have taught me. And I give thanks to the mile ahead, the one that takes me home."

My inheritance has been gnawing at me."

"At my stage in life thinking deeply about one’s inheritance is probably natural, even normal. An inheritance, to me, is something that’s given to us, or sometimes forced upon us. But at its root, to me, an inheritance is something for which we did nothing to earn and can do little to change. Our inheritance is the proverbial box of random tools we are given at our start; each of which we must deploy to make our end."
""
"I’m in my 40’s. I realize clearer than ever that I inherited good health. I inherited the love of a strong family, and I inherited drive, an ability to connect and integrity. I also inherited stubbornness and inconsistency with my empathy and am loose with details."

"My inheritance runs deep and is as beautiful as I chose to make it."
""

""
"I can’t thank my parents, their parents, and their parents enough for giving me the gifts for which I did nothing to earn, but am responsible to deploy. After all, doing so will impact the inheritance of my kids, their kids, their kids and theirs."

I remember learning about it in grade school, then debating the pros and cons in high school."
""
"The proponents tended to argue for the good of the community and the individuals who participated. The contrarians tended to argue the unfairness and financial costs to the country were too great."
""
"This photo is taken a few miles from our farm. It’s a small dam and spillway built under the authority of the Works Project Administration (WPA) during the 1930s. This and thousands of other stimulus projects were used as a tool to support the public good while also providing a meaningful work and a wage that could provide life’s basics (earning about $30/ month)."
""
"This dam and spillway was built more than 80s years ago by area residents willing to trade physical labor for a wage. This WPA project represents a once-used path to stimulus. In 80 years from now, I have no idea how today’s approach to stimulus will impact my great grandkids - I hope it’s awesome. But if I’m honest, I do find it hard to see how any of it will be providing as much return on investment as this little dam."
""
"This WPA project has a wonderful legacy. It’s a legacy that’s shared with the hands that built it."

My dad and I didn’t always get along."
""
"It was November, 1993. My grandpa and I set out for an afternoon hunt; I remember like yesterday. While we were often together during family events, something about this setting - the cool air and warm sun, the land and the company - moved my grandpa to tell stories he had never before told."
""
"As we drove along a homesteader’s tree claim that afternoon, he softly mentioned: “My dad and I didn’t always get along.” For the next 30 minutes, as I drove slowly along the trees, I clung to every word."
""
"My grandpa attended a one-room school, and farmed with his dad. The summer after his 8th grade year, my grandpa and his dad had a falling out over a team of horses and an open gate. The unfairness of the affair was more than my grandpa could take. After crying for an hour, he picked himself up, kissed his mother goodbye, and set out alone down a dusty road. He left home that day with the clothes on his back and the two things he loved most, his baseball glove, and is .22 rifle."
""
"This school photo from 1930 found me today. In the back row there’s a kid holding a baseball and bat. That is my grandfather. He’s 14 and it’s likely his 8th grade. I’ve thought about his legacy and that ball and bat 1000 times. "
""
"Thank you, Grandpa!"

It was spring of 1982."
""
"The 1980s was not a kind decade to the family farm. The harsh reality of the business clashed violently with a will to preserve generations of family land, and it required kids to grow up faster than many do today."
""
"This morning I called my mom and asked her to take a picture of a few things for me; this 1981 Ford F-250 is one of them. It’s still found lined up along the backside of a tree grove on the farm, right where we parked it 20 years ago. It still smells like it did the last time it moved in 1998."

"There is nothing fancy about this truck. Nobody is ever going to come and ask for it as a restoration piece. But for me, that truck will forever hold the distinction of being the first vehicle I ever drove."

"I was 8, my dad needed a vehicle to follow he and the planter to the field, and I was the only one left. So, I grabbed my basketball, let half of the air out of it as to flatten one side, sat it on the seat and gently started out of the drive behind a 4630 John Deere.  "
""
"A farm kid's love affair with the land, equipment and family starts young and dies hard."
""
"I own the story of that truck. It’s part of the inheritance my parents have given to me. It's an inheritance I will forever cherish.  "

"Thank you, mom and dad!"

This is a bison rubbing stone."
""
"This stone still finds its home on the South Dakota prairie and it’s powerful reminder of the sands of time."
""
"A bison rubbing stone is large rock that the glaciers carried for thousands of miles, over thousands of years, then dropped randomly as the glaciers melted gently into the prairie.  The rocks are compositionally different from the rest of the rocks found in the area. Geologists call these rocks “erratics.”  "
""
"As the prairie was settled, not by humans but by antelope and the American Bison, the large erratic rocks found their purpose. "
""
"For a bison grazing on the prairie a thousand years ago, this rock would have served as the only option to rub against to rid themselves of their heavy winter coats. As thousands of bison pushed and rubbed their massive frames against the stone, their sharp powerful hooves softened and moved the ground around the rock. Over hundreds or thousands of years serving as a back scratcher, the rock has become forever polished (look closely - it looks wet) as it slowly settled deeper and deeper into the prairie."
""
"While many bison rubbing stones have fallen victim to modern farming, at least one still remains on our family farm and ranch. And it’s awesome.  "
""

The Time - "
""
"We’ve all said it. We’ve all done it. We’ve sat in limbo-land waiting for someone, or maybe even ourselves, to make a decision so that we can move forward.  In effect, we chalked that up to killing time."
""
"We spend small portions of our day killing time. It becomes the mental bridge we take between meetings which, ironically, can be another form of killing time."
""
"The irony is that it is impossible to kill time.  But time does kill us."
""
"Time is the ultimate currency. We’re all born with an unknown quantity and we all have the freedom to spend it - or invest it - as we please. And like financial currency, the better we invest in small amounts, the better our return over time.  And also like financial currency, we spend money to make money. "
""
"Today, spend some time to make time. "
""
"This “asset allocation review” will be the single-most-important return on investment of your lifetime. Yet most of us won’t take time to make time, and most of us will regret it. "
""
"Over a lifetime, how we invest our time will build the assets by which we’re forever remembered. "
""
"These investments define our legacy. Take time to make time. "

The Grind - "
""
"Every new idea, project, relationship or goal blasts off with rocket fuel with a trajectory so fulfilling in our mind’s eye."
""
"However, most of these visions of granduer flame out before reaching our mind’s potential. Why is that?"
""
"I’ve learned a lot in my professional career. I’ve enjoyed moments of great success, but garnered my greatest learning from my failure. Through it all, I’ve had the great fortune to be surrounded by truly remarkable people. Each of which with their own set of successes and failures."
""
"As I reach my professional halftime, the people I’ve come to admire most are those who show up everyday and work to improve everything around them. These are the teammates who take on more than you think they can handle, and make it work. These are the people who see a problem and believe they should solve it. I admire the people who finish what they start, and endure the pain we all feel at some point in our mind’s journey."
""
"These are the people we all aspire to be like; the ones who give a damn.  The people who know it will get hard, who know others will quit, but they hold the torch and marshal onward.  Ever onward."
""
"Today, give a damn. Rekindle the project that’s nearly dead and lead it. For this is how we all want to be remembered."

The Worry -"
""
"We sure do worry about a lot."
""
"We worry about our kids and our family, of course.  But we also worry about how our team or how our boss will react to something. We worry about workload and deadlines. We worry about projects around the house. We worry about mortgages and credit cards. We worry about how others perceive us. We worry about everything and nothing all at the same time. Maybe worst of all, weworry about the same things, over and over, day after day; ugh. This is normal."
""
"Worry is a defense mechanism of the brain. Worry is there to ensure something gets our focus so that we can survive. While good in small doses, worry destroys joy. Worry drains the mind of power and energy. Worry forces you into the short game. "
""
"Worry is a direct reflection of our self-confidence. If we are self confident we worry less. If we’re second guessing ourselves, we worry more. Confidence is where we demonstrate control over our worry and win the long game.  "
""
"Today, find a worry and study it for a moment. Is the worry controlling you, or can you take control over it? Is worry stealing your joy. If so, own it. Name the worry, and address it with confidence. You may get it right and you may get it wrong, but the end result is less worry. That is victory."
""

"We’re all wired for an adventure."

"This adventure has not disappointed. Wow. There are moments of great victory and moments of great challenge. But each moment brings newness to me, and me to the moment. Herein lies the fulfilling beauty of the backroad experience."

"This adventure has tested emotions, delivered great uncertainty and has demanded more than anticipated. These are the feelings my brain was trying to warn me about, but these are the same challenges that are brining me fulfillment."
""
"This adventure was not on my windshield’s roadmap only a few days ago, but is now forever part of my life’s journey."
""
"Next time an adventure finds you, take it. Or at least grant yourself the decency to dream about where the adventure may take you. Get off your mind’s highway and into your life’s backroads."

"Want fulfillment? Your adventure awaits."

The Long Game - "
""
"Winning The Long Game requires a wickedly-courageous short game."
""
"We live in a short-game world. "
""
"We’re all wiser today than we were yesterday. I’ve made decisions in the last 60 days which, in hindsight, were driven by fear of losing in the short, rather than winning in the long. It’s how our brains are hard-wired. This is normal. "
""
"Everything isn’t a crisis. It is hard to squelch the noise our minds think it wants, but if we resist the impulse of the short, everything becomes more calm - immediately. "
""
"Over the Long Game, probably 99% of the decisions we make don’t matter. So let them not matter. Quit thinking about, reading about, fretting about them. Focus on the few decisions that will have impact, and own them.  "
""
"Recall your long game from only 60 days ago and affirm it. Then, own your short game decisions. You will be amazed by the control you really have in your short game reality. "
""
"Be courageous enough to own the short game to achieve The Long Game. "

The fact that you seek instead of waiting to be sought will be part of your legacy.  "

The Gunk -"
""
"We tend to believe what we hear. And we tend to listen to what we already believe. This is confirmation bias, and it’s normal. "
""
"It’s also a disaster."
""
"For any opinion on any topic we can find an author, article, publication, podcast, radio station, club or television station that will tell us how right we are. Even more, most of these outlets spend most of their effort convincing us how other individuals are actually plotting to destroy the world. In 99% of the cases, no matter what you watch, read, listen to or believe, this simply isn’t true. "
""
"This is The Gunk, and here inlies the disaster."
""
"We’re all better when we use our minds rather than borrow others. We’re also better when we remember we’re all wrong nearly as often as we’re right. That is true of me and my beliefs, and it is true of you and your beliefs."
""
"The Gunk is an engineered opinion designed to manufacture an emotion that locks us out of other ideas. The Gunk works wonders for some applications, like creating followers, but it tends to damage friendships and destroy the common good. "

The Shift - "
""
"In every success and every failure, personal or professional, you can look back and recall The Shift. It’s that moment that finally slaps you hard enough to snap you out of false hope and into the harsh reality."

"In your life, there will be precious few hours that you can recall with vivid clarity.  These tend to be the hours of great pain, great loss and great uncertainty. They also tend to be associated with feelings of great pride, great love and great sacrifice."

The Core -"
""
"Everything has a core; the essential center-most ingredients that enables and regulates survival."
""
"Your business has a core. Your team has a core. Your family has a core. Your community, friendships, gym, church ... everything has a core."
""
"You, individually, have a core. You have core beliefs and core gifts. You have core routines, habits, values, priorities, philosophies, strategies, friends, talents ... all of us have a core of essential ingredients."
""
"Today, take inventory of your core and write it down. What are the ingredients that make you, you.  Of those ingredients, which of them will you never sacrifice, ever.  Which of them will you let go."
""
"At the core you’ll find your character. Nothing in your life will be more sacred, personal and hard fought over than your own definition of your character."
""
"Today, protect the core.  "
""
"Like, Comment and Share. "
""
"#normal40"
"#leadership"
"#coaching"
"#change

The Gauges -"
""
"In flight school you learn early how to get a feel for the aircraft. You learn to feel the pitch and yaw from your seat, and you true that feeling by the placement of the nose and the wings against the far off horizon. This form of flying is completely satisfying; it gives a clear view of the world outside the cockpit and becomes an extension of your human senses. You’re on autopilot. "
""
"The skies are not always severe clear, however. There are often clouds to navigate, unforecasted fog that sets in, ice that builds up that forces rushed decisions and wind sheer that can push you dramatically off course. During these times the view outside the cockpit is a useless flight aid in your survival."
""
"It’s during these uncertain moments that a pilot is taught to trust his or her gauges. Nothing more. The view outside isn’t the reality; the reality is found only in the gauges. The gauges are the instruments that have always been there to guide you, to show you and to lead you. The gauges don’t make up a false truth, they tell you where you are, they give you your altitude and bearing, and most importantly they give you your attitude when your eyes deceive you.  "
""
"We all fly through storms. When you do, stop, don’t panic, and learn to trust your gauges."
""
"#normal40

The Storm – "
""
"Defined as a violent weather event involving rain, wind, thunder and lightning, storms predate humans."
""
"For thousands of years, humans have tried to predict storms. Even today, humans are limited to interpreting the ingredients that may manifest a storm by severity and geography, but only hours in advance."
""
"Given humans long history with storms, most of us are at our best when a storm is at its worst. "
""
"Most of us sacrifice everything to protect the well-being of our family during a storm, and the well-being of our assets after the calm sets in. "
""
"This is an instinct, not a skill."
""
"As any storm rages, trust your instinct. Hold close the well-being of family first, and the accounting of your assets after the calm."
""
"#normal40

The Gut -"
""
"There’s a constant battle between your brain and your gut; that’s normal. "
""
"Your brain seeks dots in order to connect them into a sequenced solution. Your brain seeks black and white and will sacrifice speed. "
""
"Your gut is not burdened with the need for all of the dots, let alone for them to connect. "
""
"Your gut seeks speed and action, and it craves a beginning over an ending. Your gut will comfortably operate in shades of grey when your brain will not. "
""
"There’s a place for each, but the best leaders operate from the head when they can, but from the gut when it matters.  "
""
"Trust your gut and the world will start to unfold differently for you. "
""
" #normal40 - #leadership - #Coaching

Relationships -"
""
"Curating relationships, new or old, are essential to the feeling of fulfillment. "
""
"Each day, knowingly or unknowingly, you are either investing in or withdrawing from your relationship bank. "
""
"As the deposits grow, so will you.  Should the deposits run empty, so will you.   "
""
"It sounds easy, but it’s not. And harder for some than others. And at points in your life it often becomes a convenient truth to ignore. "
""
"That’s normal. "
""
"In the last 7 days I’ve been fulfilled by the deposit of many new relationships; friends, leaders and inspiring growers.  "
""
"Many thanks to Andrew Neitlich  #andrewneitlich and #centerforexecutivecoaching. "
""
" #normal40 - #leadership - #coaching

Decisions - "
""
"All decisions have three elements:"
""
"A SELECTION from the range of options you control, an associated ACTION taken, and the TIMING for which you carry forward your action. "
""
"Each of these (selection / action / timing) impact outcome. And each require courage.  "
""
"Rule #1: Failure through the process is an option. This is called learning. "
""
"Rule #2: Failure to start the process when needed is, in fact, a failure.  "
""
"Never be afraid to learn, and don’t fail to start."
""
" #normal40 - #leadership - #coaching
